data.title: Burnham ‘failed to conduct full checks’ before lending to property tycoon
data.description:
Andy Burnham has been accused of failing to conduct full financial checks on a property developer before lending him £140m of public money, it has emerged.

The Mayor of Greater Manchester did not obtain a statement of assets and liabilities before handing over the taxpayers’ cash to Renaker, a builder of residential skyscrapers. This exposed taxpayers to the risk that the public money could be “wiped out”, a tribunal concluded.

The developer used the money to fund luxury apartments in Manchester, despite Mr Burnham promising to end such arrangements when he stood for election as the city’s mayor.

Mr Burnham’s office insisted that due diligence had been carried out and that it was “false” to suggest otherwise.

Mr Burnham is facing a legal challenge over £140m of loans granted to Renaker, which will be heard in the Court of Appeal on June 9, nine days before the Makerfield by-election in which he will be the Labour Party candidate.

The hearing could overshadow the later stages of his election campaign, as the court case will reopen a row over the £578m in public money lent to Renaker and the property tycoon behind it, Daren Whitaker.

Mr Whitaker’s companies received a large share of the £1.2bn lent for development by Mr Burnham’s Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA) through its housing investment fund.

Mr Whitaker has become hugely wealthy as a result, and he has a personal fortune estimated at £698m. Last year he registered his residency as the tax haven of Monaco before switching it back to Britain.

Before he was elected mayor, Mr Burnham criticised the use of public loans to fund “city centre and luxury schemes”, saying he would renegotiate the terms of what was then a £300m housing fund “so that we can fully focus it on the long-term goal of an affordable home for all”.

In March 2024, Mr Burnham chaired a meeting of the Greater Manchester Combined Authority, which approved £120m in loans to Renaker companies in the space of one minute.

The money was split between Trinity Developments and New Jackson (Contour) Investments, both of which were special-purpose vehicles set up by Renaker to build flats in Manchester.

But the money lent to Renaker has resulted in virtually no new affordable housing. Out of 11,000 new homes built using loans from the £1.2bn fund, just 503 – less than 5 per cent – are classed as affordable.

Renaker’s latest development is a 51-storey tower of “refined luxury living” with one-bedroom flats starting at £275,000.

Last year, Aubrey Weis, a rival developer, challenged the distribution of the loans in a hearing at the Competition Appeals Tribunal. He argued that loaning more than half of the housing investment fund’s money to Mr Whitaker’s company “distorted” the Manchester housing market and had “effectively frozen out” competitors.

He lost the case, but the tribunal did raise the allegation that GMCA failed to conduct due diligence on Mr Whitaker’s financial position, saying there was a risk that he had liabilities that could have swallowed the money lent from the public purse.

Hodge Malek, KC, the tribunal’s chairman, said in his ruling: “Mr Whitaker evidently had access to very substantial amounts of funds as a result of the liquidation of XL Developments [another property company], that said it would have been better had the GMCA obtained a statement of his assets and liabilities prior to entering into the 2024 Renaker Loans.

“There was a residual, although most probably unlikely, risk that he had large liabilities which could have wiped out these funds, but as the equity had to be in place at the time of drawdown, the GMCA had substantial protection against the consequences of such an eventuality.”

During the hearing, which concerned the most recent loans made to Renaker, lawyers for Mr Weis argued that GMCA never looked into Mr Whitaker’s creditworthiness.

Joseph Barrett KC, for Mr Weis, told the hearing: “It is obviously fundamentally important that one would do the due diligence on Mr. Whitaker because it may be that he was in a robust position. One cannot assume that. One does not have the first idea absent due diligence…due diligence never occurs.”

Mr Weis maintained that the loans granted to Renaker amounted to a subsidy and that he would appeal against the tribunal’s decision at the Court of Appeal hearing next month.

Mr Whitaker, 54, studied building studies at Leeds College of Building for four years before a BSc in quantity surveying and building at the University of Salford in the early 1990s. He worked for the construction firm Laing O’Rourke for 18 years before setting up Renaker in 2006.

Since then he has become Manchester’s most prolific developer, transforming the city with 12 high-rise buildings containing more than 8,500 homes, many bought by Chinese landlords. Renaker has plans for another 8,000 homes.

In November last year Mr Whitaker registered his main residence as Monaco in filings at Companies House, before switching it back to the UK.

A Greater Manchester Combined Authority spokesman said: “The suggestion that proper due diligence was not carried out on these loans is false. We carried out necessary financial due diligence and this formed part of the documents seen by the Competition Appeal Tribunal, which praised our approach and found we had ‘substantial protection’ against any risk.

“Our Housing Investment Loans Fund helped build 11,000 new homes and directly supported 503 affordable homes, operating alongside our Brownfield Housing Fund, which is bringing forward another 11,000 affordable homes across Greater Manchester.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: New homes discounted by £100k as builder scrambles to sell properties
data.description:
Troubled housebuilder Vistry has knocked more than £100,000 off the price of its homes in an effort to spur sales and raise cash.

The London-listed developer has reduced the prices of its new builds by as much as 17pc, according to the investment bank Royal Bank of Canada (RBC). Analysts found one example of a four-bed home in West Sussex that was reduced from £680,000 to £565,000.

Steep discounts come as Vistry, which has been called Labour’s favourite housebuilder, scrambles to raise cash.

The company has been left reeling after cost overruns on projects and delays to Labour’s £39bn social housing programme, on which Vistry is a crucial housebuilding partner.

Shares have tumbled by close to 65pc during the past year.

Vistry announced earlier this year that it would discount new-build homes for the private sales market to help boost sales and raise cash. The company hasn’t shared the level of price reductions, but said in May that the cost-cutting drive was set to significantly dent its profits this year.

RBC’s research covered 1,273 of Vistry’s newly built homes put up for sale between Jan 3 and May 29 this year. Not all were discounted, but of those that were, the average discount was 8.4pc.

In one case, two five-bed homes next door to each other were listed at £460,000 at the start of the year, before being cut to £425,000 in the first week of February.

One was then cut to £400,000 a week later and sold. The other stayed at £425,000 and was sold two weeks later.

Anthony Codling, an analyst at RBC, said Vistry may need to continue cutting prices if Andy Burnham challenges Sir Keir Starmer for the Labour leadership, as a change of prime minister could “slow the wheels” of Labour’s social housing programme. The housebuilder has received hundreds of millions of pounds a year in taxpayer-funded loans and grants under the scheme.

“It could be a summer and autumn of discontent for Vistry,” Mr Codling said.

Trouble has been brewing at Vistry since late 2024, when it revealed cost overruns of £165m on its projects, leading to a string of profit warnings.

Vistry recently warned it could face a cash crunch under what it called a “severe but plausible downside scenario”.

It has reportedly ordered subcontractors to down tools on private-sector projects that are either unsold or will not be completed until after June 30.

In March, Greg Fitzgerald, then chief executive, told analysts that he had “demanded that each business unit go forth and double their sales rate” in an effort to raise cash.

Mr Codling said Vistry’s sales rates “have yet to double” but rose by 30pc in May for the builder’s open-market homes, where properties are sold directly to the public.

Mr Fitzgerald has since been replaced by Adam Daniels. Mr Codling said: “The new chief executive appears to be keeping the policy of his predecessor: cutting prices to drive sales rates. The big question is, how low will they have to go?”

All of Britain’s major housebuilders have been hit this year by rising build costs as the war in Iran drives up inflation. The conflict has also kept mortgage rates high, denting demand for properties.

A spokesman for Vistry said: “We have clearly outlined the actions we are taking to improve cash generation and reduce debt levels, which include targeted pricing initiatives to reduce inventory.

“Whilst current market conditions are challenging for all companies in our sector, the group has a robust forward order book of £4.5bn, and we continue to build at scale and pace, delivering the homes this country so desperately needs.”

data.title: Burnham plans £2 cap on bus fares nationwide to help jobless
data.description:
Andy Burnham is planning a £2 cap on bus fares to help get people back to work, The Telegraph can disclose.

The Greater Manchester mayor has introduced the cap in his city region and now plans to roll out a similar scheme nationwide if he succeeds in ousting Sir Keir Starmer, sources told The Telegraph.

The scheme would be badged as an attempt to help working families with the cost of living, and to encourage unemployed people to travel to job interviews.

Before a potential announcement later this week, Mr Burnham said low-cost or free public transport was “a brilliant way to get people back to work and connect communities”.

The England-wide bus fare cap was previously set at £2, but Labour increased that to £3 shortly after winning the last general election.

Some city mayors have used their powers to keep local fares lower – including in Manchester, where Mr Burnham has placed bus services under public control. The £2 single bus fare cap was introduced in Greater Manchester in 2022 and has remained in place there despite the increase in the nationwide cap.

Mr Burnham is now looking at a wider £2 bus fare cap across Britain, and would implement it if he were successful in challenging Sir Keir for the Labour leadership after next Thursday’s Makerfield by-election.

A source close to Mr Burnham told The Telegraph: “One of his proudest achievements as mayor was his intervention on buses.”

He is expected to set out more details in the coming days about what the policy could look like as a wider offer to the country if he is elected.

During an “ask me anything” session on Reddit at the end of last week, Mr Burnham was asked whether he would consider supporting free public transport for all.

He responded: “In an ideal world, I would like to cut the cost of public transport as low as possible. I have made it free for 16 to 18-year-olds and recently removed the 9.30am restriction on older and disabled people’s bus passes.

“I will try to keep the £2 capped bus fare for as long as I possibly can. Low-cost or free public transport is, in my view, a brilliant way to get people back to work and connect communities.”

Sources close to Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, indicated she would support a £2 bus fare cap, pointing to her success in lobbying for free bus travel for children this summer, which Rachel Reeves announced last month.

Ms Alexander is understood to be frustrated that her predecessor, Louise Haigh, was not given enough funding to keep the £2 cap after Labour won office in 2024.

Ms Haigh is close ally of Mr Burnham and is considered a leading figure on the party’s soft-Left.

Mr Burnham’s attempt to link public transport to Britain’s worklessness crisis comes as the UK’s welfare bill is set to rise by £74bn in the next four years to reach £406bn by 2030,

Critics, including Sir Tony Blair, the former prime minister, have called that “unsustainable”.

Asked during an interview with The Telegraph last week whether he thought the welfare bill should be reduced, Mr Burnham replied: “Yes, I do.”

He told the BBC on Monday that the number of young people out of work was “a bit of a national scandal, to be honest” and argued for an “education system that is balanced between academic and technical”.

Mr Burnham is said to be considering the introduction of free travel for certain age groups if he were to enter No 10, extending Ms Reeves’s temporary policy for children aged between five and 15 this August.

The Manchester mayor’s forthcoming proposals are the latest in a policy blitz. He has already declared that Sir Keir’s administration has unfairly targeted Britain’s pubs and farmers.

Mr Burnham also urged Sir Keir to “go further” on tackling small boat crossings and agreed migration is a “huge issue” for voters. 

“I think we need to make greater use of detention, so that people who’ve got no basis for a claim are not actually admitted into the country, but that’s dealt with quickly and there’s speedier return.”

“The issue has to be dealt with. It is something that just has to be gripped and gripped properly, because it is about trust in politics. And it is one of those things that is fraying that trust to a degree.”

His comments follow a u-turn on migration earlier in the campaign, which saw him back Shabana Mahmood’s plans to make it harder for migrants to obtain indefinate leave to remain. 

The Makerfield by-election is taking place after Josh Simons, the outgoing Labour MP, stepped aside in the hope of allowing Mr Burnham to return to the Commons to move against Sir Keir.

The Prime Minister has been fighting for his political survival since Labour’s historic defeat at the local elections last month, which prompted almost 100 backbenchers to demand his exit. However, Mr Burnham is currently unable to challenge him for the leadership because he is not an MP.

data.title: Asda tower blocks threaten to spoil views at Kew Gardens
data.description:
Asda’s new west London tower blocks threaten to spoil the views at Kew Gardens, Historic England has warned.

The supermarket’s plan to build housing blocks up to 35 storeys high around a new superstore in Park Royal, west London, could disrupt sight lines from the historic Royal Botanic Gardens in Kew, the heritage watchdog said.

Asda is planning to build around 1,510 homes at its 10-acre Park Royal site, which it is redeveloping in partnership with Barratt London. The pair hope to create a new “town centre” with a 123,900-sq-ft flagship superstore and community hub.

However, the Government’s heritage watchdog has written to local planning officials to raise “concerns” about the effect on Kew Gardens, which sits three miles away.

In a consultation response, Historic England warned the tower blocks could invade views of the gardens’ landmark Orangery when viewed from across its Great Lawn.

The Orangery, which is connected to a surviving part of the gardens’ 18th-century landscape, was originally commissioned by Augusta, Dowager Princess of Wales. It was built in 1761 by Sir William Chambers, the architect, to house citrus trees, but its light levels were too low for the plants to thrive.

From 1863 until 1959, the Orangery served instead as a timber museum. The building, which features high ceilings and grand, arched windows, now houses a restaurant.

The body has said it would not object to Asda’s proposals if it tested and confirmed that the project would not affect views from the botanic gardens, which were declared a Unesco World Heritage Site in 2003.

However, it “could be a serious matter with consequences” for the gardens if the tower blocks spoil the views.

Under the Unesco designation, the Orangery is judged to have an “outstanding universal value” that makes “their permanent protection of the highest importance to the international community”, according to Historic England’s website.

Such sites are deemed to comprise a cultural or natural significance that is “so exceptional as to transcend national boundaries and to be of common importance for present and future generations of all humanity”.

Asda and Barratt must now prove the tower blocks will not spoil the protected views.

A Historic England spokesman said: “The views from Kew Gardens are very special, and so we have requested further information to understand if the proposed development would have any impact on this important place.”

Asda and Barratt London jointly stated that they “have carried out the testing Historic England requested to ensure that the development won’t negatively impact Kew Gardens”. The outcome will be shared in due course.

Kew Gardens declined to comment.

Major projects across London have been hampered by delays caused by heritage concerns. Recently, the Government’s planning inspectorate blocked plans for 867 homes at a 1980s shopping centre in Peckham on heritage grounds – a decision that Berkeley Group, its developer, claimed showed that London was uninvestable.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: VodafoneThree plots TalkTalk takeover
data.description:
VodafoneThree has tabled a bid to buy the consumer division of struggling broadband provider TalkTalk.

The telecoms giant, which was formed through a £15bn mega-merger a year ago, is understood to have entered the running to acquire around 1.75 million residential internet customers.

It tabled a second-round bid for TalkTalk last week alongside several other bidders, believed to be private-equity firms.

VodafoneThree has long been viewed as a potential acquirer of TalkTalk, which has put itself up for sale as it grapples with ballooning debt costs.

Rival Virgin Media O2 previously abandoned plans for a full takeover of TalkTalk, valued at up to £3bn, including debt.

More recently, it had been exploring a smaller potential offer for the consumer division, but has walked away.

Analysts now estimate that TalkTalk’s consumer arm could be worth between £200m and £300m.

A spokesman for VodafoneThree said: “We’re very happy with our organic strategy on fixed, where we’re the fastest growing in the market.”

TalkTalk declined to comment.

The takeover interest, first reported by the Financial Times, will come as a much-needed boost to TalkTalk, which has been forced to borrow heavily to stave off collapse.

The company raised more than £100m from shareholders, including Ares Management, last summer amid concerns about its liquidity and ability to meet debt covenants.

That came less than a year after TalkTalk secured a £235m emergency bailout from Ares and Sir Charles Dunstone, its founder, to avoid a looming debt default.

But the fresh cash injections have largely been used to pay off debts to suppliers, including BT’s Openreach, which threatened to block TalkTalk from putting new customers on its network.

Meanwhile, revenues have fallen as the company cuts back on costs, including marketing, leading to a fall in customer numbers.

Its broadband customer base stood at 1.75 million at the end of last year, down by a quarter on the previous year, while revenues fell 10pc to just under £1.3bn.

TalkTalk has broken itself up into parts and is also seeking a buyer for its wholesale division, called Platform X.

VodafoneThree’s swoop underscores efforts to expand aggressively in broadband, with bosses aiming to double its UK customer base to more than four million by 2034. Shares in rival BT dropped as much as 8pc after news of the takeover interest emerged.

The Telegraph last year revealed that VodafoneThree was also exploring plans to launch a subscription TV service as it seeks to capitalise on its newfound scale.

data.title: South East Water under fire after taps run dry during heatwave
data.description:
South East Water is facing renewed scrutiny from MPs after taps ran dry during the May bank holiday heatwave.

More than a thousand customers across Kent and East Sussex were left without water during the weekend after a series of infrastructure failures.

The crisis has prompted fresh criticism of South East Water just weeks after Dave Hinton, its former chief executive, resigned because of his response to a string of recent supply issues.

Chris Train, the company’s former chairman, also resigned earlier this month.

‘Getting the basics right’

Rosie Duffield, the MP for Canterbury, said: “Despite the recent dramatic change of management at South East Water, we have yet to see any change in acceptable levels of service or visible leadership. Yet again my constituents are left without water in a heatwave, the most basic necessity.

“We need to see proper investment, effective management and urgent intervention by Ofwat and the Government to ensure these incidents stop happening on a regular basis.”

Alistair Carmichael, the chairman of the Commons environment committee, said: “Yet again it is the long-suffering customers of South East Water who bear the consequences of the company’s failure.

“The select committee highlighted the lack of resilience in South East Water and sadly this latest outage provides further proof of that.”

Mr Carmichael, who spearheaded calls for Mr Hinton and Mr Train to resign, added: “They need to focus on getting the basics right, being honest with their customers and to stop always looking to blame some external problem.”

The crisis began on Saturday when a pumping station in Charing, at the foot of the North Downs in Kent, suffered an outage that triggered a significant reduction in water availability.

It hit during the hottest May weekend on record in England as South East Water customers filling up paddling pools and watering their gardens put severe demand on depleted supplies.

The disruption continued for three days, with residents in the Kent village of Challock still forced to rely on a bottled water station on Monday.

South East Water struck a casual tone on social media on Saturday as it urged customers to use water sparingly.

The water provider said: “Sun’s out. We know the drill: nobody wants a water company telling them to save water when there’s leaks. Fair point.

“Our reservoirs are healthy. There is no shortage of water. But if we all use hoses at Saturday lunchtime, the water pressure drops and homes at the top of the hill can run dry.

“Spacing out heavy water tasks over the week means everyone on your street keeps their water pressure.”

South East Water has come under scrutiny because of a string of shortages that left homes without water for weeks.

In December, around 24,000 customers in the Tunbridge Wells area went without water for a fortnight and were forced to rely on collecting water bottles from stations.

Mr Hinton rated his company’s response to the incident as an “eight out of 10”. However, the environment committee said it had no confidence in the utility’s leadership and accused bosses of a “culture of unaccountability”.

South East Water was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Trump backer’s Hollywood takeover threatens democracy, warn journalists
data.description:
The Warner Bros takeover by a billionaire Trump ally will pave the way for “improper political meddling”, leading US media figures have warned.

More than 200 academics, documentary makers and TV journalists have signed an open letter warning that the proposed takeover by David Ellison’s Paramount Skydance will have “severe consequences for American democracy”.

Warner Bros shareholders have backed the merger with Paramount, which will take control of the CNN news network as well as the historic Hollywood studios.

However, the letter, orchestrated by the Freedom of the Press Foundation, raised concerns about editorial intervention in CNN’s output, pointing to recent changes at CBS News, which was acquired by Mr Ellison as part of his $8bn (£6bn) takeover of Paramount last year.

Among the signatories is Jim Acosta, a former CNN host, and Laura Poitras, who won an Oscar for her documentary on Edward Snowden.

It accused CBS of making “sweeping changes” to appease Donald Trump, including pulling segments critical of the US president’s policies.

Mr Ellison held a recent dinner in Washington in honour of Mr Trump, while the US president has described the Paramount owner’s father, Larry Ellison, the Oracle billionaire, as a “good friend”.

‘Big fat bribe’

Several high-profile CBS reporters, including 60 Minutes host Anderson Cooper, have left the news network since Paramount installed Right-wing journalist Bari Weiss as its editor-in-chief.

The Late Show, hosted by Stephen Colbert, was cancelled after the presenter branded Paramount’s decision to settle a $16m lawsuit with Mr Trump shortly before the Ellison takeover was approved as a “big fat bribe”.

Paramount said the cancellation was “purely a financial decision” and “not related in any way to the show’s performance, content or other matters happening at Paramount”.

Sharyn Alfonsi, another veteran 60 Minutes correspondent, this week said she was pushed out of the network after clashing with executives over its output.

In an excoriating statement, Ms Alfonsi accused CBS of punishing her for “refusing to sanitise factually accurate reporting” and sending a “chilling message to the entire newsroom”.

The letter raised concerns about similar interference in CNN’s reporting.

It said: “With this track record, Ellison will likely alter CNN’s editorial direction (not to mention meddle with HBO’s documentaries) to be more friendly to the administration, threatening press freedom.

“We urge federal and state regulators and lawmakers, as well as Paramount shareholders and news consumers, to treat this merger not as an arms-length and above board business transaction, but as what it plainly is: a political arrangement to circumvent constitutional safeguards, with severe consequences for American democracy.”

It comes after Christiane Amanpour, CNN’s long-serving chief international anchor, this month raised concerns about the channel’s independence following the takeover.

Speaking at the Truth Tellers conference in London, she said: “Clearly I’m concerned ... I am obviously, as a person, as a journalist with a record, concerned.”

In addition to potential editorial interference, insiders fear sweeping job cuts if Paramount seeks to merge CNN with CBS. Mr Ellison’s company has said it will cut at least $6bn in costs after the deal.

Mr Ellison defended his takeover in a recent interview with CNBC, saying CNN was an “incredible brand with an incredible team, and we absolutely believe in the independence that needs to be maintained”.

He added: “We’re going to invest in the news business, and we think this transaction will be a positive for both CBS News and CNN.

“Editorial independence will absolutely be maintained. It’s maintained at CBS – it’ll be maintained at CNN.”

Thousands of Hollywood stars, including Emma Thompson, Mark Ruffalo and Ben Stiller, have also signed an open letter opposing the deal, warning that it could lead to fewer films being made and less choice for audiences.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Paddy Power owner to abandon London Stock Exchange
data.description:
The owner of Paddy Power and Betfair is to ditch the London stock market after shifting its primary listing to New York.

Dublin-headquartered Flutter said its last day of trading in London will be on Friday, July 31, with the delisting to take effect the following week.

The move, which deals a fresh blow to the beleaguered London Stock Exchange, comes after bosses launched a review of the company’s future on the market last month.

In an update to investors on Friday, Flutter blamed poor levels of trading, high costs and red tape for its decision.

The gambling giant, which also owns Sky Bet and PokerStars, switched its primary listing to New York two years ago.

The shift came as the company pursued an aggressive expansion plan in the US through the acquisition of sports-betting company FanDuel as it sought to capitalise on a loosening of gambling laws in the US.

But Flutter’s defection comes amid a broader exodus of companies from London’s stock market in response to low valuations and a relative lack of activity.

Its exit mirrors a similar decision by building materials group CRH, which said in March that it would delist entirely after switching its primary listing to New York.

Other companies including fintech business Wise, construction equipment company Ashtead, investment firm Petershill Partners and drugmaker Indivior have also either switched their primary listings or left the London market completely in recent years.

The decline of London’s stock exchange stands in stark contrast to the booming US market, which is gearing up for a string of trillion-dollar floats.

Elon Musk’s SpaceX has raised $75bn (£55bn) in a record-breaking initial public offering that drew significant demand from armchair investors as well as institutional investors.

The satellite and rocket business could achieve a valuation of $1.8tn when shares begin trading on Friday.

Rival AI firms OpenAI and Anthropic are also preparing blockbuster stock market debuts this year as they seek to cash in on huge demand from investors who expect the technology to transform global economies.

Flutter’s decision to abandon London comes despite Rachel Reeves’s efforts to revitalise the exchange by cutting red tape in the City and encouraging savers to buy stocks and shares.

The Chancellor has also cut the amount of cash that savers are allowed to hold in tax-free individual savings accounts and urged pension funds to invest more in UK stocks.

Flutter reported revenues of $4.3bn in the first quarter, up 17pc on the previous year, thanks to growth in online gambling.

However, bosses cut guidance for the full year as they warned the company had suffered from unfavourable sports results, denting markets including the UK.

data.title: South East Water warns customers will be cut off as reservoirs run dry
data.description:
South East Water has told customers to brace for fresh shortages as the beleaguered utility’s reservoirs start to run dry.

The company warned that some customers would lose supplies on Wednesday and urged them to put water aside while they still can.

A spokesman for South East Water said 18,000 customers are currently affected and urged those who still have access to running water to use it for essential purposes only, meaning drinking, washing and cooking.

Matthew Dean, the head of operations control at South East Water, said: “Extremely high demand for tap water in this very hot weather means our stored water reservoirs are running very low in areas across Kent.

“Yesterday, we pumped 660 million litres of water to customers across our region, which is more than 100 million litres more than the average for a day.

“We are doing everything we can to get water into our reservoirs, but some customers will lose supplies this afternoon.”

The company said that Herne Bay and Whitstable on the Kentish coast were among the worst-affected areas as nearby storage reservoirs had reached a “critical” level.

The disruption comes days after more than 1,000 customers served by the supplier across Kent and the South East were left without water over the bank holiday weekend.

The crisis, which was caused by the failure of a pumping station in Charing at the foot of the North Downs in Kent, coincided with the hottest May weekend on record.

South East Water has blamed recent supply issues on the heatwave, while reservoir levels were also affected by one of the driest Aprils on record, with rainfall standing at 38pc of its average level, according to the Met Office.

But the issues follow severe disruption at the end of last year that left about 24,000 homes and businesses across Kent and East Sussex without water for almost a fortnight. Local businesses are estimated to have lost up to £8m as a result of the crisis.

Rosie Duffield, the MP for Canterbury, said: “I am surprised and disappointed that South East Water find themselves in this position yet again, and rather than lurching from crisis to crisis, it suggests that forward planning for increasingly warm temperatures is vital.”

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of South East Water, and Chris Train, the chairman, have both stood down in recent weeks amid intense criticism.

While Mr Hinton rated his company’s response to the incident as an “eight out of 10”, MPs on the environment select committee said they had no confidence in the utility’s leadership and accused bosses of a “culture of unaccountability”.

Feargal Sharkey, who is campaigning for water companies to be forced to invest more heavily in their infrastructure, said: “The recent outage demonstrates how fragile and on the edge of a precipice the whole public water supply is in southern England.”

South East Water added: “We’re sorry for the inconvenience caused and can reassure customers our teams are out working hard to resolve these issues as quickly as we can.”

data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: The endless data centre whine threatening to blight Britain’s peaceful countryside
data.description:
In the little town of Sterling, Virginia, 25 miles north-west of Washington, DC, residents have had enough of the data centre hum.

A few hundred metres from rows of neat, suburban houses, a vast warehouse operated by technology giant Vantage Data Centers has been whirring non-stop, according to its neighbours.

Videos posted on social media, taken from a road near the facility, reveal a tinny whine that locals say is audible almost all the time.

“They built a building with gas turbines in it – the gas turbines create this high-pitched whistle,” says Lindsay Shaw, a cyber security specialist who lives next door to one Vantage building. “There is just no stop.”

The area around Northern Virginia where Shaw lives, just south of the Potomac River, has been nicknamed “Data Centre Alley” for its cluster of sites that take advantage of the high-speed fibre grid. There are some 200 data centres in Loudoun County alone, helping to power America’s AI revolution.

According to Shaw, Vantage told residents its new facility would only need on-site gas power temporarily while the data centre was linked up to the local grid, which would mitigate the noise. Vantage did not respond to requests for comment. But Shaw says it has since told residents that it would now “be another two years”.

Across the US, the technology industry’s multitrillion-dollar spending plans for city-sized data centres have been met with a growing backlash. Campaigners argue that data centres are driving higher carbon emissions, ruining the landscape and placing strain on the power grid.

Increasingly, however, the group who are most vocal in their complaints is nearby residents, who have been left to deal with the infuriating sounds that their new data-centre neighbours are generating.

The noise pollution from these data centres can vary from piercing whines to a persistent gurgling as external generators rev up for weekly tests.

Daniel Bresette, the president of the Environmental and Energy Studies Institute in Washington, which has documented rising concerns about AI data centres, says this is “not just a nuisance”. “When you get close enough they can be very loud,” he adds.

It is a problem which threatens to blight swathes of English countryside soon.

According to the National Energy Systems Operator – Neso – which manages the UK’s grid, there are around 173 grid connection requests in the pipeline for new-build data centre projects as Labour seeks to attract a surge in AI investment.

Some of the biggest sites planned in the UK would be similar to the sprawling, town-sized campuses that US tech giants including OpenAI, Google, Meta and Elon Musk’s xAI have been constructing across Virginia, Texas and the Mid-West.

A growing number are being powered by on-site generators or gas turbines – worsening noise issues for neighbours.

Musk’s Colossus data site near Memphis, for instance, has been described by residents as like “Mordor”. It hosts 27 methane turbines that run night and day. xAI has reportedly spent $7m (£5.2m) on a sound-muffling barrier, but disgruntled locals have nicknamed the construct the “Temu soundwall”.

Around 100 proposed UK data sites are also exploring using on-site gas power. Others could use energy from waste power.

It is already starting to ruffle feathers. Across Britain, a number of data centre construction projects have met local resistance. Noise concerns are now cropping up among objections to new data projects.

The campaign group No to East Havering Data Centre, a proposed £15bn, 600-megawatt (MW) site, notes: “Data centres produce a constant 24-hour hum, which has been known to cause stress and damage to health in the US.”

North Weald Bassett parish council, in Epping Forest, Essex, lodged a complaint about a planned Google data centre, warning that locals were “extremely concerned” about the “unacceptable level of local environmental impacts relating to noise” from the proposed site.

UK planning rules state that any increase in noise levels of 10 decibels (dB) above the average background noise could be considered a significant adverse effect for planning purposes.

This generally means that if you are already living near a major road or within earshot of the M25, the construction of a new nearby data centre may not tip the scales.

“Any proposed new noise sources being introduced into an area are assessed through this method and compared with the existing background noise levels,” says Mark Jones, of the Chartered Institute of Environmental Health.

In the case of North Weald, residents argued that an average increase of 7dB under normal conditions or 18dB in the rare event of the backup generators being engaged, would have a harmful effect on the area.

However, local planning officers decided that the proposed noise levels at the facility were “not significant overall”.

A government spokesman said: “The whole Government is determined to create the right conditions for investment in the UK’s AI and data centre infrastructure, which will deliver jobs and opportunities that hardworking people deserve.

“Data centres come under the same planning rules when it comes to noise, as any other development, with policies in place to keep adverse impacts on communities to a minimum.”

Still, noise pollution is a common concern among those living near new developments. More contentious are claims that almost undetectable noise from data centres is more than an irritation but is also harmful to human health.

The potential effect of “infrasound” – extremely low-frequency noise that is imperceptible to the human ear – has prompted hotly contested claims that AI data centres could be causing health problems.

Andy Masley, an AI researcher who has criticised what he has called the data centre “panic”, argues such worries are “more or less completely fake” and are being “[mixed] up with very real issues with normal noise pollution”. However, he agrees that some data centres have been erected that “should absolutely not have been built so close to homes”.

Ketan Joshi, a renewable energy analyst, meanwhile, argues that objections to data centres that focus on unproven noise effects on human health risk being dismissed as “kooky” by tech executives.

He says they could be used by the industry to shrug off wider concerns about the projects, such as their “shockingly energy-intensive” nature.

Yet for many living near the centres in the US, the overriding issue is the problems they create for the area – and they are willing to take legal action.

Some communities and activist groups have already launched lawsuits against nearby data centre developers, including the NAACP in Memphis, which sued Musk’s xAI over alleged air pollution violations.

Its claim also alleged that one resident had been forced to wear earplugs to mask the endless noise of its Colossus site. xAI was contacted for comment.

In the town of Dowagiac, Michigan, locals sued the operator of a 30MW data centre claiming that their homes have been “physically invaded by excessive noise” that is “constant at all hours of the day, every day of the week”.

‘It impacts your quality of life’

Last week, Erin Brockovich joined calls for tighter controls on US data centres. Brockovich, an environmental activist, who in 1996 won $333m in a water contamination scandal, has launched a website to track the spread of data centres across the US.

On it, she called for “sustainable, secure and efficient AI data-centre practices”, warning that the “constant humming” could “disrupt sleep, daily life and wildlife in surrounding neighbourhoods”.

In Congress, US politicians have been pushing for tighter environmental controls over data centres from federal regulators. Dozens of state legislatures, meanwhile, have started looking into new environmental controls on data centres.

For Shaw, in Virginia, the experience of Sterling is a warning for those in the UK living in the shadow of new data projects.

“You can still hear it two or three kilometres away. It impacts your quality of life,” she says.

Locals no longer “want to be outside”. “We are trying to get the awareness out that these companies need to be respectful,” Shaw says.

If they are not, then tech companies should expect one thing: with each new project, complaints will only get louder and, soon, they will be impossible to ignore.

Additional reporting by Shaista Kheria

data.title: BP backs Amanda Blanc to lead search for new chairman
data.description:
BP has backed Dame Amanda Blanc to lead its search for a new chairman, despite investor concerns about the board’s judgement after the shock sacking of Albert Manifold.

Dame Amanda has received support from BP bosses to run a process to find a replacement for Mr Manifold after he was ousted as its chairman last week, just eight months into the job.

Ian Tyler, the interim chairman of the oil major, said: “At the request of the board, Amanda Blanc will lead the search process for BP’s next chair. 

“As in previous searches, this will be a rigorous process involving the entire board and the final decision will reflect our collective view.”

It means that Dame Amanda, the senior independent director at BP, will be leading the oil major’s search for a new chairman for the second time in a year.

She ran BP’s search for a replacement for Helge Lund in 2025, with the board appointing Mr Manifold as its new chairman last July.

The company’s decision to back Dame Amanda – which was first reported by the Financial Times – comes despite growing criticism from shareholders regarding the board’s conduct during the dismissal of Mr Manifold.

The board decided to sack Mr Manifold last week, less than a year into the role, saying there were “serious concerns” about his behaviour. A source close to the board alleged that Mr Manifold bullied and verbally abused colleagues.

Mr Manifold has said lies were being spread about him and suggested he was ousted for being too aggressive in pushing cost-cutting measures at BP. He is understood to be considering legal action.

The Telegraph revealed last weekend that investors were calling for a clear-out of the board because of the handling of the situation. 

One shareholder said: “The entire board – including the senior independent director, Amanda Blanc, who led both the hiring and the departure of Albert Manifold – needs to make a collective ‘soul search’ and learn any lessons.

“Over time, there will be the need to inject new blood in the board and progressively replace some members so that serenity can return.”

Investors raised fresh questions this week about how decisions are made at the oil major.

One large shareholder said: “The board recruited the outgoing chair and the chief executive, it’s impossible to believe they didn’t get what they asked for.

“Manifold was known as a direct communicator, laser focused on performance. The directors leading the search pronounced him on the day of his appointment as the ‘ideal candidate’ with a ‘relentless focus on performance’. It’s head scratching how the company could now find his approach to be inappropriate.”

They added: “BP is fast becoming synonymous with board level ineptitude, after a period of relative stability under the steady leadership of [former chief executive] Bob Dudley. Whomever comes in next needs to radically change the narrative of perceived failure.”

Shares in BP, which is on its third chairman and fifth chief executive since 2020, slumped by more than 5pc last week after news of Mr Manifold’s sacking broke.

The support for Dame Amanda, who is also the chief executive of insurer Aviva, came hours after it emerged that BP’s green energy boss was leaving the company as turmoil gripped the British oil and gas giant.

The company confirmed on Monday that William Lin was leaving as part of a major restructuring being carried out by Meg O’Neill, the new chief executive.

Mr Lin, a BP lifer, headed up the company’s gas and low-carbon business, which included investments in wind farms, solar power and hydrogen production.

In a note to employees, Ms O’Neill said: “We ​thank William for ​his leadership, impact ⁠and long-standing commitment to BP and wish him every success for the future.”

A BP spokesman declined to comment on his departure further.

Ben Mathews, the company secretary, is also on medical leave for stress, adding to the sense of turmoil at the top of the £80bn oil company.

data.title: Germany will not surrender Hugo Boss to Mike Ashley without a fight
data.description:
When it comes to foreign takeovers of national champions, the attitude of the German establishment could not be more different from that of successive UK governments.

In Britain we have a baffling tendency to see overseas ownership of important UK companies as overwhelmingly a good thing to the extent that almost no British firm – aside from a small handful of exceptions – is out of bounds.

On the contrary, we lay out the welcome mat on the spurious grounds that this represents a genuine form of foreign investment, and that can only be a good thing in a free market economy, naive ministers are fond of telling us.

This, of course, is simplistic nonsense as history has proven repeatedly. Whether it’s Cadbury, Heathrow airport, Thames Water or any other number of prestigious and important national assets, foreign ownership has repeatedly proven to be detrimental to our interests.

Yet Britain continues to allow UK companies to be passed from one buyer to the next like a second-hand board game at a village fete.

Germany, on the other hand, has a natural aversion to foreign interlopers eyeing the best of Deutschland AG, which can stray into outright hostility, as Mike Ashley may be about to find out after his Frasers empire launched a daring £1.7bn offer to buy Hugo Boss.

It is certainly hard to imagine a situation more likely to ruffle the feathers of the German elites than a self-made British tracksuit tycoon with a natural disdain for the ruling class wanting to waltz off with a national icon such as Hugo Boss.

Any overseas bid for the fashion house is unlikely to be welcomed. The company’s links to the Nazis during the Second World War mean the rest of Germany has a somewhat complicated relationship with Hugo Boss.

Its founder Hugo Ferdinand Boss was a loyal Nazi Party member and his factory in Metzingen, Germany, produced uniforms for the SS and Hitler Youth, using Polish and French forced labour.

But the company has confronted this history, commissioning an independent historical study in 2011 to investigate its wartime activities and expressing “profound regret” for the treatment of those – mostly women – who were forced to work in the factory.

These days it is known less as a facilitator of dictatorships and more for its engineering prowess, as a large employer and its world-renowned luxury couture.

Indeed, it is the country’s only real global fashion label, so one suspects Germany will fight for it to stay that way as it so often does when the country’s corporate beasts find themselves in the crosshairs of overseas predators.

Ashley would be advised to take a crash course in the recent cross-border takeover saga that has engulfed Commerzbank as he plots his next move. The German bank has been stuck in an almost perpetual state of restructuring since its bailout at the hands of the German state during the financial crisis.

Yet the response to a bumper €24bn (£20.4bn) bid from Italy’s Unicredit has been one of outright hostility bordering on horror and outrage in some quarters at the prospect of this Ältere Dame of German finance falling into foreign hands.

Commerzbank has been subjected to repeated overhaul attempts, numerous emergency capital raising and persistently weak profitability.

However, it remains a huge provider of export finance and one of the largest lenders to the country’s army of small and medium-sized Mittelstand businesses, and Commerzbank executives have warned its loss of independence represents a direct threat to the German economy.

Bettina Orlopp, the chief executive, fears that a takeover could lead to “deep cuts, massive job losses and a retreat from entire business areas”, she told shareholders only last month.

The German government meanwhile has described Unicredit’s approach as “completely inappropriate and unfair”, and the finance ministry has said a “hostile, aggressive takeover” of such an important institution would be unacceptable.

UK companies haven’t fared much better on the few occasions they’ve strayed into German corporate territory. A proposed merger of the London Stock Exchange and its Frankfurt counterpart in 2016 was ultimately scuppered by EU regulators.

However, if Brussels hadn’t blocked it, opposition among German politicians to the prospect of the combined entity being based in the UK, particularly after Brexit, probably would have torpedoed the tie-up anyway.

Ashley would do well too to study the swashbuckling exploits of Vodafone, which could provide enough material for a book about what it’s like to navigate the political minefield that comes with pursuing Germany’s corporate giants.

When Vodafone tabled a blockbuster £100bn bid for German telecoms titan Mannesmann in 1999, it unleashed a tidal wave of fierce opposition. The German tabloid Bild urged shareholders not “to watch, powerless, as a pearl of German industry is sacrificed to greed”.

Even Gerhard Schröder, the German chancellor at the time, weighed in, warning “hostile takeovers destroy corporate culture”. Chris Gent, the free-wheeling Vodafone boss, meanwhile was dismissed in Germany as “Herr Cricket” because of his love of a quintessentially English game.

Vodafone’s €7.7bn (£6.6bn) takeover of Kabel Deutschland in 2013 passed with less drama then quickly ran into serious problems when a sudden and unexpected law change led to the loss of four million subscribers in apartment blocks. Its German arm is still trying to recover from the hit.

The potential for serious conflict regarding Frasers’ interest in Hugo Boss is tempered by the fact that it’s not a systemically important lender or a provider of vital communications infrastructure. 

It makes suits, albeit snazzy ones, and other trendy clothes, therefore it isn’t quite so critical to German prosperity.

However, at the very least one would assume some desire to ensure an undoubted fashion icon doesn’t fall into the hands of someone whose fortune has been built on polyester tracksuits and cheap gold umbrellas. 

There will also be a desire to protect an employer of an estimated 19,000 people, more than 3,000 of them in Germany where manufacturing is still hugely important.

Politicians may also contrast the country’s strong labour culture with Ashley’s record, which includes historic allegations of sweatshop conditions at its site in Shirebrook, Derbyshire. The billionaire apologised publicly, and took personal responsibility for the failings at the warehouse.

Still, a man who seems to revel in his status as an outsider and a rabble-rouser is unlikely to shy away from any fight.

“If you don’t trust me, then don’t own the shares. Don’t sit in the cheap seats at the back and say I can’t be house-trained,” he once told unhappy investors. 

Germany’s stuffy elites may be in for the shock of their lives.

data.title: The best bank accounts if you earn £100k
data.description:
Reaching a six-figure income is an impressive financial milestone. While it can mean you’re dragged into the dreaded 60pc tax trap, it also comes with advantages – such as qualifying for a premier bank account.

These accounts are exclusively for higher earners, and come with a host of perks such as dedicated financial support, preferential savings and mortgage rates, and a variety of insurance policies. Some have generous switching incentives, too.

Here, Telegraph Money has compiled the best of these exclusive accounts, revealing what each offers high-flyers.

What is a premier bank account?

Premier bank accounts are pitched somewhere between everyday current accounts and private banking, according to Clare Stinton, senior personal finance analyst at Hargreaves Lansdown.

“Qualifying for a premier bank account is a little like getting pass access to an airport lounge, granted it’s not private jet territory, but it is a step up – typically linked to your income – that comes with special treatment and perks,” she said.

“What you get varies by account and provider, but banking benefits typically include elevated and personalised customer service, fee-free overdrafts, higher daily cash withdrawals, and exclusive rates on mortgages and loans.

“Many accounts also throw in lifestyle perks, everything from shopping discounts, to travel and health insurance, to lounge airport passes and TV streaming subscriptions.”

Unlike packaged bank accounts that can offer similar benefits, some premier bank accounts won’t charge a monthly fee.

Best premier bank accounts

Below is a list of some of the best premier bank accounts currently available and what they offer. You’ll see that NatWest has three entries here – while they can all be opened by someone earning £100,000, they come with very different features and monthly costs.

Lloyds Premier account

Eligibility requirements: You must have at least £100,000 of qualifying savings or investments with Lloyds or pay in £5,000 each month.

Cost: £15 monthly fee, but this is refunded when you meet the above criteria.

Switching bonuses: None.

Key features: Bupa Family GP and Wellbeing subscription, expert financial coaching, 1pc cashback on most debit card spending (up to £10 a month), preferential savings and mortgage rates, fee-free spending overseas, interest on current account balances up to £5,000, your choice of Lifestyle Benefit each year (such as a Disney+ subscription or cinema tickets).

NatWest Premier Select

Eligibility requirements: You will need to have a minimum of £100,000 sole income or £120,000 joint income paid into your NatWest account, or a minimum of £100,000 savings or investments held with NatWest, or a minimum £500,000 mortgage with NatWest.

Cost: £0 monthly fee.

Switching bonuses: Up to £1,000 for new customers. Get £250 if you apply online or via the app and switch using the Current Account Switch Service (CASS), plus pay in a minimum of £5,000. Get an extra £750 if you open a new Flexible Saver account with NatWest within 60 days of the switch completing, and deposit £100,000. T&Cs apply.

Features: Fee-free spending abroad, £500 interest-free overdraft, access to financial planning and advice.

NatWest Premier Reward

Eligibility requirements: You will need to have a minimum of £100,000 sole income or £120,000 joint income paid into your NatWest account, or a minimum of £100,000 savings or investments held with NatWest, or a minimum £500,000 mortgage with NatWest.

Cost: £2 monthly fee.

Switching bonuses: Up to £1,000 for new customers. Get £250 if you apply online or via the app and switch using the Current Account Switch Service, plus pay in a minimum of £5,000. Get an extra £750 if you open a new Flexible Saver account with NatWest within 60 days of the switch completing, and deposit £100,000. T&Cs apply.

Features: Access to financial planning and advice, earn £9 a month in Rewards when you set up two direct debits, and £1 in Rewards each month you log into the app, plus earn at least 1pc in Rewards at selected retail partners. Rewards can be donated to charity or redeemed for cash or e-gift codes.

NatWest Premier Reward Black

Eligibility requirements: You will need to have a minimum of £100,000 sole income or £120,000 joint income paid into your NatWest account, or a minimum of £100,000 savings or investments held with NatWest, or a minimum £500,000 mortgage with NatWest. You must be an existing NatWest Premier Select current account customer to upgrade your account.

Cost: £36 monthly fee.

Switching bonuses: None.

Features: Worldwide family travel insurance, airport lounge access, fee-free foreign spending, UK and Europe car breakdown cover, mobile phone insurance, home emergency cover, access to financial planning and advice, £500 interest-free overdraft, 24/7 concierge service, 25pc cashback on tickets to concerts and shows, discounts on cinema tickets, plus earn Rewards in the same way as the Premier Reward account.

Barclays Premier account

Eligibility requirements: You must have a current account with Barclays and pay in an annual income of at least £75,000 or have a balance of at least £100,000 in Barclays’ savings or investments, or both.

Cost: £0 monthly fee.

Switching bonuses: A “signature experience” worth up to £600 if you open a Premier Current Account in the Barclays app and switch using the CASS by Aug 27, paying in at least £4,000 within 30 days of opening. Experiences include premium access to certain live events, overnight stays at selected UK hotels, family days out and dining experiences at restaurants including the Savoy Grill and Lucky Cat. T&Cs apply.

Features: Access to a dedicated wealth planner and wealth manager, Premier banking 24/7 support and Premier financial guides, preferential savings and mortgage options, earn up to 15pc cashback at participating retailers, free Apple TV+ subscription, £500 interest-free overdraft, join Barclays Avios Rewards for £12 a month and collect Avios.

HSBC Premier bank account

Eligibility requirements: You must have an annual income of at least £100,000 paid into your account or have savings and investments of at least £100,000 with HSBC in the UK or already qualify for HSBC Premier in another country.

Cost: £0 monthly fee.

Switching bonuses: Get £500 if you make a full switch using the CASS, and within 90 days either pay in your monthly salary (typically £5,100 a month for those who receive the required salary), or deposit and maintain at least £100,000 in savings or investments. T&Cs apply.

Features: Worldwide family travel insurance, access to a range of online health services including digital GP appointments, access to an HSBC Global Money account to convert, hold and send money in multiple currencies, preferential rates on loans, mortgages and savings, access to financial advice, £500 interest-free overdraft.

Premier bank accounts compared

Is a premier account worth it?

If you’re considering a premier bank account that charges a fee, you’ll need to be certain that you’ll make use of the benefits.

Will Stevens, head of wealth planning at Killik & Co, said: “Choosing a premier account means considering what you need to prioritise most. For example, some will provide family benefits for children or parents, while others might provide international banking support and fee-free international transfers, which provides more flexibility for those with global interests.”

It’s crucial to review the exclusions and conditions of any insurance policies included with the account too, as these could render the benefit useless. Some mobile phone insurance policies only include one phone, for instance, while travel insurance will probably only cover you until a certain age.

It may be worthwhile to compare how much you’d pay for cover separately to see whether the account offers you a good deal.

Other features also require some scrutiny. Kate Steere, banking expert at comparison site Finder, said: “Some premier account perks include preferential rates for mortgages and savings, but in reality, the rates on offer aren’t top of the market and could dissuade you from shopping around.”

Finally, remember that if you are required to have a certain amount of savings or investments with the bank, only the first £120,000 you hold in a current account or savings account is covered by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS).

data.title: Energy failures are destined to doom Wall Street’s AI euphoria
data.description:
Sign up here for more exclusive insight from Ambrose Evans-Pritchard in our Economic Intelligence newsletter, delivered direct to your inbox every Tuesday.

Market manias have patterns. The most powerful ones are genuine technological revolutions pushed far beyond rational limits by crowd psychology.

By mid-1999 it was already clear to veteran investors and students of economic history that the dotcom bubble had reached parabolic insanity.

The speculative momentum was still unstoppable – and would run a lot further – but grown-ups knew by then that few of the high-flying start-ups were ever going to generate a viable revenue stream. The authentic success stories would have to fight each other in a cannibalistic struggle for survival.

We are nearing the same point today with AI, although this time for a different and overwhelming reason. The $20tn (£15tn) valuation of hyperscalers, chipmakers and the larger AI complex, has wildly outpaced the electrical infrastructure needed to run data centres and sustain the technology on anything like the projected scale.

The physical constraint is rock hard. “Our grid in the United States hasn’t had any meaningful upgrade since the 1970s,” said Bobby Majumder from the industrial law firm FBT Gibbons.

The threat to AI stock mania is not so much lack of energy – though that is serious – but rather the global bottleneck of transformers, substations, switchgear, transmission lines and all the unsexy stuff we rarely think about, leaving aside the acute shortage of skilled workers in the US able to install and run such kit.

A single big campus in the data centre hub of Hays County, Texas – an area where I once played a lot of golf (misspent youth) and know well – can use 10 million gallons of water a day for evaporative cooling and power generation, draining the Edwards Aquifer that also supplies the Austin-San Antonio corridor.

“Nobody is talking about cooling; nobody is talking about water,” said Majumder, speaking at the recent Marshall & Stevens forum on energy infrastructure. “The farmers are not going to be happy at all about you pumping down their aquifer for cooling.”

There are other obvious catalysts that could puncture the bubble. Stubborn US inflation – input prices are rising at the fastest pace in four years – may force the Federal Reserve to stop its “stealth-QE” via bill purchases. The bond markets may hold Kevin Warsh’s feet to the fire as he takes over the institution.

Inflation may stop Scott Bessent, the poacher turned gamekeeper now running the US treasury like a hedge fund, from using the $8tn money market to help soak up massive fiscal deficits at the peak of the economic cycle.

Cheaper “commoditised” AI from the likes of DeepSeek in China may start to undercut American rivals, threatening the implicit pricing model behind today’s equity valuations. If it is true that DeepSeek v4 can achieve 80pc-90pc of the performance of Anthropic’s Claude at 10pc of the cost, you start to see the problem.

Liaquat Ahamed, author of the wonderful Lords of Finance covering the Great Depression and now releasing his new book 1873, likens the AI boom to the American railway mania after the Civil War. Routes were duplicated in the rush for dominance.

Costly lines passed through sparsely inhabited regions where there would never be enough human traffic in time to justify the scale of debt issuance.

What brought it to a crashing halt in 1873 was fallout from the Franco-Prussian War and the collapse of world silver prices, a forgotten episode that led to two decades of global deflation and was known thereafter as the Great Depression until trumped by the more traumatic 1930s.

Europeans were the buyers-in-chief of US railway bonds. When Europe went into financial convulsions and the money dried up, the railway barons struggled to tap the debt market.

Historical parallels can mislead, but there is now the little matter of Gulf War II, nearing 100 days of regional havoc and incubating at least another 100 days of future havoc, even if peace breaks out today. The biggest sting is in the tail, and it has yet to come.

The Gulf states and Saudi Arabia between them have $6tn of assets in sovereign wealth funds, plus $1.7tn of foreign exchange reserves. Flows of recycled petro-wealth have been a critical source of investment for US infrastructure and tech.

The petro-states have pledged to cover a further $2.4tn of funding for US chips, AI, quantum computing and biotech. This is now for the birds. They are licking their wounds and may have to sell US assets.

So, yes, there are plenty of threats out there. But the electricity crunch is the clearest and most certain.

So far this year, data companies have asked the Texas power system operator ERCOT for grid connections for 200 projects. Outstanding requests have spiralled to 446 gigawatts (GW) by 2033. That is five times peak power use for the entire state of Texas today, or 13 times the UK’s average use.

“That’s just not happening. It’ll be 10pc, maximum,” said Brent Nelson, an energy strategist at Ascend Analytics. I would bet on 5pc.

Hyperscalers can try to leapfrog the grid bottleneck by building their own power plants, but that will not solve the problem either, at least not in time to alleviate the burden of fast-mounting and opaque AI debt.

It took 17 years to plan, license and build the recent Vogtle nuclear plant in Georgia. Costs ballooned from $12bn to $30bn. Small modular reactors may be cheaper per gigawatt – don’t hold your breath – but none yet exist in the West, and there will be no serious supply chain until circa 2040.

Shale gas frackers can drill until they drop, but that makes no difference if there are no gas turbines available on the world market. The waiting list for heavy-duty models used in combined-cycle plants has stretched to seven years, although hyperscalers with the deepest pockets are jumping the queue for a fat fee with 2030 delivery dates.

Germany’s Siemens Energy, America’s GE Vernova and Japan’s Mitsubishi Heavy Industries have a triopoly with 90pc of the global market for these marvels of engineering. The Chinese have only just begun to crack the complex metal alloys which must withstand temperatures of 1,500F (or about 815C).

The trio have boosted their combined global production to 45 GW a year, but they are slow-walking further expansion because a) they fear a boom-bust cycle and b) because they are making a fortune on what GE Vernova’s boss candidly calls “premium pricing”.

The turbine package cost was $200 per kilowatt (kW) in 2022, but consultants Wood Mackenzie expects it to hit $600 per kW by next year. Good luck with gas, my friends.

The AI revolution is real. The language models are fabulous. The technology will make economic life almost unrecognisable by mid-century.

But the internet revolution was also real in 1999 before the Nasdaq index dropped 77pc, flushed out the commercial nonsense and overshot in the other direction.

Don’t track Nvidia chip orders if you want to know where the AI market is heading. Track the metaphorical picks and shovels that make it all possible. You cannot wish away a physical constraint.

data.title: Miliband’s clean energy tsar quits Labour’s flagship net zero quango
data.description:
Jürgen Maier, Ed Miliband’s clean power tsar, has quit Labour’s flagship energy quango after just two years in the job.

Mr Maier was appointed in 2024 to set up Great British Energy (GBE), which Labour’s election manifesto pledged would “cut bills for good” and create jobs “in every corner of the UK”.

Confirming his departure, Mr Maier said his role at GBE had always been to act as “start-up chair” through its first two years.

“I will remain fully committed to GBE until the new chair has been selected, which we expect to be later this autumn,” he wrote on LinkedIn.

However, his departure will raise fresh questions about the future of GBE, which many industry insiders believe lacks the financial resources to have any significant effect on bills or UK jobs.

Mr Maier, a veteran engineer and industrialist, was previously chief executive at Siemens, the £200bn German engineering and technology giant.

Before his appointment, Mr Maier was known for his criticism of Brexit, arguing that quitting the EU would hit UK industries like a “slow puncture” and make it far harder to export.

At GBE, he was given a budget of £8.3bn, a relatively small sum in the context of UK energy investments. A single large offshore wind farm, for example, costs several billion pounds.

About £2.5bn of that money was subsequently hived off to support Great British Nuclear, a separate quango.

The role was also highly political after Labour made the creation of GBE a central pillar of its 2024 election manifesto.

Sir Keir Starmer promised GBE would create 1,000 jobs at its Scottish headquarters and cut bills by up to £300.

However, in early 2025, Mr Maier faced criticism after admitting that creating 1,000 jobs could take two decades. He also repeatedly refused to say when household bills would be cut at all, let alone by £300.

He faced further scrutiny in March this year after he argued in a LinkedIn post for “more North Sea oil and gas production” to slow job losses in the sector.

The remarks clashed with Mr Miliband’s pledge to halt all new drilling in UK waters.

Within hours of his comments coming to the attention of Mr Miliband’s officials, Mr Maier issued another social media post “clarifying” that he was “fully supportive” of the Government’s position.

GBE’s main project to date has been a £200m investment in rooftop solar for public-sector buildings such as schools and hospitals.

However, this has also run into controversy because of claims that the projects it was funding were using solar panels linked to Chinese slave labour. Mr Miliband ordered GBE last year to ensure there was no slavery in its supply chain.

Questioned in the Commons this week, Michael Shanks, the energy minister, said GBE was “setting up a function within to look at sustainable supply chains and to make sure they’re free from [forced labour]”.

An advertisement for Mr Maier’s successor said GBE was offering a £114,000 salary for the two-days-per-week post.

Dan McGrail, GBE’s chief executive, said: “After completing his term in October, Jürgen Maier will be stepping down from his role as start-up chair of Great British Energy. This marks a natural milestone in GBE’s evolution.”
