data.title: Don’t drive after staying up late to watch World Cup, AA warns
data.description:
Tired drivers staying up late to watch the World Cup could cause a spike in road crashes, a motoring organisation has warned.

The AA’s Accident Assist claim support service said fatigue has a similar effect on drivers as alcohol, with slower reaction times, worse decision-making and less vigilance.

The difference in time zones between the World Cup hosts – the US, Canada and Mexico – compared with the UK means many of England and Scotland’s matches will not finish until very late at night or even the early hours of the morning.

It will be nearly 4am before Scotland’s opening match against Haiti on Sunday ends.

AA Accident Assist warned it could be “particularly dangerous” if drivers followed late nights watching football with journeys during the school run and rush hour.

Winding down the window to let in fresh air or turning the radio up are not effective ways to combat tiredness behind the wheel, it said, and drivers should stop and take a break at “the first sign of tiredness”.

It recommended that they drink two cups of coffee or an equivalent caffeinated drink, and take a nap of about 15-20 minutes.

Tim Rankin, managing director of AA Accident Assist, said: “While fans are dreaming about the prospect of their team lifting the cup, it could soon become a personal nightmare if they crash due to a lack of sleep.

“Drowsy driving is an underestimated risk on our roads, and we encourage drivers watching the World Cup to get as much sleep as possible before getting behind the wheel.

“Driving is a skill that needs full attention, and battling through tiredness won’t keep drivers alert.

“Recognising the signs of drowsiness, pulling over and taking a nap can make a huge difference in keeping our roads safe.”

data.title: BP drops net zero division in wake of boardroom turmoil
data.description:
BP has relegated its net zero division in an overhaul aimed at increasing its focus on oil and gas, just weeks after the company’s chairman was dismissed.

The oil and gas giant confirmed plans on Tuesday to split its business into “two distinct segments” – upstream and downstream.

It currently operates with three business divisions, including one focused on “gas and low carbon energy”.

The restructuring is part of a continued effort to win over shareholders amid growing scrutiny regarding its weak share price performance compared with rivals.

Questions about the company’s strategy have heightened after the unexpected departure of Albert Manifold last month, leaving the oil giant on the hunt for its third chairman in three years.

The upstream team will include BP’s oil and gas operations, exploration and oil production, although it will also include its carbon capture and storage businesses.

Its downstream division will include refineries, pipelines, biofuels, aviation and hydrogen and its Castrol vehicle lubricants offering.

Its renewable power operations were previously part of its gas and low carbon group.

However, these will now be carved off under BP’s “Other Businesses” segment as the company said it would continue to “advance a capital-light model” for net zero technology.

Meg O’Neill, the chief executive of BP, said: “Focusing BP around two distinct segments is an important step in accelerating delivery. It will reduce complexity and strengthen execution.

“BP has an incredibly capable team, with deep expertise across the oil and gas value chain. We are capitalising on opportunities across our portfolio, strengthening the balance sheet and unlocking sustainable growth.”

The latest move highlights BP’s ongoing retreat from renewables.

The oil and gas giant had attempted to go green under Bernard Looney, its former chief executive, although it has since sought to reverse this after pressure from investors such as Elliot Investment.

Under Ms O’Neill, the former boss of Australian oil and gas producer Woodside Energy, BP has been attempting to revive its ambitions in the fossil fuel industry with renewed exploration and drilling.

Having been appointed last October, Mr Manifold was among the executives steering this shift.

However, he was ousted a fortnight ago amid claims of “bullying” and “verbal abuse”.

Mr Manifold has denied the claims and dismissed them as “lies”. He is understood to be exploring his options for a potential legal challenge against his former employer.

He has also accused some leaders at BP of engaging in “unnecessary or excessive expenditure”.

Mr Manifold said last month there was a “considerable distance between driving an organisation with urgency and the characterisation of my conduct that is now being put about”.

data.title: Home Bargains prepares to rescue 217-year old Denby Pottery
data.description:
Home Bargains is plotting a rescue of the historic Denby pottery brand, two months after the 217-year-old British manufacturer collapsed into administration.

The discount retailer, owned by Tom Morris, a Liverpool businessman and Labour donor, is understood to be pursuing a deal for the Denby name and other assets.

If successful, the deal would hand control of one of Britain’s most recognisable ceramics brands to a retailer which is primarily known for selling cut-price versions of homeware items, including cleaning products, garden furniture and bird feeders.

Sky News reported on Tuesday that Home Bargains was among the parties pursuing Denby through a sale process that was being run by FRP Advisory.

Other homeware groups are also said to have expressed an interest in buying the brand’s assets out of administration.

Founded in 1809, Denby had manufactured pottery for more than two centuries before it plunged into administration at the end of March.

The company had produced tableware from locally sourced Derbyshire clay on the same site where the pottery first originated.

However, in its latest financial accounts, it warned it was struggling with higher costs and weak consumer spending.

Earlier this year, it entered administration, becoming the latest casualty of a crisis that has swept through Britain’s ceramics industry. Denby’s collapse ignited a political row about energy costs.

Campaigners argued that pottery manufacturers were left at a disadvantage by being excluded from government support packages, which had been made available to other energy-intensive industries during the war in Iran amid rising oil and gas prices.

Former workers backed a campaign to include ceramics in the British Industry Supercharger scheme, which helps sectors such as steel and chemicals with electricity bills.

More than 40,000 people signed a petition calling for ministers to extend the relief to pottery makers. The Government eventually unveiled a £120m support package for the ceramics sector in May, but only after Denby had already fallen into administration and halted manufacturing.

It prompted criticism that ministers had failed to act quickly enough to protect one of Britain’s best-known manufacturing sectors, with former workers at Denby claiming support came “too late”.

Denby also owns the Burleigh pottery brand, which was rescued in 2010 when the then Prince of Wales, now the King, helped secure £9m for emergency repairs at its historic Middleport Pottery site.

The brand has long been associated with British manufacturing heritage, with its products sold in several overseas markets including South Korea, China and the US.

FRP Advisory has continued to trade Denby during the administration process, although manufacturing operations ceased in April, triggering dozens of redundancies.

More than 130 jobs have been lost since Denby entered administration in March.

A takeover by Home Bargains would mark an unlikely pairing between a heritage pottery maker and a retail empire built on bargain prices.

Mr Morris, who owns Home Bargains’ holding company TJ Morris, has become one of Britain’s wealthiest retailers through the rapid expansion of the chain, which now operates hundreds of stores nationwide and has ambitions to grow further.

Nicknamed “the richest Scouser to have ever lived”, he recently took a £66m dividend from his retail empire after annual sales climbed to £4.5bn.

FRP Advisory declined to comment. Home Bargains was approached for comment.

data.title: WH Smith issues urgent cash plea as airport sales crash
data.description:
WH Smith is seeking to raise more than £120m in a bid to combat falling airport sales caused by the Iran war.

The stationery chain announced the capital raise alongside a fresh profit warning on Tuesday, which bosses blamed on flight cancellations and a drop in passenger numbers.

It said it was attempting to strengthen its balance sheet by issuing 26 million new shares, or roughly 20pc of its existing share capital.

This is aimed at raising at least £120m after WH Smith slashed its expectations for full-year profit to between £75m and £90m, down from previous forecasts of between £100m and £115m. It is the company’s second profit downgrade this year.

WH Smith shares sank by more than 15pc in the wake of the announcement.

Bosses said the revised forecasts reflected an anticipated decline in passenger numbers as the outbreak of war in Iran had triggered scores of flight cancellations in the Middle East.

WH Smith has sought to focus entirely on stores within airports and railway stations by selling off its high-street shops to private-equity firm Modella Capital last year. These stores have since been rebranded as TG Jones.

WH Smith said its like-for-like revenue in the UK grew by 2pc in the past 14 weeks, with growth in its hospital sites offsetting the decline at airports.

North American sales were down 1pc, with the retailer’s resorts business dented by a fall in visitor numbers to Las Vegas.

The chain has begun selling or renegotiating loss-making sites to help shore up its finances, while it is replacing directly-run operations with franchises in smaller markets.

WH Smith warned it expected to take a £150m hit from closures of its InMotion electronics stores in the US.

The fresh fundraising caps off a tumultuous period for the retailer, which is still reeling from the fallout of an accounting scandal last year.

Almost £500m was wiped off WH Smith’s market value after it admitted to having overstated profits in its North American business by tens of millions of pounds because of an accounting error.

The saga led to the resignation of Carl Cowling, the chief executive, while the company said it would claw back up to £7m in “overpaid” bonuses to top bosses.

WH Smith is under investigation by the Financial Conduct Authority, which is assessing whether the business breached UK listing and transparency rules.

Britain’s audit watchdog also opened an investigation into PwC this week over its role in the blunder.

Leo Quinn, the executive chairman of WH Smith, said: “There is no doubt that current economic uncertainty and its effect on consumer appetite for spending has created headwinds. In this environment, sorting legacy issues while investing in the core model requires the financial flexibility of a stronger balance sheet in lock-step with self-help.

“This placing is a prudent and proactive step to accelerate our transformation of what is, at heart, a good business with some great people and clear opportunity for profitable growth.”

WH Smith’s largest shareholder, Causeway Capital Management, has said it will take part in the capital raise, while directors including the executive chairman and chief financial officer will contribute around £1.7m.

data.title: Forget Italy – this Cumbrian couple are changing the UK pasta market
data.description:
When 18-year-old Imogen Royall met her future husband Matt Kenyon in a grimy nightclub in 2010, she was locked in a deep struggle in her relationship with food – to the point that she could hardly bear to eat in front of him. So it’s remarkable that the couple now run an award-winning pasta company whose British-made casarecce and conchigliette are stocked in more than 600 delis, farm shops and supermarkets across the country.

Growing up, Royall’s family bounced from Oxford to London to Sheffield before settling in the Lake District, and she describes their household as “traumatic”. From as young as six she remembers her control-based approach to food creeping in. “I became fixated and obsessed with monitoring my food, or not eating,” says Royall.

As she grew up, the glorification of stick-thin bodies in programmes such as America’s Next Top Model didn’t help. Nor did “the covers of those awful magazines at the time, like Sugar”, Royall says. “Being a teenager in the 2000s, carbs were demons.”

It was her desire to appear “normal” when starting to date Matt, a 21-year-old builder from Windermere, that forced Royall to confront her fears.

“At some point, I just wanted to be with my boyfriend and not have a panic attack halfway through a meal,” she says now. “I wanted to be able to enjoy things and connect with somebody.” 

Royall recalls a specific dinner in their early courtship that changed everything. They were at the Wheelhouse Crab and Oyster Bar in Falmouth: “We were eating loads of seafood and had to destroy this crab. That was the scariest food for me. But we had bibs on, and we were making a mess and just enjoying it. It was a massive turning point – I found joy in food.”

Recounting her story from her sun-streamed office in their Cumbria home, Royall, 33, says her younger self could never have imagined she’d start a carbs-based company with her now-husband – let alone one stocked next to the Ottolenghi sauces in the temple of middle-class nourishment that is Waitrose.

Established in 2022, Northern Pasta Co started as a farmer’s market stall in Kendal, where the couple sold their 100 per cent British-grown spelt pasta. It soon became a staple at independent grocers, finally earning a listing at Waitrose in April. It is one of a crop of prospering homegrown pasta brands, among them Yorkshire Pasta and Devon’s Fresh Flour Company, trying to provide Britons with one of their best-loved foods, the lion’s share of which is imported from Italy.

The idea struck when Royall was working in a bakery during the Covid-19 pandemic. “I learnt that we grow ancient grains in the UK; they were in the sourdough we sold. But pasta is my favourite carb, and I’d always seen it on the shelves looking bright yellow and really unappealing,” she recalls. “So I thought, ‘why can’t we do something about that? Why can’t our British farmers show up in another area of the supermarket?’”

“It’s such a simple product, just flour and water,” says Kenyon, 35, calling in from the factory, a 20-minute drive from their front door. With Kenyon’s extended family living in Italy, the couple married in Tuscany in 2018, serving fresh pasta at their nuptials. But it wasn’t until they were back in their kitchen during Covid – and inspired after briefly living on the island of Hvar in Croatia, where they saw the residents eating seasonally and locally – that they decided to make pasta together.

Royall and Kenyon have always tried to eat as sustainably as possible, so prioritising regenerative farming came naturally. Northern Pasta Co’s spelt is grown by two Cotswolds-based millers who are “hugely passionate” about soil health, says Royall. “A customer has the power to impact the UK landscape by buying a bag of pasta. That’s so exciting,” she adds.

Spelt, an ancient wheat, has been grown in Britain since the Bronze Age, and many find it easier to digest than modern wheat. For a tummy-troubled nation obsessed with probiotics, the idea that eating pasta needn’t be naughty – or painful – is welcome. Plus, Royall and Kenyon argue that their pasta is beautifully nutty and flavoursome, while the shapes, including casarecce, gigli and radiatori, are extruded through bronze dies, a 17th-century Neapolitan method that provides more texture, so it can better grip on to sauce.

“We’ve become so detached from joy in our food with all these smart watches and devices,” says Royall, who is clearly no longer afraid of tucking into a bowl of carbs. “There’s a lot of ‘shoulds’ and ‘shouldn’ts’. We wanted to tap into the joy of food. The Italians do it so well.”

Speaking of our continental neighbours – has this thoroughly British company received any heat for producing an Italian staple? “I don’t think there’s a rivalry,” says Kenyon. “We’ve never tried to pit ourselves against Italian pasta. But my auntie takes it back to Italy when she visits, so that’s saying something”. High praise also came in the form of a Great Taste label, but the fortune-flipping moment came in 2023 when Royall and Kenyon were featured on James Martin’s Saturday Morning.

“It was pretty scary,” recalls Royall. “We had 100 bags of pasta, went on the telly, then had 6,000 orders over a weekend. I’d never seen anything like it.” Kenyon adds: “Our capacity was 100 bags per day. Filling those orders was insanity. The pasta dryer takes 16 hours, and when that time was up, I’d go back and make more – even if it was 2am.”

Kenyon now oversees a team of seven in their new Lancashire factory, where they churn out 1,500 bags each day. “An insanely hard worker,” Royall says, addressing her husband. “You started working as a builder at 16 and figured out how to build a house. Even if you don’t know how to do something, by the end of the day, you will have done it.”

Their respective strengths play out in the running of the business. “I’m an emotional person, so I do the marketing,” Royall says, referencing the recipes and videos she posts for their 20,000-strong Instagram audience, as well as the cookbook she released earlier this spring. Wisely, they don’t shy away from being the face of the brand. Both in their 30s, with what seems like an effortlessly healthy glow, their aspirational yet accessible image has helped grow a following that’s invested in their entrepreneurial journey.

But most couples wouldn’t dream of going into business together, and navigating overlapping domestic and professional spheres can’t have been simple. “When we started off, we did everything together. It wasn’t great. You’re just watching over each other with a magnifying glass, and [thinking] ‘they’re not quite doing it right’,” laughs Royall. “To be completely transparent, I think we made pasta [for the business] together twice,” adds Kenyon.

Remembering those first dinners together when Royall struggled to eat, Kenyon addresses her directly. “How far you’ve come with it is incredible.” With their kitchen creation now available on shelves across the country, their story is incredible indeed.

Northern Pasta Co Pasta for the People by Imogen Royall is out now (Murdoch Books, £20)

data.title: Britain’s adders threatened by extinction
data.description:
Adders are under increasing threat of extinction across Britain’s countryside as building projects destroy their habitat.

The UK’s population of the venomous snakes has been put at risk from “intense habitat loss”, according to a report from the West of England Nature Partnership.

The study warned that adders, which are native to Britain, could be restricted to just a few UK sites as soon as 2032, making them increasingly “vulnerable to extinction”.

Adders are present in places including the South Downs in Sussex and in the New Forest in Hampshire, as well as parts of East Anglia.

Chris Monk of the Amphibian and Reptile Group told The Telegraph that housing and road developments were partly to blame for falling adder numbers.

He said there had been an incident two years ago in Hampshire where “adders were living on a new development of housing” and found their habitat had been destroyed after emerging from hibernation.

Mr Monk added: “Road deaths can be quite significant. In some areas we’ve got them living in verges next to main roads with the main road on one side and then a hedge, and then you’re into intensive agriculture.”

Experts have warned that rural fires, often caused by portable barbecues, also pose a threat to the UK adder population.

It is not known how many adders there are in the UK – they are extremely difficult to track – but it is thought to be in the tens of thousands. However, individual populations of the snakes are increasingly becoming isolated, which means they interbreed.

Mr Monk said: “There’s a lot of very small populations, with maximum numbers seen basking [in the sun], in single figures.” Interbreeding can cause genetic weakness.

The warning about Britain’s adder population comes amid wider concerns over the future of the UK’s wildlife. The West of England Nature Partnership report said that 62pc of fish species in the region were declining, along with 44pc of bird species.

Campaigners have argued that Labour’s demands for more development across the UK are putting habitats and species at risk.

The Government is in the midst of a “build, baby, build” push to help boost the economy, with plans to build 1.5 million new homes in this parliament.

Ministers have claimed that nature protection should not stand in the way of this. Rachel Reeves has said that we cannot allow the lives of “bats and newts” to halt projects.

Last October, the Chancellor said the building of 20,000 homes in Sussex was being held up by “some snails that are a protected species or something”.

In February, 60 of the country’s leading conservationists, scientists and environmental charity leaders signed a letter to the Government, demanding it stop “attacks on nature protections”.

A government spokesman said: “This Government is investing £60m over the next three years – more than double the previous round of funding – into the Species Recovery Programme. Previously, the programme has funded specific projects to help protect adders, including adder tunnels in Berkshire.

“We are committed to a win-win for nature and for development.”

data.title: Taxpayers lose £100m on investment in broadband company
data.description:
Taxpayers have lost almost £100m after lenders to Gigaclear were forced to take a major writedown on their investment in the struggling broadband firm.

The taxpayer-backed National Wealth Fund (NWF) is among the lenders that seized control of Gigaclear in a major restructuring of the debt-laden company in April.

However, newly published accounts reveal that the backers were forced to write off £388m – or 40pc – of their loans as part of the deal. The NWF committed £240m in debt to Gigaclear during a wider fundraising round in 2023, suggesting it has suffered a £96m loss.

The public body is now Gigaclear’s largest shareholder, while other lenders, including NatWest and Lloyds, have also taken equity holdings in the company.

It will be viewed as a warning over the scale of potential losses facing the taxpayer after the Government pumped vast sums of money into start-up broadband challengers known as alt-nets.

The NWF, which reports to Rachel Reeves, has invested around £1.7bn in alt-nets in recent years, including at least £90m in CityFibre’s latest fundraising round. Other bets include Dorset-based Wessex Internet and Netomnia, which has agreed a £2bn takeover by the owners of Virgin Media O2.

Dozens of alt-nets sprang up across the UK to build full-fibre broadband networks, aiming to challenge the dominance of BT’s Openreach.

However, rising interest rates and sluggish uptake have put their debt-fuelled business models under strain, threatening to spark a wave of collapses and putting billions of pounds of investment at risk.

£1bn debt pile

Gigaclear’s accounts show the company’s net finance costs surged to £96.5m last year, an almost threefold increase from the previous year, as its debt pile rose to just under £1bn.

The Oxfordshire-based firm, which has 165,000 customers, said lenders had committed a further £80m of funding, helping to shore up its balance sheet until early next year.

Nevertheless, it acknowledged there was a “material uncertainty” over its ability to continue as a going concern.

Directors warned the company would drop below its minimum liquidity covenants if its average revenue per user declined by just 10pc, adding that some of its new shareholders were not accustomed to taking equity positions.

Like many of its rivals, Gigaclear, which is focused on rural areas, has scaled back its full-fibre rollout in response to the financial crunch.

The company cut its capital expenditure to £72.6m last year, from £139.2m the previous year, while staff numbers reduced from 779 to 654, largely because of a reduction in the number of engineers.

This cost-cutting and the absence of an impairment on its network helped Gigaclear slim down its losses to £166m from £218m, while an increase in customer numbers boosted revenues by 38pc to £65m.

A spokesman for the NWF said: “We expect the [broadband] sector to go through continued evolution, change and consolidation, and we will manage our exposure by balancing the delivery of government policy objectives with protecting and maximising value for money for the taxpayer.”

A Gigaclear spokesman said the restructuring had “strengthened Gigaclear’s capital structure, providing the company with a ... fully funded business plan”.

They added: “Gigaclear is now in its next phase of growth from a position of financial stability and is currently delivering on its plan for long-term, sustainable growth.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Burnham ‘relied on deeply flawed analysis’ to pay out skyscraper loans
data.description:
Andy Burnham’s Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA) used a “deeply flawed” analysis to justify £140m in loans to a skyscraper tycoon, a court has heard.

The Mayor’s authority has been accused of failing to carry out basic checks on property developer Daren Whitaker before approving loans worth roughly half of its public housing investment fund.

The legal challenge has been brought by Aubrey Weis, a rival developer, who argues the loans amounted to an unlawful subsidy for projects linked to Mr Whitaker.

The timing of the case is uncomfortable for Mr Burnham, who is the chairman of the Greater Manchester Combined Authority.

It comes nine days before the Makerfield by-election, which could offer Mr Burnham a route back to Westminster and potentially bolster his standing as a future Labour leadership contender.

Mr Burnham has repeatedly pointed to his record as Manchester Mayor as proof of his credibility as a potential prime minister.

On the campaign trail, he has highlighted his “effective use of public funds” to regenerate Manchester as evidence of his competency.

Mr Weis has long argued that concentrating such a large share of public lending with one developer distorted Manchester’s property market and froze out rivals.

The appeal follows a series of disputes about the GMCA’s housing investment fund, which has lent around £800m to companies linked to Mr Whitaker’s Renaker property empire.

‘It cannot be rational’

Tuesday’s hearing centred on £140m of those loans. Joseph Barrett KC, representing Mr Weis, told the court that GMCA officials approved the loans without properly scrutinising Mr Whitaker’s finances, while still pricing the lending as if it were secured by his personal backing.

He said: “It cannot be rational for a public authority to lend up to £140m of public money on the basis of someone’s creditworthiness where it has conducted no inquiry into or consideration of his liabilities or creditworthiness.”

The loans were approved through Greater Manchester’s housing investment fund and related to two development vehicles known as Trinity and Jackson.

Mr Weis argued that Renaker told planners the developments were too unprofitable to provide affordable housing, helping secure exemptions from affordable housing requirements.

However, when seeking taxpayer-backed loans from the GMCA, the company later submitted viability assessments indicating the same schemes would generate substantial profits.

Mr Barrett said the GMCA later scrambled to justify the loans with a post-decision paper written by an official who had played no part in approving them, which he said was based on a “deeply flawed and legally erroneous” reading of the rules.

Mr Barrett argued that the Competition Appeal Tribunal had been too willing to accept the GMCA’s explanations when it threw out Mr Weis’s challenge last year.

The Competition Appeal Tribunal rejected Mr Weis’s original challenge last year and found that the loans did not amount to an unlawful subsidy.

The GMCA has repeatedly denied wrongdoing, saying its lending process was commercially assessed, subject to due diligence and delivered thousands of homes while regenerating brownfield sites without losses to the taxpayer.

Mr Burnham has staunchly defended his administration’s decisions, previously telling The Telegraph that proceeds from the loans will help pay for other housing initiatives, including some social and affordable homes outside the city centre.

The court battle comes amid criticism that the fund has done little to address Manchester’s shortage of affordable homes.

Internal GMCA figures published this year showed that fewer than 5pc of the homes supported by the scheme were classed as affordable.

Renaker has transformed Manchester’s skyline by building a host of luxury skyscrapers, partly funded through taxpayer loans, across the city.

Some of its latest projects are luxury towers with amenities such as a dog spa, a golf simulator and a Mahjong room.

The taxpayer-backed development projects have helped Mr Whitaker become hugely wealthy, with a personal fortune estimated at £698m. Last year, he registered his residency as the tax haven of Monaco before switching it back to Britain.

The hearing continues.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Stop being snobby about buses
data.description:
The late Baroness Thatcher is often credited with declaring that any man who, at the age of 30, is still using a bus can consider himself a failure. It’s one of those “gotcha” quotes beloved of Labour politicians who revel in the snobbery and political clumsiness of the observation.

You might think that those qualities – snobbishness and clumsiness – would have alerted the more thoughtful MPs to the possibility that they did not, in fact, emanate from the mouth of the former prime minister. 

Margaret Thatcher was many things but she was more often the victim of snobbery from her own side than the perpetrator of it. As for clumsiness, the woman who dominated British politics for more than a decade and who led her party to three consecutive general election victories should be credited with astute, rather than clumsy, political instincts.

In fact the quote is more accurately attributed to Loelia, Duchess of Westminster, though the precise context of her comment is, sadly, lost in time. But whoever coined it, it says much about the attitude of the British establishment towards the Cinderella public transport service, a mode of transport that is used far more frequently than train or tram but which has been the lowest priority of every government’s transport strategy for the past 40 years.

In my first day as a minister at the Department for Transport in 2006, I was briefed about various (expensive) tram projects to which the Government was committed and I asked why buses were not considered a more appropriate and cheaper solution. My senior civil servant sighed mournfully and replied: “Because suits don’t use buses, minister.”

It’s true. The typical bus passenger is young or comes from a low-income household. Buses are seen as working class, downmarket, and far less attractive to professionals than even the oldest and most decrepit train carriage.

Meanwhile, rail passengers are generally better off and consist, among others, of MPs and journalists. This is why so much parliamentary time, not to mention news coverage, is given to the railways, with every increase in fares and electrification project analysed and debated extensively. But bus services and their passengers just get on with it.

Only recently – amid the prospect of Andy Burnham being carried shoulder-high by an enthusiastic crowd through the gates of Downing Street – has the subject of buses intruded on the thoughts of mandarins and ministers in Westminster. His recent suggestion that a £2 cap could be placed on fares nationally suggests he recognises the electoral salience of buses as a topic.

Since 1985, when the Thatcher government deregulated the bus industry across the country (apart from London), local services have been an entirely private matter. Local authorities’ role in bus services became restricted to funding routes that were socially necessary but not profitable enough to attract the interest of the private sector.

And across the country, passenger numbers fell disastrously as fares rose and services became more piecemeal – except in London, of course, where the continuation of regulation by Transport for London (TfL) ensured a healthy growth in patronage. Just a coincidence, I’m sure.

To be clear: thanks to the Thatcher government, anyone today can buy a bus and, provided it is roadworthy, drive it along any route, as frequently or as infrequently as they like and charge any level of fare they choose. This is privatisation in tooth and claw, and it is the one Thatcherite reform that has rarely received any attention, let alone been subject to any attempt to reverse it.

Some – mostly Labour – local authorities tried to introduce bus franchising to impose a level of democratic control over local services. But it was not until Burnham launched his Bee network of franchised buses in the Greater Manchester area that the wider body politic started to sit up and take notice.

Whatever criticisms have been made of the ambitious Manchester mayor, he has clearly and accurately identified an issue that could have – in fact has had – a major positive effect on the quality of lives, not of wealthier middle-class commuters, but of working-class citizens whose income has always restricted their choice of travel.

The last Labour government did very little to enable local authorities to improve local bus services, and even in the devolved areas of Scotland and Wales little progress has been made (though Wales is further ahead than Scotland). MPs tend to use and praise buses while they’re in London without really appreciating the different regulatory regime that governs their own local services in their constituencies.

If Burnham succeeds in replacing Keir Starmer as prime minister, we could see a genuine revolution in transport policy, a radical change without which the yearned-for and much-debated “local integration” of public transport – trains, buses, trams and private car – will always be out of reach.

The Duchess Loelia may have been an unforgivable snob but her frequently misattributed maxim may yet end up encouraging this government, under new leadership, to fix a fundamental weakness of British transport policy.

data.title: Auditors of collapsed shadow bank to be investigated by City watchdog
data.description:
The UK’s accountancy watchdog is to investigate the auditors of a shadow bank that collapsed amid allegations of fraud earlier this year.

The Financial Reporting Council (FRC) said on Thursday it had opened investigations into a network of four small accountancy firms that signed off accounts for Market Financial Solutions (MFS).

Magus Chartered Accountants, which had just two employees and was listed as MFS’s main auditor before its insolvency, is among those being investigated.

It comes after the collapse of MFS prompted a barrage of fraud claims against the company and its founder, Paresh Raja.

Mr Raja, in particular, has been accused of “plundering” MFS to fund a lavish lifestyle. He is accused of buying “a vast number of cars”, including three Aston Martins, two Mercedes, six Ferraris and three Rolls-Royces.

Berkeley Finch, which signed off MFS’s 2024 accounts and has 11 employees, is also under investigation. Some of Mr Raja’s companies were registered to the same address as Berkeley Finch.

The FRC did not provide any details of its investigation.

Meanwhile, a legal filing from creditors has claimed that some MFS clients presented as “genuine borrowers” but were “closely connected with” the company.

A number of these clients were also owned by two individuals connected to Magus.

Silver Levene (UK), which audited lending vehicles linked to MFS, is also under investigation by the FRC, alongside another unnamed individual accountant.

The latest investigation highlights the ongoing fallout from MFS’s collapse.

The business was branded a shadow bank owing to the fact it did not accept deposits and instead funded loans by borrowing from banks and other lenders.

In particular, the company described itself as a specialist provider of buy-to-let mortgage lending and bridging finance, borrowing from high-profile banks such as Barclays, Santander, Wells Fargo and Jefferies.

Creditors have claimed that at least £1.3bn was “misappropriated” from MFS, which was plunged into administration in February 2026.

Mr Raja and his wife allegedly received more than £408m from “funds managed by MFS” to “personal bank accounts” in the UK, Monaco, Singapore and the United Arab Emirates, according to creditors.

Mr Raja, who has been hit by a worldwide freezing order, strongly denies the allegations and has consistently maintained that there was no fraud or dishonesty.

A spokesman for Mr Raja said: “Assets which the administrators characterise as missing were held through nominee structures for the benefit of MFS and its creditors, a position set out in detail to the administrators in March 2026.”

A Silver Levene spokesman said: “We are aware that the FRC has opened an investigation in relation to the statutory audit of the consolidated financial statements of Zircon Group Limited for the financial year ended 31 December 2024. As the FRC has noted, the opening of an investigation does not indicate that any findings have been made. We will cooperate fully.”

Magus and Berkeley Finch were contacted for comment.

data.title: Britain’s rural broadband boom turns to bust
data.description:
When Boris Johnson unveiled Project Gigabit, a £5bn plan to deliver full-fibre broadband to the furthest reaches of the UK, he was in characteristically enthusiastic form.

“Project Gigabit is the rocket boost that we need to get lightning-fast broadband to all areas of the country,” the then-prime minister said in 2021.

The scheme has distributed subsidies to a range of scrappy broadband start-ups known as alternative networks (alt-nets), as well as to incumbent BT, to build full-fibre networks in areas that would otherwise be commercially unviable.

Five years on, full-fibre coverage has increased from just 17pc to 78pc of UK homes.

But while households have benefited, the legacy for industry has been more mixed. The flood of money into the sector created a boom period – but as the rollout reaches its final phase, that boom is turning to bust.

Debt-laden alt-nets are struggling to stay afloat, with many scaling back their rollout targets or handing back government contracts altogether.

The crunch threatens to delay the completion of the projects, with households in remote locations forced to wait longer for fast internet, and the risk of sector collapse has raised fears that even those with coverage could lose it if their provider goes under.

Ministers and regulators face a crucial decision: how to manage Britain’s overcrowded broadband market as Project Gigabit winds down?

While most of the UK’s full-fibre broadband rollout has been privately funded, taxpayer money has played a key role. A central aim of Project Gigabit was to ensure broadband still reached areas where it was commercially unviable to build networks, largely in remote rural areas where small populations and challenging geography make it difficult to do so. This was achieved through government contracts to build in these areas.

As the largest operator, BT’s network division, Openreach, won several of the larger contracts. But deals were also inked with a smattering of smaller alt-nets, among them CityFibre, rural specialist Gigaclear and Dorset-based Wessex Internet.

The contracts covered one million homes and businesses. As of March, around 227,000 of these had been connected.

Project Gigabit boom then bust

Yet rising interest rates have piled strain on the debt-fuelled business model of the alt-nets, forcing many to slam the brakes on their rollout plans. Some have been unable to honour their multi-million-pound government contracts.

Freedom Fibre and Full Fibre both handed back their contracts earlier this year. CityFibre, which has nine Gigabit contracts, scrapped a £60m agreement last month to build in Nottinghamshire and West Lincolnshire. It was part of a broader scaling back of the company’s rural rollout plan, euphemistically described by bosses as a “rescoping”.

Meanwhile, BT has swept in. The former state monopoly has been awarded eight of the abandoned contracts, collectively worth almost £800m.

Supporters of the scheme, which is overseen by government body Building Digital UK (BDUK), argue that it was always intended to be flexible and adapt to shifting demand.

“Projects very rarely go how they were planned on the first day,” says Paddy Paddison, co-founder of Wildanet and chief executive of alt-net trade body Inca.

“What I think BDUK were very clever with was to make sure that the risk always sat with the bidder and not with the Government. So from their side, I think they played it right.”

Nevertheless, the financial jeopardy facing some of the companies involved raises questions about whether the Government’s rural broadband drive has worked.

Gigaclear, which holds three contracts under the scheme, was recently taken over by its lenders as it struggled under a ballooning debt pile. One of its lenders is the taxpayer-backed National Wealth Fund, which took a near-£100m haircut on its investment.

Consolidation crunch

Should struggling alt-nets fail, it would raise questions about service continuity for those in rural areas without alternative providers.

And with Openreach emerging as the only viable player to scoop up contracts abandoned by alt-nets, analysts warn this could slow down the rollout.

“I think the impact on consumers is that it’s definitely going to take longer for them to get fibre than BDUK would have liked,” says Karen Egan at Enders Analysis. “Everything’s going to end up being much, much slower than initially hoped.”

This rural reckoning has reignited arguments about the lack of competition in the UK broadband market.

The owners of Virgin Media O2 (VMO2) have struck a £2bn deal to buy Netomnia, the second-largest alt-net, through their joint venture Nexfibre in a deal that could fire the starting gun on a cascade of consolidation. But it now faces the prospect of a lengthy review by competition regulators.

Bosses at VMO2’s parent companies – Telefónica and Liberty Global – have raised concerns with the watchdog that funding for the takeover could fall through if the review goes on for too long.

CityFibre, which mounted its own unsuccessful attempt to buy Netomnia, has been the deal’s most vocal opponent, warning it “risks re-establishing an effective duopoly of BT and VMO2”.

Yet questions linger over its own future. After narrowly avoiding collapse last year, the company needs to raise yet more money in the coming months to keep it afloat.

Court documents filed at the time of last year’s £1.5bn refinancing show CityFibre had exhausted its debt facilities by June 2024. The company believes its latest cash injection will last until early to mid-2027, meaning it will need to raise more money this year. Bosses estimate it will need £2.5bn to tide it over between 2025 and 2030.

A source close to CityFibre said the company now had enough money to last until 2028, in part because of cost-cutting, but acknowledged it would likely need more funding.

A spokesman said: “We are immensely proud of CityFibre’s involvement in Project Gigabit and we continue our rollout in rural and harder-to-reach areas as others choose to simply stand on the sidelines.”

Yet securing more financing could prove difficult in an increasingly squeezed market. Making matters worse, one of CityFibre’s largest shareholders is the United Arab Emirates (UAE) sovereign wealth fund Mubadala. As conflict rages in the Middle East and appetite for Gulf investment cools, further backing from the UAE could prove complicated.

The Telegraph has learned that Mubadala’s initial investment in CityFibre in 2021 was scrutinised by Britain’s security services amid national security concerns, though it was ultimately waved through.

Beyond Project Gigabit, there is renewed controversy over BT’s dominance after Openreach this week announced discounts on the amount it charges providers such as Vodafone and Sky to use its network.

Speaking at a conference in London this week, Rajiv Datta, Nexfibre’s chief executive, described the price cuts as the “monopolist’s handbook rule 101”.

“You go and squeeze your marketplace and get effectively a condition where people are not able to compete or survive under those economic conditions,” he said. “They are flexing their muscles in the middle of the arena while the rest of the market is in a fragile state.”

Katie Milligan, Openreach’s chief executive, dismissed the accusations, saying: “This is about being able to compete and have the choice, and we are hugely respectful of our obligations.” She added: “As the lowest-cost builder, we’re passing that on. That’s exactly what our new pricing is designed to do.”

Boris Johnson would no doubt claim he has achieved his “rocket boost” as the rollout of full-fibre continues at pace. But as crunch time looms for a slew of precarious broadband challengers, the plan’s final phase could yet be the most fraught.

An Openreach spokesman said: “Project Gigabit is extremely competitive and Openreach has only been awarded work that no-one else felt capable of delivering. At the same time we’ve built heavily in rural areas off our own backs, reducing the overall need for taxpayer subsidy.”

A spokesman for the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology said: “All contracts are awarded through a fair, competitive process. Where contracts are amended, we reassess the market and award work to the supplier best placed to deliver for taxpayers and get gigabit broadband to the communities promised it. We welcome proposals from all suppliers and continue to engage regularly with the market.”

data.title: Revealed: Barclays’ last-ditch MFS deals before £1.3bn ‘fraud’ collapse
data.description:
Barclays is facing questions in the City about what it knew about scandal-hit Market Financial Solutions (MFS) after reducing its exposure in the months running up to its collapse.

MFS paid off millions of pounds owed to Barclays by borrowing from other banks before it plunged into administration, which led to accusations of a £1.3bn fraud by the company.

However, The Telegraph can reveal that Barclays was already in the process of shutting MFS’s bank accounts while some of these deals were going on.

Its failure to warn other banks that lent to MFS has angered fellow creditors and raised questions about exactly what it knew about MFS. Its relationship with the company dated back at least 15 years.

One City creditor of MFS said they suspected that Barclays was aware of concerning activity around MFS but chose to protect itself rather than alert other lenders about the concerns.

Typically, banks are limited in what they can disclose about clients because of confidentiality agreements. However, the concerns of some creditors highlight anger across the City that the fraud was not spotted sooner.

The collapse of MFS has triggered a blame game in the City and prompted a renewed focus on possible hidden dangers lurking in the shadow banking system.

There were several deals leading up to its collapse which cut Barclays’s exposure.

Barclays provided banking services to MFS and most of its associated lending vehicles. After recognising financial irregularities with the company, it started blocking transactions in November.

However, MFS attempted to refinance £190m of Barclays ’ loans with another UK institution, just days before it collapsed, well after Barclays first noticed irregularities at MFS.

The deal to offload £190m of Barclays exposure to MFS was never completed, after other creditors of the shadow bank pushed it into administration in February.

MFS did successfully refinance £134m of Barclays’ exposure to Wells Fargo, in a deal that was completed in November after Barclays had already started freezing accounts at the shadow bank.

A source close to that transaction said that Barclays had been warned about concerns at MFS but chose to protect its position first.

MFS was a shadow bank, which meant it did not accept deposits and instead funded its loans by borrowing from banks and other lenders.

Banks including Barclays, Santander, Wells Fargo and Jefferies all provided funding to MFS.

Barclays said that its total exposure to MFS amounted to around £500m. However, it expects to recover more than 50pc of that, after writing off just £228m of loans tied to the company in its first quarter results.

MFS collapsed into administration in February, prompting allegations that at least £1.3bn has been “misappropriated” from the business.

Paresh Raja, the company’s founder, has been accused of “plundering” MFS to fund a lavish lifestyle.

Mr Raja and his wife allegedly received more than £408m from “funds managed by MFS” to “personal bank accounts” in the UK, Monaco, Singapore and the United Arab Emirates.

He is accused of buying “a vast number of cars”, including three Aston Martins, two Mercedes, six Ferraris and three Rolls-Royces with the funds in a lawsuit on behalf of some of the shadow bank’s creditors.

Mr Raja currently lives in Dubai, is subject to a worldwide travel ban and asset freeze. He strongly denies the allegations and has consistently maintained that there was no fraud or dishonesty.

Barclays was not the only creditor to have its exposure to MFS refinanced in the months leading up to its collapse.

One of MFS’s lending vehicles, which BNP Paribas had lent to, sold approximately a third of its loan book, roughly £41m, to the Japanese bank Sumitomo Mitsui Banking Corporation last August.

Jefferies also provided £100m in financing to MFS in November, just before Barclays began blocking transactions at the bank. Jefferies has stated that it expects its maximum exposure to be around $20m (£17m).

Barclays and representatives for Mr Raja declined to comment.

Wells Fargo, Jefferies, SMBC and BNP Paribas also declined to comment.

data.title: Labour spends £36k finding out that people without cars use trains
data.description:
Labour has been accused of “burning money” by spending £35,834 to find out that some people use trains because they do not own a car.

The Department for Transport (DfT) has come under fire for wasting money on “obvious” research findings, as London Underground users faced disruption caused by Tube strikes.

The report, by Verian, a research agency, and the University of the West of England, cost the DfT £35,834, according to figures obtained by The Telegraph.

It found that trains were considered poor value for money, and some people did not like using them because they did not enjoy travelling with other people.

Users ‘face higher fares and declining services’

The Tories accused the Government of wasting money on the report. Richard Holden, the shadow transport secretary, said: “Labour are addicted to burning taxpayers’ money on already obvious, navel-gazing nonsense while passengers face higher fares and declining services.

“It appears Labour would rather ‘study’ passengers and taxpayers while giving their own union paymasters’ inflation-busting pay rises with no strings attached. Labour promised passengers that state control would mean a better railway but they have got the opposite.”

The report said: “Frequent train use, defined as using trains at least once a week, has been shown to be most influenced by not having access to a car (about three times more likely than average) and living in Greater London (about five times more likely than average).”

It added: “A lack of access to alternative transport, eg a car, can encourage rail use. By contrast, people who have access to a car or other mode of transport may find that more convenient than rail for their journey.”

Theorising why drivers may be less likely to take trains, the report noted that people with jobs linked to car or van travel might use trains less, and observed that those with heavy luggage might travel by car or van instead.

The report also identified “barriers to planning and making a rail journey”.

It said: “Individual characteristics that affect physical capability include mobility impairments, neurodiversity and poor physical health ... For non-users, barriers included fear, dislike of travelling with others, or overstimulation on rail journeys.”

Anxiety and stress could be more likely in people with mental health issues, who therefore might prefer not to plan train travel, the review said. Others might not use the train because of “perceptions of rail as poor value for money”, the report found.

A DfT spokesman said: “If we want more people using our railways, we need to understand what’s actually stopping them.

“This research identifies 24 specific barriers and drivers of rail use – from accessibility issues to the complexity of buying tickets – and is already informing vital work to attract more passengers. 

“The cost reflects the standard market rate for this type of rigorous, independently conducted review.”

Members of the Rail, Maritime and Transport union who work on the London Underground walked out on Tuesday and are due to strike again on Thursday, after last-ditch talks failed to resolve a dispute with Transport for London over working hours.

However, the disruption caused by Tuesday’s strike was limited. On the Jubilee line, only two drivers walked out, and overall about 60 per cent of drivers went to work as trains ran on many lines.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Google ordered to let news websites block AI search
data.description:
Google has caved to regulatory pressure to let websites block the search giant from using their content in its AI overviews feature.

Publishers will be able to opt out of the AI-generated summaries that appear at the top of Google’s search results.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), which forced Google to make the changes, said it would make it easier for publishers, such as news organisations, to negotiate deals with the tech giant.

The CMA said it would mean a “fairer deal for publishers and consumers and improve Google’s search services in the UK”.

Google is increasingly adding AI overviews, which are designed to quickly answer questions, to the top of its search results.

However, website publishers have bristled at the changes, saying they deprive them of traffic since users are less likely to visit their own sites, and that they have no control over whether they appear in the results.

AI companies have been accused of freeloading on news publishers’ output, using their material without permission.

Under “conduct requirements” imposed by the CMA on Wednesday, websites will be able to activate an opt-out to stop Google from scraping their content to power search features such as AI overviews.

Google said it was testing the changes with a subset of UK websites before introducing them globally.

The company said last month that 2.5 billion people now use AI overviews, which publishers say have led to a plummet in traffic.

Before now, websites have been unable to opt out of powering the feature without removing themselves from Google search completely.

News publishers have accused AI companies, including Google, of scraping their websites without permission to train AI while saying that companies should pay for access.

Some publishers have struck deals with companies including OpenAI to allow their content to train chatbots.

Sarah Cardell, the CMA’s chief executive, said: “With features like AI overviews rapidly reshaping online search, it is crucial that content publishers, including news organisations, have appropriate bargaining power over how their content is used.”

Responding to the changes, Mrinalini Loew, a general manager at Google, said its AI features “are designed to help people find and visit great websites, and to help publishers and websites strengthen their audiences”.

However, she said the company had already made several changes such as making links to websites within AI overviews more prominent.

Theo Bamber, the chief executive of the News Media Association, whose members include The Telegraph, said: “The legally enforceable conduct requirements for Google Search published today are a significant step towards levelling the playing field and building a fair, transparent digital economy where premium content is properly respected and fairly compensated.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Government bond sale costs taxpayers £36bn
data.description:
The Bank of England’s controversial decision to sell off UK debt has cost taxpayers £36bn in just four years, according to new figures.

Deutsche Bank said Threadneedle Street’s decision to actively sell government bonds, rather than letting them simply mature, meant taxpayers faced much heavier short-term losses.

The move threatens to add to Britain’s debt pile, which is on course to hit £3tn by September. Economists said Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, would be spared from transferring billions of pounds to the Bank if it stopped actively selling gilts, amid a dramatic drop in bond prices.

Deutsche Bank’s figures will fuel a growing political backlash against the Bank regarding its so-called quantitative tightening policy, amid mounting concern about the state of Britain’s public finances.

Prominent economists have warned that the rapidly rising public debt means Labour could be forced to seek help from the International Monetary Fund to repair the nation’s finances.

The Bank is gradually reducing the £875bn stockpile of gilts, as UK government bonds are known, that it amassed to prop up the economy during the financial crisis and Covid lockdowns.

While central banks around the world are pursuing a similar policy, most simply let bonds mature and then do not reinvest the money.

The Bank is the only major central bank to actively sell bonds before they mature, immediately crystallising billions of pounds of losses for the taxpayer.

Deutsche said the central bank was selling gilts back to investors at a discount of up to 50pc. This is because higher inflation and growing political uncertainty have driven up yields, in turn driving down prices.

Yields on long-term UK debt have hit the highest levels since 1998, pushing losses on bonds with maturities above 20 years to about £22bn alone since the Bank sales began in 2022.

Losses from sales of short-term bonds of up to seven years have amounted to £5.6bn, while sales of gilts with a maturity of up to 20 years are roughly £8bn, according to Deutsche Bank.

Sanjay Raja, the chief UK economist at Deutsche Bank, highlighted that the overall cost to the taxpayer of the Bank’s bond buying programme had jumped by £13bn since March alone in the wake of the Iran war, which has pushed up interest rate expectations.

MPs across the political divide, including allies of Andy Burnham, the Labour leadership front-runner, are demanding that the Treasury and the Bank abandon the policy of proactively selling gilts back to investors.

Louise Haigh, who is managing the Manchester Mayor’s campaign in the Makerfield by-election, has pledged to stop Andrew Bailey, the Bank governor, from “pursuing policies that actively damage the Government’s balance sheet”.

A working paper published by the Bank this month estimated that asset sales had driven up UK borrowing costs by almost half a percentage point, piling more pressure on the Chancellor’s increasingly stretched budget.

Richard Tice, the deputy leader of Reform, described the policy as “a scandalous waste of taxpayers’ money” that was driving up the national debt.

Losses on bond sales have to be covered by the Treasury under a deal brokered during the 2008 financial crisis.

The Bank’s own estimates last month showed taxpayers were on the hook for £175bn of lifetime losses from the Bank’s money-printing programme after the end of an era of low interest rates.

Mr Raja said: “In the aftermath of the Iran shock, markets have shifted interest rate expectations meaningfully. Gilt yields are up materially.

“And markets expect the Bank of England to raise rates – as opposed to cut rates – in the wake of the forthcoming inflation wave.”

He added: “Long-dated bonds now sell at a near 50pc discount to what the Bank of England purchased them.”

Mr Tice, who has consistently highlighted the growing cost of quantitative tightening to taxpayers, said Reform would stop the policy of active sales “immediately”.

He said: “Think about what this Government could do with £36bn? That pays for your whole defence strategy. That money could have paid for the entire defence investment plan they are considering.”

Downing Street is expected to unveil a £15bn plan to fund extra defence spending this week.

Mr Tice added: “The people at the Bank of England and the Treasury should hang their hands in shame. The real question is: who’s going to resign?”

Mr Bailey said in a letter to Mr Tice last year that the Bank expected losses “over time be the same regardless of whether we sell the assets or hold them to maturity”.

The Treasury and the Bank of England declined to comment.

data.title: Raffles hotel builder faces collapse
data.description:
The building contractor behind the Raffles hotel and The Ned is on the brink of collapse, casting doubt on the future of several construction projects across London.

Ardmore Group’s businesses, including its construction and major projects arms, have filed a notice of intention to appoint administrators.

The move, revealed in court documents, has left nine active projects in the capital in limbo, ranging from luxury hospitality schemes in the West End to residential towers in east London.

Work was halted on those sites on Thursday morning, according to industry publication Construction Enquirer.

The notice of intention to appoint administration was reportedly filed after missed payments to staff and subcontractors.

The largest of Ardmore’s stalled projects is a £500m scheme with laboratories and housing in King’s Cross, known as Tribeca.

It had also been working on high-end hotels in Mayfair and Kensington, flats at Earl’s Court and Hackney Wick, and offices at Chancery Lane.

Founded in Catford in 1974 by Irish brothers Cormac and Patrick Byrne, Ardmore is known for its role in building some of London’s best-known hospitality schemes.

These include Raffles London, in which Ardmore transformed the historic Old War Office building on Whitehall, near Buckingham Palace, into a luxury five-star hotel.

The building, originally completed in 1906, once served as the headquarters for prominent figures including Winston Churchill during World War II.

It also delivered The Ned, the lavish hotel and members’ club created at Edwin “Ned” Lutyens’ former Midland Bank headquarters in the City.

As part of the project, it converted a sprawling banking hall into several restaurants while restoring its green verdite marble columns, original 1920s walnut banking counters and period chandeliers.

One of the building’s most distinctive features, the original bank vault, was turned into a bar and lounge where more than 3,000 restored safety deposit boxes line the walls.

Beyond prestige hospitality, Ardmore was also a substantial partner for major housebuilders, constructing high-rise residential blocks across the country for builders including Barratt Redrow, Berkeley and Crest Nicholson.

However, scrutiny of apartment blocks that were built before the Grenfell disaster uncovered fire safety deficiencies at multiple buildings that Ardmore had built decades earlier.

Last year, Ardmore’s construction arm was put into administration in an attempt to protect the wider business group from being hit by client claims.

Despite this, Crest Nicholson won a landmark High Court challenge against the group over remediation costs at its Admiralty Quarter development in Portsmouth, a large waterfront scheme of 19 buildings completed in 2009. It was awarded close to £15m.

This paved the way for other builders to pursue claims against Ardmore, including Taylor Wimpey, Bellway and Barratt Redrow.

Ardmore recorded a pre-tax loss of £42.6m during the year to September 2024, according to its latest Companies House filings.

Ardmore was contacted for comment.

data.title: Top earners targeted in Coutts banking scam
data.description:
Have you been affected by this scam? Get in touch with us at money@telegraph.co.uk*.

Coutts customers are being targeted by scammers to trick them into handing over their money, the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) has warned.

The NatWest-owned private bank’s details have been used to set up a “clone firm”, called Coutts Wealth Management, with fake email addresses, telephone numbers and postal addresses sent out to unsuspecting customers.

The FCA warned consumers of the scam on Friday and said that they would not be protected by compensation schemes or the Financial Ombudsman if they lost money.

The regulator stated: “Scammers may give out false details – they may mix these details with the genuine details of authorised firms.

“Almost all firms and individuals must be authorised by us to carry out or promote financial services in the UK.”

Coutts, which is used by the Royal family and remains the King’s bank today, caters exclusively to high earners.

In April, Coutts raised its minimum threshold for new clients from £1m to a minimum of £3m in savings, investments or borrowings.

The FCA said the fraudsters were impersonating the bank by using the pseudonym “Jonathan Smith”, but the regulator warned that contact details could change over time.

It urged potential victims to only deal with authorised firms and to check this status using the FCA Firms Checker.

“Clone firms” remain a persistent problem for the FCA. Victims are pointed towards the website of a genuine firm, with email addresses that are very similar to official addresses.

Seven other warnings on similar scams have been issued by the FCA in the last two weeks. Consumers have been urged to report these scams immediately. Victims can contact the FCA or call Report Fraud.

Alasdair Walker, of Optimum Path Financial Planning, warned that artificial intelligence (AI) makes it easier to reproduce websites and marketing materials, and can make these scams even more convincing.

He said: “Clone firms succeed because they impersonate the real thing down to the detail, then blend genuine information into their fake details, meaning it can be very hard to spot.

“The safest habit is to treat any unexpected approach as unverified until you have checked the firm yourself on the FCA Register, and contacted the firm directly using the details from there.”

Mr Walker noted that the use of AI on social media to create “deepfakes” is an example of how genuine these scams can appear. These can simulate a realistic picture or video of a trusted individual, such as Martin Lewis, the money saving expert, endorsing a fraudulent business.

A Coutts spokesman said: “Coutts takes the security of our clients extremely seriously and works continuously to identify and remove fraudulent websites impersonating our brand, in partnership with specialist third parties.

“These types of scams are a common and evolving threat, often opportunistic rather than targeted. We would remind clients that Coutts will never ask them to move money or share sensitive information in response to an unexpected request.

“Anyone who believes they may have encountered a suspicious communication should contact us immediately.”

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.
