data.title: Labour’s rail nationalisation dream hurtles toward disaster
data.description:
Britain’s railways are creaking at the seams. The rail regulator ruled last year that a 7am Avanti service from Manchester to London should run empty to guarantee the train’s availability in the capital and ease overcrowding.

It led to the bizarre prospect of a 600-seat “ghost train” departing the platform of Manchester Piccadilly with a full complement of staff – while hundreds of stranded passengers looked on in despair.

The debacle seemed to sum up Britain’s ailing railway system. Yet judging by the recent upbeat assessment of Labour ministers, the UK is on the cusp of a great rail renaissance as more services come under Government control.

Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, last month hailed the state-backed takeover of Govia Thameslink, Britain’s largest train operator, as a “defining moment” for the nation’s railways.

The takeover forms part of Sir Keir Starmer’s flagship transport policy to nationalise the railways, slowly taking back franchises from private companies one by one.

Great British Railways (GBR), the name of the new state operator, will take over the running of the fully nationalised network next year, with the first GBR-branded train launched last month by Alexander in Brighton.

Yet, as the “ghost train” fiasco demonstrates, beneath the surface Britain’s railway system is at a breaking point.

Overcrowding and the HS2 debacle have left the system facing deep-seated issues familiar to any train travellers who have been forced to stand for hours on their journey – or worse, sit down on the floor next to a train toilet.

Now a looming crisis on the West Coast line is threatening to derail the Government’s rail policy – and undermine the Left’s case for further nationalisation across the British economy.

With politicians of all stripes, including the possible future prime minister, Andy Burnham, hailing greater public ownership of key industries, the battle to fix the railways is set to become a litmus test for state control.

That risks quashing a wider nationalisation drive that, under a new Labour leader, might extend to water firms, energy supply networks, Royal Mail and broadband providers. Nothing less than the future of the British economy could be at stake.

Capacity crunch

Just as Labour’s long-dreamed nationalisation plan becomes a reality, delays and timetable changes are leading to an ever-worsening capacity crunch between several of Britain’s biggest cities.

Plans to bring the West Coast Main Line between London and Glasgow – which is Europe’s busiest intercity route – into public ownership are already in disarray.

Avanti, the current operator, is set to be nationalised next year, meaning GBR trains will soon be ferrying passengers on the ultra-busy and crowded route.

Ministers recently forced Avanti to make mass cancellations on the West Coast route at the height of summer, ostensibly as part of a clampdown on costs.

However, industry experts say the line – which is used by a staggering 13 different rail operators – is already under extreme strain as 125mph passenger services jostle for space with commuter and container trains.

A sign of the overcrowding chaos is the familiar scenes of rush hour at Euston station, from where many West Coast services depart.

Known as the “Euston stampede”, last-minute announcements for platforms have often meant a mad dash for commuters travelling on the West Coast line.

These stampedes underline the anxieties of overcrowding. Many passengers rush because of fears they will be left standing in the corridor – or crammed in like sardines in the carriage itself.

Euston has attempted to relieve the pressure by announcing platforms earlier, but passengers’ fears of overcrowding remain.

HS2 was supposed to relieve this pressure on the West Coast route, but the opening date has recently slipped again after an exhaustive assessment of the state of ongoing works by new chief Mark Wild.

Moreover, ministers have given only vague commitments on linking the line – now expected to cost around £100bn – into the wider rail network.

For campaigners, the recent mass cancellations on the West Coast are a sign that the stark reality of fixing Britain’s railways is starting to dawn on optimistic Labour ministers.

Delays on the line are already the worst in Britain as the firm’s expresses jostle for space with local passenger services and slow-moving freight trains dragging rakes of containers from Felixstowe, London Gateway and Southampton ports to the Midlands and beyond.

The capacity reductions on the route, which also serves Manchester, Liverpool and North Wales, will eliminate 38 daily trains – equivalent to one in seven Avanti services.

Silviya Barrett, the Campaign for Better Transport’s policy director, said the West Coast cuts were a sign that the Government is starting to lower its sights for the railways.

“The Department for Transport [DFT] insists growth would be a natural outcome, but we feel that unless there is something explicit, GBR might resort to managed decline,” she said.

Barrett added: “We are already seeing them try to manage passenger outcomes with taxpayer outcomes and one sign of that is the call for cuts to services. It’s an attempt to balance the books. The Treasury thinks that the railway takes up a lot of public investment.

“That is concerning because you have to invest to attract passengers. One understands the need to ensure financial efficacy, but if you want to increase passenger use, you need to provide frequent, uncrowded services.”

‘Colossally expensive’ paths ahead

At the heart of the capacity crunch on the West Coast line is the failure of HS2, which was originally scheduled to take its first trains this December.

The opening of the line may now be delayed until October 2039 – instead of the most recent target of 2033 – with costs reaching a potential £103bn.

Even then, trains will leave London from Old Oak Common and may not run from Euston station until 2043.

Stephen Joseph, a transport consultant and visiting professor at the University of Hertfordshire, said the only option for coping with the coming logjam from the HS2 delays will be to widen the M6 motorway or spend more money on the West Coast line.

Either option would be “colossally expensive”, he said, “but the situation can’t be ignored.”

Despite an eagerness to draw a line under the HS2 debacle, Labour also needs to confront how best to link the new route back to the West Coast line north of Birmingham.


Since HS2 was designed as a standalone railway, its double-length trains will not fit into the existing platforms at Manchester or other stations beyond Handsacre Junction, where HS2 will now end.

Chris Gibb, a former chief operating officer at Virgin West Coast, which Avanti displaced, was dismissed as a government adviser in April after telling the all-party parliamentary rail group that two separate fleets might be required to make HS2 even work.

“He was sacked but nobody has said why his view was not right,” Joseph said. “That does not mean you need to spend another £100bn between Birmingham and Manchester, but you do need something because otherwise there will be jams on the roads and railway.

“Given the cost of HS2, it’s going to be difficult for any government to say that they’re going back to it, but they are going to have to do something.

“Nigel Farage could come in and say let’s have none of this rail nonsense, we’re going to double deck the M6. But the cost of that would be eye-wateringly expensive.”

Burnham’s northern drive

For Sir Keir, who has made rail nationalisation a key plank of his transport policy, the HS2 debacle and its impact on the West Coast route raise thorny questions.

The UK rail system has long been a political lightning rod for Labour.

A picture of Jeremy Corbyn, the former Labour leader, sitting on the floor of a Virgin train service in 2016 because it was “ram-packed” stoked debate about whether train companies should return to public hands.

In 2024, the party under Sir Keir officially put forward its policy of placing “train operators under public ownership and control”. However, HS2 and the West Coast mainline pose a challenge to this policy.

Burnham, in his role as Greater Manchester mayor, has been an outspoken advocate of resurrecting the northern leg of HS2 abandoned by the previous Conservative government.

Victory next week in the Makerfield by-election for Burnham, followed by a successful challenge to Sir Keir, would put such a solution firmly on the agenda.

However, resurrecting the northern reaches of HS2 would put a Burnham premiership on a collision course with Treasury officials desperate to see the back of the project.

Richard Bowker, the former chairman of the Strategic Rail Authority, said ministers face a choice between dusting down elements of HS2 or letting the railway waste away to solve the capacity crunch.

He said suppressing rail freight to allow more passenger services to run while pricing some people out of travelling by increasing fares could buy some breathing space for the West Coast route.

Genuine growth, however, will require investment in longer trains or more services per hour, with solutions adopted in mainland Europe, such as double-decker expresses, not an option because of Britain’s rail infrastructure.

He said the only option is to follow Burnham’s plan – and to resurrect HS2’s cancelled northern leg.

“Call it something else, anything other than HS2,” he said. “And do what the French do, design it fully to within an inch of its life and get all the consents sorted before you start digging. It is complex and it will cost money, but it’s the right ambition to have.”

How else could the bubbling West Coast crisis be solved? The most obvious alternative to pouring billions more into making the best of HS2 would be to raise ticket prices to help manage demand.

However, that would make for an extremely short honeymoon period for the nationalised railway, with surveys indicating that support for state ownership running at around 66pc would drop to just 6pc were fares to increase.

“The public generally supports nationalisation, but that is because they expect better outcomes, including cheaper fares,” Barrett said. “Will we even have the same number of people travelling if they are pushed out by higher fares?

“The danger is that if you allow the railway to decline then more people will resort to driving and there is a pressure to increase road capacity, which does not come cheap either.”

Huw Merriman, a former rail minister who now heads the High Speed Rail Group and has worked with Mr Burnham on plans for a faster Liverpool-Manchester railway, praised Labour for not walking away from what remains of HS2.

The former chairman of the parliamentary transport committee said that with land acquisitions along the abandoned HS2 route north of Birmingham preserved by the Government, the opportunity remains to make the best of the situation.

He said HS2 could form the “spine” of a faster and more efficient railway if ministers were prepared to back a clear plan connecting it with the existing network further north.

Merriman urged Labour to take account of changing travel habits, including an increase in long-distance travel even as the increase in home-working since Covid holds back passenger numbers in shorter commuter routes.

“My children don’t drive and don’t wish to drive because they take the train everywhere,” he said.

“There’s an environmental aspect to it, but there’s also the flexibility. Those changing habits are another reason why we need to plan for the next generation of capacity, which is just around the corner for the West Coast Main Line.

“It’s also really important that we start to give the public faith that HS2 is going to be delivered to the times and budget that have been set and explain some of the benefits.”

The former rail minister added: “Look at the Crossrail, which also had its critics. Since it opened this year as the Elizabeth Line, people have been asking where has this been all of our lives?

“It’s right that we focus on projects that aren’t delivering because it’s taxpayers’ money. But equally we perhaps need to be more confident that we can deliver good infrastructure projects and are doing so.”

‘We need to get it done’

Dick Fearn, an industry veteran who joined British Rail in 1973 and oversaw sections of the West Coast Main Line at Network Rail, said the Government should hold its nerve and do what is necessary to make HS2 work.

He said: “I still think that nettle can be grasped. I’m not a supporter of looking for small infrastructure solutions, which I think will divert resources which we could be using on HS2.

“The argument was never about the highest possible speed. It was always about adding capacity. We just need to get it done. Stitching HS2 into the West Coast Main Line is crucial. Simply going into Birmingham is not enough.”

Fearn added: “The DfT has to stand back and not try to manage the railway day to day. If GBR is to work and maximise capacity, it has to be as empowered.”

The DfT said it plans to proceed with a new line between Birmingham and Manchester to boost capacity and improve connectivity, but has yet to determine exactly what will be delivered and to what specification.

It said the Northern Powerhouse Rail route, linking the cities of northern England, will also be delivered as a long-term programme in the coming decades.

A spokesman said: “This will mean real change for passengers, delivering turn up and go services and faster journeys, creating opportunities and jobs.”

For Britain’s legions of long-suffering commuters and frustrated long-distance passengers, that cannot come soon enough.

data.title: Elon Musk becomes world’s first trillionaire
data.description:
Elon Musk has become the first trillionaire in history after his SpaceX business floated on the stock market in New York.

The world’s richest man’s net worth soared above the $1tn mark (£860bn) as shares in his rocket business surged by 20pc in initial trading after the company went public on the Nasdaq stock exchange.

Mr Musk has a stake of around 40pc in the rocket venture, which was valued at $2.1tn (£1.8tn) as markets opened, on top of his shares in Tesla and his other start-ups.

SpaceX’s shares jumped to $162 in early trading, flying past its initial listing price of $135.

It means Mr Musk’s stakes in SpaceX and Tesla have crystallised into a $1tn paper fortune, although he is prevented from selling shares in SpaceX for at least one year under the terms of the float.

SpaceX raised more than $75bn from investors on Friday as part of its record share offering, which was many times oversubscribed in a clamour to own a slice of the space venture.

The sale is equivalent to about 4pc of the company, with the other 96pc of the company’s shares remaining in the hands of Mr Musk, employees and original investors.

The SpaceX chief, 54, retook the top spot as the world’s richest person in 2024 from Bernard Arnault, the luxury goods tycoon.

His net worth surged above $500bn in October last year, the first person ever to be worth more than $500bn, and has more than doubled since.

Mr Musk was already a wealthy man when he founded SpaceX in 2002. He made $165m when PayPal was sold earlier that year.

He used his wealth to pump funding into SpaceX with the goal of using the rocket venture to reach Mars by cutting the cost of space launches.

However, the space business nearly bankrupted Mr Musk in 2008 after a series of failed launches, before it undertook its first successful mission from the US Marshall Islands and avoided collapse.

The company became the first private company to undertake an orbital space launch. It later developed its reusable Falcon 9 design, which has become a workhorse for space missions for Nasa.

Its latest rocket, Starship, is intended to ferry humans to Mars.

Mr Musk’s wealth could balloon further after the deal.

Earlier this year, SpaceX agreed a bonus deal with Mr Musk that will hand him hundreds of billions of dollars in shares if he can build a colony on Mars for one million people.

He also stands to secure a similar $1tn pay deal at Tesla if he can lift its valuation to more than $8.5tn and sell one million humanoid robots.

At $1tn, Mr Musk’s net worth now stands above Larry Page and Sergey Brin, the founders of Google – who are worth $292bn and $270bn respectively – Jeff Bezos, the founder of Amazon, and Larry Ellison, the Oracle chief.

Mr Musk has largely spent his earnings on business ventures or personal technological quests.

He has claimed he spends little on luxuries, at times sleeping in his company offices, factories or at friends’ houses. He has said his one “exception” is a fleet of Gulfstream jets on which he conducts much of his personal business.

However, he did spend roughly $33bn of his own cash and equity buying Twitter – now X – to reshape the social network to focus on free speech.

Mr Musk has said his main residence is a $50,000 building at SpaceX’s Starbase launch site. He previously sold a property empire of luxury houses for around $130m.

His girlfriend, Shivon Zilis, lives with four of Mr Musk’s children, reportedly in a gated community near Lake Austin in Texas. Mr Musk has a total of 14 children with four different women.

The trillionaire claimed he was “selling almost all physical possessions” in 2020 and intended to “own no house”.

data.title: Supermarkets take £800m hit from fat jabs
data.description:
Supermarkets suffered a sales hit of almost £800m last year because of the growing popularity of fat jabs, according to new research.

People on weight-loss drugs cut their grocery spending by an average of £418 per household in the year after starting treatment, according to Worldpanel.

The research found that 6.3pc of households in Britain now include at least one current user of the weight-loss treatments, up from just 2.3pc in 2024.

Based on those figures, Worldpanel estimates that supermarket receipts have fallen by £780m over the past year.

Spending on unhealthy snacks, such as crisps and chocolate, has been worst hit, falling by 18pc more in households with a fat-jab user than in the general population.

But while smaller appetites are denting demand for food, supermarkets have benefited from some side effects linked to the drugs.

So-called Ozempic mouth – a dry tongue and bad breath – is driving a rise in spending on mouthwash and chewing gum, according to the research.

Drugs such as Ozempic and Wegovy were originally developed as treatments for diabetes, but the number of adults in the UK using them has tripled in the past two years to 1.9 million as their weight-loss benefits have become apparent.

Women account for more than three-quarters of those using the so-called glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) drugs.

Chantel Kennaugh at Worldpanel by Numerator said: “What was once a specialised treatment, primarily prescribed for type 2 diabetes, has in just a few short years become a mainstream force.”

Worldpanel’s survey of more than 11,500 households found that more than a quarter claimed they may use the medication to lose weight in the future even if they do not have a major health issue.

The rapid uptake of fat jabs poses a major challenge to supermarkets, drinks makers and restaurants, as the drugs work by suppressing appetite and increasing feelings of fullness.

Ms Kennaugh said: “These drugs are fundamentally disrupting how people engage with food and drink, with ripple effects already being felt across grocery and lifestyle, forcing brands and businesses to adapt at pace.”

And some retailers are trying to capitalise on Britain’s shifting eating habits by launching specialist meal ranges.

Morrisons has rolled out a new range of “weight-loss jab-friendly” high-protein meals, sandwiches, salads, breads and cheeses, while Marks & Spencer has also released a range of nutrient-dense meals and snacks designed for GLP-1 users.

Despite the growing popularity of the drugs, high prices remain a major barrier to adoption. Four in 10 people who were taking the medication stopped this year because it was too expensive.

data.title: Retail gave a generation their first job – now we’re being priced out of hiring them
data.description:
More than a million young people in this country are not in education, employment or training.

For many of them, the longer that goes on, the harder it becomes to turn around – reduced earnings, damaged confidence and a growing dependence on state support that nobody wants and nobody planned for.

This is not a problem on the horizon. It is happening now.

Retail has always been part of the answer. Almost a quarter of all youth employment in the UK runs through our industry – flexible hours, local jobs, no minimum qualifications required.

The kind of roles where any young person can walk in and begin building something.

One in five people started their career in retail. Many of today’s business leaders began on the shop floor before working their way up to the boardroom.

It is something we are deeply proud of – but that ladder into employment and progression is beginning to crack.

That is why more than 80 retail chief executives have written to the Prime Minister with a clear message. At exactly the moment the Government is trying to reduce youth inactivity, its policies are making it harder and more expensive to hire the very people that businesses are being asked to bring into work.

Our concern is not with the Government’s ambition. Retailers support efforts to raise employment standards and help more young people into work. However, policy only succeeds if it works in practice.

In just 14 months, the industry has faced an additional £6.5bn in costs through higher National Insurance contributions and increases to the National Living Wage. These are not abstract figures. They affect decisions about recruitment, hours and investment.

Retail operates on some of the tightest margins in the economy – often just a few pence of profit from every pound spent. When costs rise this fast, businesses have to make difficult choices.

Too often, the first casualties are the roles that matter most to young people trying to get started – part-time jobs, flexible shifts, entry-level openings, the hours that get quietly reduced rather than advertised.

Across the industry, retailers are already reporting reduced headcount, frozen recruitment and fewer hours on offer. The people most likely to miss out are those who would otherwise have secured their first job.

On top of that comes growing concern over implementation of the Employment Rights Act. For an industry built around flexible and entry-level employment, implementation matters enormously.

Millions of workers actively choose flexible roles because they fit around study, childcare, caring responsibilities and other commitments. Recent British Retail Consortium polling found 67pc of part-time workers took their job because they needed that flexibility.

These jobs are not a loophole in the labour market. For many young people, these roles provide a first foothold in employment and a pathway to something more. If reforms make those jobs harder to offer or sustain, the people most affected will not be those already established in their careers. It will be those trying to get started.

Alan Milburn’s review warned that the number of under-25s not in education, employment or training could reach 1.25 million within five years if action is not taken. That warning should set alarm bells ringing. The economic and social cost of youth inactivity far outweighs the cost of helping businesses create opportunities in the first place.

That is why in our letter, retailers are calling for practical action: a joint government-retail taskforce focused on getting more young people into work, measures to reduce the cost of hiring and employment and skills reforms that support entry-level recruitment rather than unintentionally restricting it.

Retail employs nearly three million people in every part of the country and has long been one of Britain’s most effective routes into work. We stand ready to play our part in tackling youth inactivity.

The question is whether the Government will create the conditions that allow us to do so.

Helen Dickinson OBE is the chief executive of the British Retail Consortium
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain risks ‘terminal decline’ unless Labour backs business, warns Wrightbus boss
data.description:
Britain faces “terminal decline” unless Labour adopts a more pro-business approach to entrepreneurs, the owner of Wrightbus has warned.

Jo Bamford, whose bus manufacturer employs 2,500 staff in Northern Ireland, said an ignorance of corporate Britain in the Government and among London’s civil servants was holding back growth.

Bamford, a scion of the JCB digger empire and son of its founder Lord Anthony Bamford, is one of Britain’s leading industrialists, having acquired Wrightbus in 2019.

He said Labour needed to do more to back UK start-ups or risk hollowing out swathes of the country and consigning their residents to the scrap heap.

“If you don’t start getting people working hard and don’t start having businesses that they can come into, you just go into terminal decline,” he warned

“We’ve got to have jobs for communities which will create taxpayers who will fund the schools and the police force. They are left behind, the tertiary towns, communities outside of London. There’s a disconnect at the moment.”

JCB, based outside Uttoxeter in rural Staffordshire where it employs 7,000 people, and his own Wrightbus venture, were examples of how businesses in the provinces could anchor entire communities, he said.

“If that goes, the town is gutted and gone and the schools are gutted and gone. You bring the worklessness in and the parents are in despair, the children are in despair,” he added.

Wrightbus was acquired out of administration by Bamford seven years ago.

Before his ownership, the company was best known for pioneering the infamous “bendy bus” and London’s so-called “Boris bus”, which tried to revive the “hop on hop off” design from the original 1960s Routemaster.

Since then, Bamford has pushed the boundaries of bus design even further with a new fleet of hydrogen-powered vehicles and has also founded hydrogen producers Ryze and Hygen Energy.

But Bamford said that despite owning Wrightbus, one of only two UK busmakers and among a handful worldwide to produce a range of traditional and net zero models, he has struggled to get a hearing in Whitehall.

“I’ve never met the minister of transport,” he admitted. “I met the head of buses once. This is the guy coming up with the bus policy for the UK. I’m one of the only people in the world with a hybrid, diesel and battery bus, and he won’t see me.

“I go and talk to them and they go, no, you’re wrong. But I’ve been doing this my whole life, and I’m not sure what you’ve done before.”

Britain’s political class, from the 650 MPs to the Civil Service and government-appointed quangos, also have a lack of business know-how, which was holding the UK back, he added.

“You’ve got these 650 people standing up and saying, yeah, I’ve managed loads of people before and I know how to do it,” he said. “But none of them do.

“It doesn’t matter what political stripe they are – who has got any experience? I think there are 72,000 people in the Ministry of Defence. Has our minister of defence or our shadow minister of defence ever managed more than 10 people in an office?

“Look at the committee on climate change, which the Government is mandated to listen to. I think barely one of them has ever been an engineer. Most of them are commentators or civil servants who have done PPE. And they are designing our plan on energy transition.”

With the illustrious Bamford name behind him, there is always speculation about his relationship with his father Lord Bamford and the wider JCB empire.

Last month, Lord Bamford named George Bamford – Bamford’s brother and previously a luxury watch designer – as successor to the JCB business.

Bamford said he’s not bitter about the move because his own ambitions lie in building a global hydrogen player to match the success of the construction equipment giant, of which Wrightbus could be a part.

“George is my brother, he has my full support and so does my dad,” he said. “I left JCB 10 years ago and did my own thing. I have my own plan and that’s what I want to do.

“I haven’t done a brilliant job yet but I haven’t done a bad job. It was just me and a desk in Oxford, and it’s now two and a half thousand people.

“JCB is a fabulous business, but I’m 48 years old, I’ve got young children. It would have been a bit boring, wouldn’t it? Imagine, having to sit there for the next 10 years.”

Not content with being major industrialists, the Bamfords are also major political donors.

They have handed more than £10m to the Tories – and also funded Reform UK.

But Bamford says it would be wrong to view him as a dyed-in-the-wool Conservative and that what he most wanted was to see “competency” in government.

“You can have your own personal views, but business should be apolitical,” he said. “I gave a little bit of money years ago to Nadhim Zahawi who was my local MP.

“But my job is to play what’s in front of me and to get on with all of them, Labour, the Tories and all the other bits and bobs.”

He added: “I’m not going to pin my colours to anyone. I want the roads to be fixed, I want the NHS to work. Then you can decide whether you want to have a slight left or slightly right bent on it.”

Bamford said Britain’s 4.7 million family-owned businesses – employing between a handful of people and many thousands – get particularly short shrift.

However, his recent criticism of Labour’s inheritance tax, which will hit family businesses like JCB, had been misinterpreted, he admitted, and said he had no desire to quit the UK.

“I want to live here, I want to invest here and I want my children to be here,” he said. “I don’t have a problem paying tax. But I get p- - - - - off paying tax and not getting good services.

“I feel it’s a bit of a con job to be honest. You get to a tipping point – and I do know lots of people who are looking at their exit plans.”

Bamford was speaking at the launch of the X75, a machine designed to refuel hydrogen-powered diggers and tractors on building sites and farms.

He predicts that hydrogen will become mainstream as Britain weans itself off natural gas. With the national grid already limited by capacity, any expansion in the power supply will be quickly swallowed up by energy-hungry data centres, making mass-electrification impossible.

Rather than electrifying buses, trucks, diggers and tractors, he said, such vehicles could be hydrogen-powered, reducing electricity consumption and taking the pressure off the grid.

He said that a single order for hydrogen-powered buses could kickstart the entire sector if mayoral authorities were prepared to order the 17,000 vehicles required over the next decade en masse, rather than in dribs and drabs.

That could establish Britain as a leading player in hydrogen, from production through storage, supply and hydrogen-powered vehicles and equipment, before China comes to dominate just as it has in electric cars and batteries.

He said: “You’ve got 10 Labour mayors who should be seizing on this plan. You’d have 10 factories employing hardworking people, each in a tertiary town.

“We chuck loads of subsidy at lots of people who just do one little element of it. The reason I’m in every element is I’m trying to drive down the cost base so that’s the same as diesel.”

However, he said senior civil servants and key figures in Whitehall today would be unable to countenance the adoption of such an interventionist industrial strategy.

“They’d look at me like I’m a monster with two heads.”

data.title: The survival secrets of Britain’s 300-year-old family businesses
data.description:
I am peering into a wooden mash tun, one of the huge vessels used by brewers to turn malt and water into a liquid that will – after fermentation and the addition of hops – be transformed into beer, in this case Shepherd Neame’s Classic Collection IPA. My glasses fog up as the warm, malty steam hits my face. It is a satisfyingly old-fashioned experience.

“If someone from the Victorian era, or even Georgian era, came in here, they’d recognise this,” says Rupert Hodgkins, 45, Shepherd Neame’s brewing manager, who is showing me round the site in Faversham, Kent.

What makes Shepherd Neame unusual, however, is that it is brewing on the same site that it has been making beer continuously since at least 1698. “We can prove that, because we started paying alcohol tax in that year,” says Hodgkins, who leads me from an office with original Tudor beams and oak panelling, across a cobbled courtyard that once echoed to the noise of horses’ hooves, into the brewhouse.

Old companies are rare. The biggest, most valuable companies in the world are all technology companies founded within my lifetime; some, such as Meta and Tesla, within my children’s lifetime. Only a handful of banks, insurers and retailers celebrate a centenary in business. Shepherd Neame has celebrated its tercentenary. But what makes it even rarer is that it is still family-owned.

Indeed, it is a member of a peculiarly British and very exclusive club: the Tercentenarians. To become a member, you not only have to have made it to 300 years, you still need to be family-owned. A mere 15 companies in Britain meet these criteria. Some are quite well known, such as Fortnum & Mason, wine merchant Berry Bros & Rudd and Lock & Co Hatters. Some less so, such as CPJ Field, an undertaker, and Webster & Horsfall, an industrial wire maker in Birmingham.

All, by definition, have unparalleled staying power, having survived at least 17 recessions, a clutch of banking crises, the start of the Industrial Revolution and the end of horsepower, the defeat of Napoleon, stock market crashes, the Great Depression, two world wars and the rise of AI.

The club is very low-key. The owners and their families meet just once a year, with one of the companies hosting a lunch at its premises for the others. It’s a jolly affair, often with a good meal, especially if it is hosted by Berry Bros in its cellars. “It’s a very nice group of people. There’s no agenda, but there’s a useful exchange of views – on whatever the topic of the day is, be it inheritance tax or whatever,” says Jonathan Neame, 62, chief executive of the brewery. Alexander Hoare, the senior partner of C Hoare & Co, the exclusive bank, tells me with a chuckle that sometimes business is done, too, over a glass of wine: “I bought a property at one of those luncheons. A company in Falmouth wanted to sell their office and I wanted to buy it.”

There’s no website, and some of the members would prefer its existence to remain under the radar. I asked whether I could come to this year’s lunch, having attended one 15 years ago. I was told firmly by this year’s host, via email, that “having a reporter” as a guest was “something that our members are not keen on”.

Others, however, are understandably proud of their heritage and their ability to thrive in the 21st century, and believe that the Tercentenarians are not just a quaint collection of old brands but that they have something useful to say about the British economy and what may lie ahead.

To find out what that is, I visited and spoke to some of the chattier Tercentenarians. The oldest of all of them is RJ Balson & Son, a butcher’s shop in Bridport, Dorset. It started business in 1515, when Catholicism was the religion of England. Apart from a sign on the outside wall proclaiming it is “England’s oldest family butchers”, you would have no idea that the man behind the counter, Richard, is the 25th generation of Balsons to be making faggots, pies and 18 different types of sausage in this market town. “If you look at the statistics, only about 3 per cent of family businesses make it to four generations or more,” he says. “When you think we’re the 25th generation, it’s pretty extraordinary.” The secret? “We’ve kept it small and we can control what we’ve got.”

It is just one shop. Richard, 68, briefly dipped his toe into online retailing during the Covid lockdowns, but soon realised that he was not in control of the deliveries. “We stopped sending stuff out, because the couriers were just not reliable.” He much prefers meeting customers face to face. “When people come into the shop, we like to ask how their dad is in the nursing home, or if their daughter’s passed her driving test.”

Banking is very different from butchering, but Alexander Hoare agrees that the advantage of being small is knowing your customers, who, in his case, are some of the wealthiest people in Britain. Hoares has just two branches, in London – one in Belgravia and the original in Fleet Street, which has been there since 1672, making it older than the Bank of England. “We call it the shop,” he says. There’s no bulletproof glass, digital signage, or anything as ghastly as a cash machine.

To open an account at the bank, you need to be able to borrow or deposit at least £1m. That’s the easy part. The harder aspect is that you have to be vetted by one of the eight partners – the family members who own the bank. “Are they [the potential customers] like-minded? Do they share the same values? Would we enjoy having them to lunch with other customers?” says Alexander, 64, the senior partner. He admits that this may not be seen as “professional” in the modern City, determined to maximise profits. “But if you’re trying to develop a really excellent brand to hand down to your descendants, to hand down to their descendants, I think you want a bank which is relatively small and very simple.”

When it comes to recruiting customers, it has a firm “no potentates” rule. “It means no sultans, no sheiks, no oligarchs, no princes from anywhere – and that includes Windsor Castle,” laughs Hoare. He is being mischievous, but the bank’s informal vetting has stood it in good stead in recent years, when other financial organisations have been hit by scandals.

He says that Hoares has a mere 12,000 “relationship groups”, the bank’s term for a customer, which might be a charity, a family office, or a wealthy individual; Hoare reckons that Coutts, the more famous private bank, has “10 times as many”.

Keeping it simple and oligarch-free has worked. When Sir Richard Hoare, the founder of the bank, died in 1719, the bank had £263,000 on its balance sheet; last year it had £7.2bn.

But having survived for more than three centuries is no guarantee of success. Far from it. The Tercentenarians, as a club, was founded in the early 1970s by Geoffrey Durtnell, who was then running his family’s house-building company, R Durtnell & Sons, in Kent, which started in the reign of Elizabeth I. It ceased business in 2019, though the family retains a small property development company. The Whitechapel Bell Foundry, established in 1570, stopped operations in 2017, aged 447. Early’s of Witney, a blanket maker and early member of the Tercentenarians, shut down in 2002 after 333 years.

Alex Durtnell, 49, says he is relieved that he’s no longer running a large housebuilding empire: “It’s become so much tougher to run a business. It’s bloody hard out there. Thank God we’re not having to deal with employing lots of people, National Insurance, fuel costs.”

For a moment during Covid, it looked as if Toye, Kenning & Spencer might also run out of steam. Founded in 1685 by a French Huguenot, it started in east London as a textile company, making military uniforms, before specialising in buttons, cap badges, ribbons, aiguillettes, sashes, gold braid and other regalia used by anyone from the Army to a golf club that wants to add a bit of swagger to its uniform or bling to its trophies. “Everything we do is ceremonial, outgoing, regalia, events,” says Frederick Toye, 38, the company’s current chairman, who explains how business struggled at the start of the pandemic. “The government [commissions] continued, but all the private-sector stuff stopped, and it took a long time to come back.”

The company went into a Company Voluntary Arrangement – a way of keeping a company going while reducing its debts. But then HM Revenue & Customs demanded that an unpaid tax bill be paid. “We thought that the government was aware of the problems of Covid… we were a bit taken aback by the sudden requirement to pay all the money,” says Frederick.

The company then went into administration, which is often fatal for a business. “I think our heritage was totally a burden,” says Frederick, who – with his older brother – was faced with trying to save a company that had survived 11 generations. “It was a huge pressure.”

He and Charles, 42, who runs the company day to day, had to “rationalise the business” before buying it out of administration. “We’re now in a much better position,” says Frederick. The company employed 200 people a few years ago; it now employs 80 across its two sites in the Birmingham Jewellery Quarter and in Bedworth, Warwickshire.

“Whatever happens, we want to keep the business going and keep all the craftsmanship going.”

He was helped by the fact that the family owned its factories, meaning it did not have a landlord breathing down its neck, demanding rent. “If I could advise any Tercentenarian, I would say: do whatever you can to never lose your property,” Frederick says. Even Richard Balson, the butcher, says: “A lot of our luck is because our forefathers had the good sense to buy the shop in 1892 for £275 – we’ve had no rent to pay. Town centres have been decimated by high rents.”

The one advantage, also, of being really old is that a company has an institutional memory, Toye believes – however bad things are, they’ve probably been worse at some point. “The single biggest disaster for us was the late 1800s when they invented cordite – smokeless gunpowder,” Toye tells me as we tour the Birmingham factory. With cordite, in came khaki uniforms, out went brightly coloured tunics with sparkly braid. “Almost overnight we lost our biggest customer – the military. So we turned to associations and clubs. We made all the medals and sashes for the Suffragette movement.”

You adapt or die. You find new customers or new ways of working. We are in the cellars of the factory, where the company stores – on miles of dusty shelves – all the dies used to stamp medals and badges, each one a tiny mirror image of some crest or logo. There’s a section for the Orient Express uniform buttons, the Rolls-Royce marque, cap badges for the Honourable Artillery Company, masonic badges, family crests, a badge for the Rolling Stones and a medal for the Omani royal family.

Upstairs, where the medals are designed and made, it’s a mix of old and new. Gary Hiley, 63, is in an office in front of a large computer screen, using 3D software to design a skateboard brand, while in the “stamping room” they are finalising the medals for the Commonwealth Games in Glasgow this summer – all 1,206 of them. Here, it is unashamedly industrial, with bins overflowing with swarf – metal offcuts – stained workbenches and sparks flying out of grinding machines.

Each medal is “tapped” at least four times by a vast, Victorian-looking friction screw press that exerts 3.5 tons of pressure. The man in charge is Mick Puusta, 63, whom Toye describes as “the expert on all things that need to be hit hard”.

Puusta, with a long ponytail and Brummie accent, has worked in the Jewellery Quarter since he left school. I ask him how the company has survived. “Grim determination and quality,” he answers drily. We discuss how hard it is to persuade people to join an industry that feels closer in time to the Industrial Revolution than the era of AI, and also the difficulty of competing with China. But he brightens and says: “I think there will be a backlash. People want craftsmanship. The Turkish barbers nearby has been replaced by an artisan bakery.”

This is the hope of many Tercentenarians. They cannot survive on nostalgia, but they can offer something that neither Asia’s low-cost manufacturing facilities nor AI can supply: genuine heritage and things that bring people together.

Jonathan Neame, of Shepherd Neame, talks about “deglobalisation”, a movement that is said to have resulted in both Brexit and Donald Trump. The brewery survived the period in the 1980s and Nineties when small British breweries were being snapped up by vast multinationals. “I would say the zeitgeist of today is very different to the Eighties, which was all about consumerism, brands and marketing,” he says. “Today the zeitgeist is localism and authenticity.” Consumers increasingly want to know where their pinta or pint has come from.

The Faversham brewery is open for tours. Yes, there is a working steam engine from 1789 that is purely for the tourists, but nearly everything else ancient in the brewery is there because it is used to make the beer, from the Victorian water tank held together with rivets to the wooden malt screen (bought second-hand in 1920) that filters out any twigs and dust from the malt. Rupert Hodgkins, the brewery manager, says that “as a history and brewing enthusiast” he loves working in such a historic site, “but from a day-to-day perspective, and making things work, it’s a complete pain in the a---”. Bits of 21st-century technology have to be retrofitted on to old machines, while the site’s loading bays are designed for horses and carts, not HGVs.

Those who visit, however, immediately realise that this is a proper brewery, rooted in its geography. Climb up to the roof and, across the medieval roofs of Faversham, you can see the fields where the Kentish hops are harvested. The company runs 285 pubs – all of them in Kent and the South East. The farthest west it stretches is Shoreham-by-Sea in West Sussex and it ventures no farther north than Colchester in Essex. Go too far afield and you lose control, explains Neame.

But like Toye, Kenning & Spencer making badges for skateboard brands, or Webster & Horsfall, which has diversified to make industrial wire for offshore wind farms, you have to keep moving with the times. “Pubs are an incredibly sensitive lead indicator to consumer confidence and disposable income,” Neame says. “So when people feel happy and feel confident, they go out. When they don’t, they don’t.” He is painfully aware that the British pub industry is in crisis, hit by high taxes and changing consumer tastes. His company has not only introduced a non-alcoholic IPA to its roster of beers, it has also upgraded the Wi-Fi in all its pubs to encourage people to work from the pub, rather than from home, arguing that “the minute we stop adapting, we will fail”.

Does it matter if customers are sipping a Diet Pepsi or a coffee, rather than a delicious Master Brew bitter? “I’m agnostic – I just want people to come to my pubs,” he laughs.

He remains upbeat, however. Shepherd Neame has survived far worse things than high taxes and consumers cutting back on beer drinking. The government nearly nationalised its brewery during the First World War, and it lived through the temperance movement before that. “To have a sense of optimism – that a better future lies ahead – relies slightly on your understanding of your historical perspective,” he smiles.

“Why do any of the companies in Tercentenarian Club survive? We survive because fundamentally we’ve got a good business model and fundamentally we’re fulfilling a timeless need. And the need we’re fulfilling is not for alcohol. The need we’re fulfilling is [as a] place for the community to socialise.”

In an era when more and more of us realise that being tethered to our screens is bad for our health, companies that offer a face-to-face service, or that bring a community together, might just have a few years left in them, be they a butcher, brewer, boat-builder or bank.

data.title: Mike Ashley’s Frasers launches £1.7bn bid to buy Hugo Boss
data.description:
Mike Ashley’s retail empire has made a £1.7bn offer for Hugo Boss, six years after it first took a position in the luxury fashion house.

The British billionaire’s Frasers Group said it had made an all-cash offer to buy the 102-year-old German suit maker, in which it already holds a 26pc stake.

It comes as Mr Ashley seeks to bolster his empire, broadening its reach in luxury fashion and growing its business in Europe.

Under the offer, Frasers would buy the 74pc of shares in Hugo Boss that are currently outside its control, including those listed on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange.

The €38 (£32.80) a share offer marks a roughly 4pc premium on Hugo Boss’s current stock price of €36.46 (£31.47).

His target is Germany’s biggest luxury fashion business, which achieved €4.3bn (£3.7bn) worth of sales in 2025. The brand was founded in Metzingen, Germany, in 1922, and first floated in Frankfurt in 1989.

Frasers has sought to forge closer ties with Hugo Boss for several years, and has sold its luxury suits through House of Fraser and Flannels stores.

Michael Murray, who is both Frasers Group’s chief executive and Mr Ashley’s son-in-law, already sits on Hugo Boss’s board.

Mr Ashley, who owns an almost 74pc stake in Frasers Group, started building a stake in Hugo Boss in 2020 and is now its biggest shareholder.

Under German law, any shareholder who holds more than 30pc of a publicly listed company must make an offer to buy all remaining shares.

It will be seen as part of Frasers’ recent “elevation strategy” which has involved investments in luxury fashion brand Flannels and bespoke tailor Gieves & Hawkes, and is aimed at transforming itself from a discount retailer to a more high-end business.

Mr Ashley formed Sports Direct in 1982 before rebranding it as Frasers Group in 2019 when it took over the House of Fraser department store chain and saved it from the brink of collapse.

The retail business started with a single store in Maidenhead, trading under the name Mike Ashley Sports. 

However, Mr Ashley has since used the company to build a sprawling empire that has taken over some of Britain’s biggest retailers, including Evans Cycles and Jack Wills. It has also built sizeable stakes in online retailers such as ASOS, Boohoo and AO World.

Mr Ashley is now worth more than £3bn, making him one of the richest people in Britain, according to the Sunday Times Rich List.

The British billionaire submitted a bid in March for Sheffield Wednesday football club, having sold his stakes in Newcastle United FC to Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund and his stake in Rangers FC to the Club 1872 fan group and Julian Wolhardt, a private equity boss.

Frasers Group declined to comment. Hugo Boss was contacted for comment.

data.title: It’s official: train Wi-Fi doesn’t work
data.description:
Wi-Fi on Britain’s trains almost never works, official research has found, underscoring the blight of patchy coverage plaguing commuters.

A major new survey by Ofcom revealed that onboard Wi-Fi provided by train companies worked properly just 1pc of the time, making it all but impossible for passengers to connect.

The regulator blamed the shoddy service on outdated technology and data-speed caps imposed by train operators.

The watchdog found that nearly all train Wi-Fi failed to meet basic standards that would allow smartphone users to perform simple online tasks.

“Across all lines, on-board Wi-Fi met our good performance standard in only 1pc of tests,” Ofcom said, “although on some journeys Wi-Fi was not available or could not be tested.”

Almost half of all rail connections in the UK rely on older Wi-Fi standards dating back to 2009, according to Ookla’s separate research last year. Around 40pc of onboard services also use wireless spectrum bands that have lower capacity and greater susceptibility to congestion and interference than alternatives.

The one rail franchise bucking the trend is South Western Railway, Ofcom found, where targeted investment in Wi-Fi infrastructure between Earlsfield in London and Basingstoke in Hampshire meant passengers enjoyed a good connection 83pc of the time.

Ofcom also found that mobile networks offered an overwhelmingly poor-quality service on train lines, with passengers able to access a good connection a quarter of the time on average.

The figures varied significantly across different networks. BT-owned EE was the top performer, providing a good service on 42pc of the measured journeys.

Three and O2 achieved good signals 21pc and 20pc of the time, respectively, while Vodafone lagged behind at 17pc.

The survey, which is the most comprehensive on the subject to date, defined good performance as minimum download speeds of five megabits per second (Mbps), upload speeds of at least 1.5 Mbps, and a response time of 50 milliseconds or less.

These speeds would typically allow users to make video calls, stream films and TV shows, and scroll through social media.

The figures reflect the experience of train passengers across the UK who have long struggled with patchy coverage.

Railway lines are notoriously challenging for mobile network operators as trains pass rapidly between different mobile masts through tunnels, cuttings and valleys, while signals often struggle to penetrate metal carriages.

Pledge for change

Heidi Alexander, the Secretary of State for Transport, has vowed to upgrade poor Wi-Fi on more than a thousand trains across the country as part of Labour’s nationalisation of the railways.

New technology will be rolled out across train services, allowing them to connect to services such as Elon Musk’s Starlink, which beams down internet connections from a network of satellites in low-Earth orbit.

Ministers have also unveiled plans to extend connectivity to dozens of tunnels and cuttings on Britain’s key intercity routes.

However, Ofcom called for a nationwide effort by the Government, mobile network operators and technology providers to help upgrade connections on railways and, more broadly, across the UK.

It also urged local authorities to step up amid concerns that Nimbyism was blocking the installation of new mobile masts required to improve the signal.

Natalie Black, head of infrastructure and connectivity at Ofcom, said: “People rightly expect connectivity they can count on – and delivering it will require a joined‑up national effort.”

Mobile UK, which represents mobile network operators, called for “dedicated public investment” to help tackle “complex trackside blackspots, as commercial rollout alone cannot bridge the gap on the rail network”.

data.title: Labour accused of ‘undermining UK’ by buying cheap Chinese steel
data.description:
Labour has been accused of undermining British industry by buying up cheap Chinese steel for defence projects.

UK steel producers claim to have missed out on work because the Ministry of Defence (MoD) is instead buying from international markets. The grades of steel being purchased include kinds that are already produced by UK mills.

It comes despite demands from Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, for government departments to “buy British” wherever possible.

Andrew Kinniburgh, director general of industry lobby group Make UK Defence, singled out the MoD’s Defence Infrastructure Organisation, which manages the military estate, for particular criticism.

He told MPs on the Treasury committee: “They are spot buying from China, which I find unacceptable. We should be buying British steel for that.”

On Friday, Mr Kinniburgh added: “MoD needs to treat the UK steel sector as a strategic asset by procuring categories of steel made in the UK for its infrastructure projects.

“Steel is not a common commodity, it’s a national asset for UK defence.

“The Defence Infrastructure Organisation should demonstrate leadership in this space by signing the UK Steel Charter and ensure they back our UK steel suppliers when we have the right capabilities here at home.”

The MoD has long been accused of taking a laissez-faire approach to supporting local steel producers, even as the industry fights for survival.

In many cases, defence officials say that specialist grades of steel needed for Dreadnought submarines and other projects are no longer made in Britain and so must be imported.

However, critics claim that work which could be done by domestic firms is also routinely handed to overseas rivals based on cost alone.

Less than one third of the tonnage procured for major defence projects was supplied by British firms in the 2024-25 financial year, according to government data.

In cash terms the share was higher, however, with British firms taking 45pc, or about £52m of a total £114m spent.

Over the next decade, the MoD is expected to procure another 139,000 tonnes of steel through contracts expected to be worth more than £300m.

However, it is thought that around three-fifths of this may ultimately need to be imported because the required products are not made in the UK.

This includes steel needed for the Type 26 and Type 31 frigates, the Boxer mechanised infantry vehicle, the Ajax armoured vehicle, Challenger 2 tanks and the Dreadnought and Aukus submarine programmes.

Recently, defence firms have raised concerns that forthcoming government tariffs on foreign steel are set to cause havoc.

Mr Kinniburgh warned that some British steel brokers were importing specialist steels that are not made domestically and supplying them to the military, but now face tariffs of up to 50pc from July for doing so.

The Government announced higher tariffs on shipments of steel from abroad beyond certain quotas amid concerns that a flood of cheap products from China threatens to wipe out the domestic industry.

However, Mr Kinniburgh said that quotas for some products had been set far too low.

He told MPs: “We absolutely need to make it very clear that there are hundreds of types of steel and we only make a relatively small number of those types of steel in the UK.”

One company alone, Barrett Steel, bought around 4,000 tonnes of specialist steel from overseas and supplied it for defence purposes, he said, while the tariffs are triggered after imports of just 1,000 tonnes.

Mr Kinniburgh added: “We’ve got very serious concerns about that.”

A spokesman for the MoD said: “We are backing British businesses, making defence an engine for growth and boosting the UK economy.

“Steel for our major defence programmes is generally sourced by prime contractors from a range of UK and international suppliers. Where steel is available in the UK, and is technically and commercially feasible, the vast majority is procured here. 

“Through this Government’s UK Steel Strategy, we have set out plans to revitalise the UK steel sector and bolster sovereign steelmaking capabilities for defence.”

data.title: Denby sends final pottery to the kiln after 217 years in business
data.description:
For generations of British households, a Denby dinner set was as reliable a gift as any for newlyweds embarking on married life.

But on Thursday, the last pieces from the historic pottery brand were rolled into the kilns, bringing 217 years of production to an end.

Founded in 1809, the ceramics company has been producing tableware from locally sourced Derbyshire clay ever since.

Prized for its weight and durability, sets of matte green Chevron mugs or brown Arabesque plates featuring geometric yellow swirls were a staple of 1970s china cabinets.

But in March 2026, the company collapsed into administration after struggling with rising energy and labour costs.

In an emotional farewell video, the company called the closure “hard to comprehend” and thanked its loyal customers and employees for their support.

A video posted on social media showed an employee called Roger, who has worked at Denby for 54 years, wheeling a trolley of clay bowls and vases to the kiln while employees clapped in support.

“Centuries of making pottery at Denby may be coming to a close, but the love and soul poured into each piece will live on,” the company captioned the post.

“Denby would not have been able to continue for this long without generations of dedicated, skilled people, from the kilns to customers’ homes and all the pairs of hands in between. Thank you to every member of staff, past and present, for playing your part in taking care of this piece of history.

“The future is always uncertain and whilst it’s hard to comprehend what things may look like in the next weeks and months, we are so hugely proud of everything this Derbyshire pottery has achieved. It’s not and has never just been about the pots.”

Often described as the biggest studio pottery in the world, Denby has always been handcrafted with more than 20 pairs touching every unique piece it produced.

It was founded in the village of Denby by William Bourne who discovered high-quality local clay, which he used to produce salt-glazed bottles that were then exported globally in the 1830s.

Known for its motto “buy well, buy once”, the manufacturer issued a 10-year guarantee on its hard-wearing stoneware collections called the “Collectors Promise” and has long been a symbol of traditional British craftsmanship.

It also showed an ability to adapt with the times, leading the way in the “oven-to-table” movement in the 1970s that marketed stoneware for practical, everyday use, not just special occasions.

When it entered administration it became the latest casualty of a crisis that has swept through Britain’s ceramics industry, igniting a political row about energy costs.

Campaigners argued that pottery manufacturers were left at a disadvantage by being excluded from government support packages, which had been made available to other energy-intensive industries during the war in Iran amid rising oil and gas prices.

Tom Tugendhat, the Tory MP, called the closure of the Denby kilns a “man-made tragedy” and blamed Ed Miliband’s net zero targets for “pushing businesses to the wall”.

A petition to save Denby reached 101,000 signatures, passing the threshold to be considered for a debate in Parliament.

Launched by Hayley Baddiley, Denby’s former global marketing director, it called for the ceramics industry to be included in the British Industry Supercharger scheme that provides relief from electricity costs for certain sectors, including steel and chemicals.

Denby also owns the Burleigh pottery brand, which was saved in 2010 when the Prince of Wales, now the King, helped secure £9m for emergency repairs at its historic Middleport Pottery site in Stoke-on-Trent.

Denby has marked key milestones in British culture, designing commemorative pieces for the Coronations of King Edward VII, George VI and Queen Elizabeth I, as well as the marriage of King Charles to Diana, Princess of Wales.

It has long been associated with British manufacturing heritage, with its products sold in several overseas markets including South Korea, China and the US.

Last week, discount retailer Home Bargains launched a last-ditch attempt to rescue the brand.

If successful, the deal would hand control to a retailer that is primarily known for selling cut-price versions of homeware, including cleaning products, garden furniture and bird feeders.

Other homeware groups are also said to have expressed an interest in buying the brand’s assets out of administration.

Craig Thomson, a GMB Union representative, said the closure was a “disaster” for Derbyshire.

“Without products being made here in the UK, the brand of Denby would not have held the unique position it has held for 200 years,” he said.

“New support for the industry changes the picture for UK ceramics, and administrators are being short-sighted in not allowing new buyers to come forward.”

The closure was met with an outpouring of grief online, with members of the public sharing personal stories of items that have been in their families for generations.

“My grandma has recently gifted us her full Denby dinner set from her wedding, it’s over 60 years old and every piece is still in perfect condition,” said one social media user. “I hope to treasure it for decades to come!”

“Honestly can’t believe that the government aren’t intervening to save @Denbypottery,” said another. “All those jobs, all that skill, all that history.”

data.title: Denby pottery is the latest victim of Labour’s indifference to business
data.description:
SIR – On May 21, Rachel Reeves announced £120m of assistance for the ceramics sector, claiming that “as a Government, we back British industry”.

However, last Thursday, Denby pottery ended 217 years of production because of rising energy and labour costs (report, June 8).

Once again, this was too little, too late from a Government that fails to recognise the damage it is causing through its policies. The Chancellor appears indifferent to the fact that the rush to net zero is wrecking British businesses.

Sadly, whoever wins the next election will spend the first term attempting to salvage what is left of the British economy after yet another failed socialist experiment.

Stephen Howey
Woodford Green, Essex

SIR – The loss of Denby pottery after 217 years is tragic, not only for the staff and their families, but for all of us. Denby has a worldwide reputation for British excellence. There is simply no domestic pottery ware that is of better quality. Yet the very high cost of energy in our country, and the rising expenses involved in employing staff, have finally defeated this historic British company.

The Government should be ashamed. How can Labour claim to be the party of working people?

John Twitchen
Leigh-on-Sea, Essex

SIR – How many more long-established businesses are we going to lose because of net zero madness?

A general election is a long way off, by which time plenty of others will have disappeared.

Jack Marriott
Churt, Surrey

SIR – Denby is the latest in a long line of businesses, large and small, that have been unable to tolerate the mushrooming costs inflicted by the Chancellor.

As you correctly point out in your report, Denby pottery was a reliable gift for newlyweds. We received ours 53 years ago, and are sad to see the company go.

Martin Henry 
Good Easter, Essex

SIR – You report (June 9) that thousands of customers are demanding refunds from tourist attractions after Rachel Reeves unveiled summer discounts for popular family destinations.

This was an ill-conceived proposal from the start. It was never likely to raise a cheer in pubs, restaurants and hotels – the industries at the heart of the British summer – which remain burdened by a 20 per cent VAT rate, roughly double that of Spain, Italy and France.

Offering consumers the occasional discounted meal or subsidised outing while continuing to impose such punitive rates of tax on British hospitality completely undermines the objective.

If the Government wants to ease pressure on household budgets, then cutting VAT on hospitality would be a far more meaningful measure. Instead, this joyless administration appears more comfortable announcing token gestures than addressing the costs and taxes weighing so heavily on businesses and consumers alike.

Clive Slater
Arundel, West Sussex

SIR – If Rachel Reeves had been the finance director of a substantial private-sector enterprise, she would have been sacked long ago for the serious mistakes she has made.

Pascal Ricketts
London SW1

 

Race and equality

SIR – The more I hear from Kemi Badenoch, the more convinced I am that she is prime minister material. 
Unlike some others, she has made measured comments about the murder of Henry Nowak.

She is quite right in calling for an end to race playing a part in decision-making, whether in the police, Armed Forces recruitment or NHS mental health services (“Tories pledge to scrap ‘ludicrous’ equality duty in public sector”, report, June 9). The best way to combat racism is to treat everyone as equals. Highlighting differences breeds resentment.

I volunteer at a venue that attracts thousands of visitors of all races, which treats everybody in exactly the same way, without any need for diversity training. The sooner big organisations follow suit, the quicker the country can concentrate on more important priorities such as job creation and growing the economy.

Barry Gibbs
Wimborne, Dorset
 

Couples’ rights

SIR – I disagree with your contention that the surviving member of a long-term cohabiting relationship should not have the same inheritance tax (IHT) rights as somebody who is married or in a civil partnership (“Marriage is not just another lifestyle choice”, Leading Article, June 7).

Should a spouse or civil partner die, the survivor may be fortunate enough to find love again, yet not feel the need to remarry. Why should this couple be treated worse than a married one?

Peter Munro
Wincanton, Somerset
 

Content warnings

SIR – I sympathise with Celia Walden’s annoyance at getting a prudish content warning about a photograph of her cat, Dennis (Features, June 9).

While at seminary, I got a similar message when looking for material for an essay on St Luke’s Passion Narrative.

Fr Richard Duncan
Birmingham

SIR – Wokery almost destroyed Doctor Who. Please don’t let the same happen to James Bond (“Elba: Don’t make James Bond woke or black – audiences won’t like it”, report, June 9).

Anne Langley
Wolverhampton
 

Rough and ready

SIR – In the 1960s, on Eel Pie Island (then famous for the Rolling Stones), we would drink a pint of “rough” scrumpy with Guinness (Letters, June 9). We only needed one pint, so it was a cheap night out. Needless to say, we got the late night bus home.

John K Greenwood
Lorigné, Deux-Sèvres, France

SIR – During my childhood, whenever my mother hosted a barbecue, she would provide beer for the men, white wine for the ladies and cider for my brothers and me.

She had grown up in Somerset and was convinced that cider was non-alcoholic. She always wondered why we awoke the next morning with severe headaches.

Gary Fielding
Disley, Cheshire

SIR – I drank my first and last pint of cider when I was 16.

I was in a pub on the side of a mountain in South Wales. Afterwards, we walked home over the mountain. Today, more than 60 years later, I can still picture the stile where I was sick.

Alun Harvey
Groningen, Netherlands

 

Chivalry at Stansted

SIR – Chivalry is not dead (Letters, June 8). As I walked with my stick through Stansted Airport on Monday, three men asked if I needed help going down stairs. I didn’t – but I was so pleased to know that people care. Well done those men.

Pam Haworth
Shipston-on-Stour, Warwickshire
 

Living with cancer

SIR – When Sir Chris Hoy was 48, he revealed that his prostate cancer had spread to his bones and he’d been told he had four years to live. Last week, discussing his charity cycle ride, Tour de 4, he said that there is plenty of life left in him (report, June 8).

My prostate cancer has spread to my bones. I was told 15 months ago that there was no more treatment available, and that I had less than a year to live.

I am still going – not so strongly as Sir Chris, but I am 38 years older. I bet he is still riding long after his allotted four years.

Bob Paterson
Oxted, Surrey
 

Pet proliferation

SIR – Each time my husband goes on a skiing holiday with our sons, he comes home to find that I have another pet (Letters, June 9). So far we have two dogs and a cat, but a horse has a certain appeal. I can’t wait for next year’s trip.

Sharon Wilson
Wendover, Buckinghamshire

 

Adults must lead by example on smartphones

SIR – I agree with Camilla Tominey (Comment, June 8) that children should be protected from the cesspit that is social media, and that a ban until 14 is more workable than a ban for under-16s.

Regardless of legislation, parents – and adults in general – have an important role to play. Children imitate adults, and as with habits such as drinking alcohol and smoking, if they see adults continually scrolling on their smartphones, they will do the same.

Currently, virtually every adult they see is at it, from their own parents – the most influential people in their lives – to Members of Parliament and media correspondents. Every film they see features smartphones.

Adults must practise what they preach – or lose their credibility and influence.

Stan Labovitch
Windsor, Berkshire

SIR – Our daughter, aged 13, will start at her new senior school in September. Included in the new parent information pack was the specification that only “brick” phones are allowed, along with images and examples. How wonderfully freeing for the pupils, teachers and parents.

Bunty Clarke
Horsted Keynes, West Sussex

SIR – As a Catholic, I am pleased that in his first encyclical, “Magnifica Humanitas”, Pope Leo is at pains to emphasise the dignity of humanity in the age of AI. This technology could be dangerous and dehumanising if allowed to run rampant, with no legal and moral containment.

Yes, we can embrace it in some areas of our lives, but personally I do not want a chatbot as a spiritual adviser. Independent or human-guided thought should remain the norm.

Judith A Daniels
Great Yarmouth, Norfolk

 

Late-night study with ‘Whispering’ Bob Harris

SIR – On behalf of my fellow undergraduates of the late 1960s and early 1970s, I would like to pay tribute to the likes of “Whispering” Bob Harris, together with his fellow broadcaster, the late John Peel (“Whispering Bob tunes out from Radio 2”, report, June 5).

Without doubt, the late-night radio companionship, wise words, comforting dialogue and musical choices provided a reassuring environment as we all strove through the night in our attempts at learning – which, in my case, meant revision of obscure facts about Darwinism, taxonomy and the biology and sex lives of jellyfish and corals.

I remain eternally grateful. I am in no doubt that I would not have obtained my honours degree without them.

Paul Strong
Claxby, Lincolnshire

data.title: South East Water breaches Ofwat licence after supply chaos
data.description:
South East Water has breached its operating licence just days after it was struck by fresh supply chaos, leaving thousands of customers without water during the heatwave.

The company’s debt has been downgraded by ratings agency Moody’s, meaning it no longer meets licence conditions imposed by the regulator Ofwat.

South East Water confirmed that Moody’s had revised the rating on its senior secured debt from Baa3 – the lowest rung of “safe” investment territory – to Ba1 – which is “junk” status.

As a condition of its operating licence with regulator Ofwat, South East Water is required to hold at least two investment-grade credit ratings at all times.

With the Moody’s downgrade, the company has lost one of those ratings and is now in breach of that requirement.

It puts South East Water at risk of a fine, with Ofwat able to issue penalties of 10pc of a company’s annual turnover if it breaches its operating licence.

An Ofwat spokesman said: “We are aware of Moody’s downgrade, which reinforces our position that a comprehensive financial and operational turnaround in the company’s operations is essential.

“The downgrade puts the company in breach of its licence. We are therefore engaging with them on potential undertakings to secure their compliance by regaining the necessary investment grade rating.”

South East Water said its board was “constructively engaging with Ofwat to agree certain commitments” to restore compliance, and added that it “maintains strong liquidity and a resilient capital structure”.

Henry Tuffnell, an MP on the environment, food and rural affairs select committee, said: “South East Water’s loss of their operating licence is long overdue. For too long the company has acted with impunity and it is right that there are finally consequences for their continued failure.

“I hope with new leadership and a radical cultural overhaul within the company that customers will finally start to get the service they deserve.”

The downgrade by Moody’s came as South East Water warned it was still facing supply issues amid “extremely high demand during the very hot weather”.

On Thursday evening, the company said 8,000 customers in Whitstable, Kent, are still without water, while another 14,000 customers in Tankerton, Ashford, and its surrounding areas were facing low water pressure or patchy supply.

Steve Benton, South East Water’s incident manager, said: “We are continuing to ask customers ... to use water for essential purposes only – for example drinking, washing and cooking.”

Parts of Whitstable are expected to see supply return this evening.

The latest issues follow severe disruption at the end of 2025 that left around 24,000 homes and businesses across Kent and East Sussex without water for nearly a fortnight.

The Drinking Water Inspectorate concluded an investigation into the outage in April, with the findings pointing to “systemic and repeated failings across both operational control and emergency management arrangements, resulting in serious consumer impact”.

Earlier this month, MPs on the environment, food and rural affairs select committee released a critical report of the utility’s handling of the supply problems.

After this report, Dave Hinton, the chief executive of the utility, and Chris Train, its chairman, resigned.

Separately, Ofwat is planning to fine the company £22.5m for repeated supply failures affecting almost 287,000 customers between 2020 and 2023.
