data.title: Weight-loss drug trial results wipe £147bn off Ozempic maker
data.description:
Almost £150bn has been wiped off Ozempic drugmaker Novo Nordisk after a trial of its new weight-loss drug fell short of expectations.

Shares in the Danish pharmaceutical giant plunged by 15pc on Monday, wiping around €169bn (£147bn) off its market value in just 90 minutes.

Trading was briefly halted in Copenhagen as a result of the sharp sell-off, which drove shares to their lowest level since 2021.

It came after Novo revealed that its new obesity treatment had proven less effective than drugs made by US rival Eli Lilly.

The Danish company’s new drug CagriSema, which is delivered via a weekly injection, achieved weight loss of 23pc after 84 weeks, it said on Monday. This compares to 25.5pc for tirzepatide, the active ingredient in Eli Lilly’s weight-loss drugs Mounjaro and Zepbound.

CagriSema – a combination of the hormone cagrilintide and semaglutide, the compound in its existing drugs Ozempic and Wegovy – had been proposed as a follow-up to today’s weight-loss drugs.

However, the disappointing trial is the latest setback for Novo, which is facing fierce competition from Eli Lilly.

The company said the new drug appeared to be safe and caused only mild gastrointestinal side effects.

Novo was once Europe’s most valuable company and was briefly worth more than the entire Danish economy, but its share price has fallen back as copycat drugs grow in popularity in the US.

It is also contending with demands from Donald Trump, the US president, that pharmaceutical companies slash the cost of medication for Americans.

Novo said last year it would cut 9,000 jobs, or around 11pc of its total workforce, as part of an effort to cut 8bn Danish kroner (£930m) in costs. It has warned that profits and sales could drop by as much as 13pc this year as a result of tough competition and falling US prices.

The company’s changing fortunes have had a knock-on effect on the wider Danish economy, which has become increasingly reliant on the drugmaker.

Funds have invested billions of dollars in Novo, meaning its declining value has wiped out large chunks of Danish savers’ pensions.

The OMX Copenhagen 25 index fell as much as 3.6pc on Monday while the krone also weakened, suggesting investors were pulling funds from Denmark.

Per Hansen at Nordnet said: “Novo Nordisk’s downturn is creating negative spillover effects for other Danish stocks. It is likely foreign investors who are collectively pulling out of Danish companies.”

data.title: Car finance scandal payouts risk being delayed until 2027
data.description:
Millions of drivers may have to wait until next year to secure payouts from the UK’s car finance compensation scheme, the city watchdog has warned.

The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) told MPs on Tuesday that payouts from a £7.5bn scheme are likely to be delayed after a series of legal complaints.

Speaking before the Treasury select committee, Sarah Pritchard, the deputy chief executive of the FCA, said the challenges also risk increasing costs for the regulator.

“I want to be straightforward that the legal challenge will add delay and extra costs to the scheme as a whole,” Ms Pritchard said. “If the scheme goes ahead, the delay, we believe, will result in payments not before 2027.”

It comes after several parties lodged legal complaints against the FCA’s compensation scheme, including Mercedes-Benz and Volkswagen.

Some 12.1 million car loan deals dating back to 2007 will be covered by the programme, which will award drivers around £830 if they were mis-sold car finance.

However, Nikhil Rathi, the chief executive of the FCA, warned that the compensation scheme could be “struck down in whole or part” as a result of the legal challenges.

This is in stark contrast to claims by the FCA last month, which said “millions of motor finance customers receive compensation this year”.

As well as warning about delayed payouts, the FCA sought to raise concerns about a flood of claims management firms seeking to cash in on the scandal.

In particular, Mr Rathi urged the Government to investigate how offshore funds are seeking to bankroll firms attempting to bring claims on behalf of drivers.

A decision two years ago by the FCA to launch a review of motor finance deals triggered a gold rush to sign up drivers who may be able to claim compensation from banks and other lenders.

Law firms and claims management companies (CMCs) have launched a marketing blitz to lure in clients with no-win, no-fee promises, with some lawyers attempting to take up to 40pc of payouts.

Mr Rathi said that the watchdog had seen “serious and unacceptable harm” by some CMCs and the law firms supporting them. Unwanted texts and emails from these firms are also a growing problem, he added.

He said that while the FCA has taken steps against some companies, there is clear “urgency” for further action from the Government and Parliament.

The watchdog has already taken down more than 800 misleading adverts from CMCs and forced three firms to reduce their fees. It has also blocked four more from taking on new clients.

In January, the FCA took the unprecedented step of announcing an investigation into the Claims Protection Agency (TCPA), which promised to recover thousands of pounds for victims of alleged car finance mis-selling in adverts featuring Tyson Fury, the heavyweight boxer.

The watchdog said it had “concerns about its advertising and sales tactics in relation to potential motor finance claims”.

TCPA said that it had fully co-operated with the FCA in relation to its investigation, which it believes will exonerate the company’s position.

“There are real concerns around how consumers have been signed up to claims management companies,” Ms Pritchard said on Tuesday.

“Some consumers [are] saying that they didn’t provide consent to do so, some having signed up for multiple CMCs, which is resulting in bad outcomes for consumers because consumers that will lose some of the compensation that they are owed.”

data.title: Denmark’s fat jab champion is slimming down the whole economy
data.description:
Novo Nordisk, Denmark’s fat jab pharmaceutical giant, reached an unwelcome milestone last week.

After a dizzying 76pc plunge in its share price over the past 18 months, a torrid week of trading meant it finally gave up the last of its market gains recorded since June 2021 when it released pioneering weight-loss drug Wegovy.

It follows years in which Novo Nordisk has been arguably Denmark’s greatest global success story. Its first-mover success with Wegovy and Ozempic turned it into Europe’s largest company, with the $100bn (£74bn) weight-loss drugs market at its feet.

The tide has since turned. This week, Novo admitted that its newest drugs were showing less promise in trials than those of its American rival Eli Lilly. It has also yielded to Donald Trump’s demand for hefty price cuts in the hitherto lucrative US market.

Novo is now in a metaphorical weight-loss programme of its own. It has shed its long-serving chief executive, cut 11pc of its workforce and lost hordes of foreign shareholders.

This is not a typical tale of corporate tumult. Novo is such a colossus in Denmark that its ructions can shake the country’s whole economy.

Yet Mette Frederiksen, the country’s Left-wing prime minister, chose this moment to take the country to an early election, set for March 24.

Since taking office in 2019, Frederiksen has steered Denmark through the turbulence of the pandemic, the Ukraine war and Trump’s threats to seize Greenland.

She will be hoping that Novo’s travails do not derail her bid for a third straight election victory.

It is something of a gamble. Novo’s fate is inextricably linked to that of the economy and thus to her own political prospects.

The company sold 309bn krone (£36bn) of drugs last year, which is more than 10pc of Denmark’s annual $430bn GDP. It employs one in every 100 Danes.

As Novo’s crisis deepened, Denmark’s economy – which was one of the fastest-growing in Europe – slowed dramatically at the end of last year. The country recorded 2.2pc growth in the third quarter, falling to just 0.2pc in the fourth.

Per Hansen, an economist at bank Nordnet, says: “One of the engines that has been driving the Danish economy over the past two to three years has been Novo Nordisk and the pharma industry.”

Las Olsen, the chief economist at Danske Bank, says that if you remove the pharmaceuticals sector, Denmark’s growth rate would be more like 1pc or 1.5pc.

“Which is fine, of course – but not the stellar growth rate that we have seen in the headline numbers,” he says. “I think we will grow more in line with European economies, perhaps a little less.”

These aren’t views that will cheer up the gloomy Danes.

Measures of Danish consumer confidence have languished in deeply negative territory ever since Russia invaded Ukraine.

“It’s a conundrum,” says Helge Pedersen, the chief economist at Nordea Bank. “House prices are at a record high, there’s almost no unemployment – everything is good but consumers are depressed.”

He says ordinary Danes are still preoccupied with inflation, even though the official rate is just 0.8pc.

“People are still worried about inflation, in particular food prices. We all discuss meat prices, they’re so high, coffee prices, chocolate prices. But that’s perceived inflation, which is higher than reality.”

Frederiksen and her centre-Left coalition partners have already started delving into the public coffers to soothe voters’ disgruntlement.

The Danish government recently cut the tax on electricity bills to almost zero. It also increased spending on childcare and welfare support and removed taxes on coffee, chocolate and sugar.

It has plenty of fiscal firepower. It was running a budget surplus estimated at more than 2pc of GDP in 2025 and its debt-to-GDP ratio was 30pc last year, one third that of Britain.

That allowed Denmark to ramp up defence spending last year by more than $4bn, which – along with higher healthcare expenditure – may prop the economy through any pharma slowdown.

The defence spending is a response to the Russia threat and also to pressure from Trump. He has called for Europe to fund its own defence and he is looking to wrestle at least de facto control over the Danish territory of Greenland.

Hansen says the Greenland issue may also have cast a pall over consumer confidence.

“Danish people are afraid that the new world order that is initiated by Mr Trump is something we have not dealt with since World War Two. That is really what is scaring the Danes,” he says.

Paradoxically, though, it may be helping the tough-talking and experienced Frederiksen. Since Trump’s Greenland manoeuvring became more explicit and tangible, her Social Democratic Party’s previously fading popularity has rebounded.

Geopolitical uncertainty and cost of living pressures are common across Europe. But in Denmark, there could be another more idiosyncratic source of economic anxiety.

Around 10pc of Danes hold shares in Novo. Many more will be exposed to its precipitous share-price decline via their private pensions.

Hansen, though, says Danish investors have been strangely patriotic: as foreigners have fled, Danes have been buying up the stock.

“The number of Danish private clients with us that hold one or more Novo Nordisk shares keeps climbing,” he says. “People still think there is a difference between what Novo Nordisk will achieve longer term and its current valuation in the stock market.”

Denmark’s economists, like its investors, also seem relatively sanguine about their country’s economic champion. Olsen says he was more worried during Novo’s breakneck growth phase than he is now.

“The fear was that it might erode the competitiveness of the rest of the economy. The large amount of hiring at Novo Nordisk could have led to very high base growth, that would then leave the rest of the economy uncompetitive if something was to go wrong,” he says.

“But what we have seen so far is that manufacturing wages growth in Denmark has, if anything, been lower over the last four or five years than we see in competitor countries. There is a competitive economy outside of Novo Nordisk.”

Pedersen agrees that the Danish economy is diversified enough to weather the Novo storm: “But of course you should keep an eye on it because the numbers are still so big on the macro level.

“Politicians do need to be aware that we have two Denmarks – one with and one without the pharmaceutical industry.”

Right now, the politician with the most riding on the Danish pharmaceuticals industry is Frederiksen.

As she called the election, she talked mostly about Greenland and wealth taxes.

Novo Nordisk’s decline might have garnered global attention with the company no longer deemed Denmark’s greatest success story.

Yet for Frederiksen, the hope is that this will simply be background noise as she bids for a third straight election victory – instead of a shot in the arm for her political rivals.

data.title: We can save the housing market and stop the Greens, all with one winning Tory policy
data.description:
Amid sluggish economic growth and rising youth unemployment, it would be easy to overlook the Government’s most glaring failure of all: its miserable effort to deliver on its promise to help young people afford a decent home by building 1.5 million properties over the course of this Parliament. The latest S&P Global Purchasing Managers’ Index shows the construction industry deeper in the doldrums than at any point since the middle of the pandemic. The index stands at just 36 for residential construction, where anything under 50 signifies contraction of the industry.

It means that 2026 will almost certainly see even lower levels of house-building than 2025, when construction began on 150,600 homes across Britain, just half the number Labour would need to reach its target. In London, construction of new homes has come to a virtual standstill as developers find that projects are no longer profitable with the excessive demands for inclusion of social housing units. Sadiq Khan, the Mayor of London, is discovering to his cost that, if you are too greedy in your demands on the private sector, you don’t get any new social housing at all.

Labour has failed on housing, partly because it saw the problem mostly in terms of the planning system. Defeat Nimbyism, believed Angela Rayner, and all would be well. She failed to realise that there are several more important reasons why developers don’t want to build. These are: excessive regulation, which has driven up the cost of building new homes, the war on landlords, which has taken away an important slice of the market, and high mortgage rates.

It is little wonder that young voters, who might normally plump for Labour, are being won over by the Greens instead. Zack Polanski might not know much about economics, but he is an astute politician.

He can see that housing is the number one frustration among the young, feeding a sense of intergenerational unfairness. Even young people with good jobs are finding it impossible to buy a decent home or in many cases even to rent one. Moreover, interest rates are no longer at rock bottom, while rental stock is low because house-building is running at far too slow a rate to keep up with population growth.

Many young graduates have also been unable to land the secure, well-paid employment which they thought would be the reward for university study. Student debt is a burden which no previous generation of 20- to 30-year-olds has had to bear to anything like the same extent. On top of that, many had their studies, or their entry into the jobs market, disrupted by Covid, and AI is eating away at professional career opportunities.

It should not be surprising, therefore, that the Greens captured the imagination of the young by passing a motion at their conference last October promising to “end landlordism”. That appeared to be the moment when the Greens surged in the polls.

But the Greens would be as big a disaster for the housing market as Labour has been, hindering rather than helping young people to find a home. Their policies are entirely negative, designed to appeal to people who are deeply frustrated, not to offer constructive solutions. They are mostly interested in helping councils to buy properties from distressed landlords, not helping people to realise the ambition of home-ownership.

There is an opportunity for another party to target the property market in a more positive way. Kemi Badenoch has made an arresting start by promising to abolish stamp duty for owner-occupiers, which has become a massive deadweight on sales. That promise, like the Greens’ policy on landlordism, helped give the Conservatives a fillip in the polls, as well as boosting Badenoch’s position as leader.

The Tories can and should go further. In the 1980s they prospered as the party of home-ownership, partly because of the right to buy, but also partly down to mortgage interest relief at source (MIRAS). Like tax relief on pensions, MIRAS effectively refunded the tax you paid on that part of your earnings you had used to pay the interest on your mortgage. “At source” meant that the money was automatically taken off your repayments, so you didn’t have to reclaim it retrospectively.

Gordon Brown eventually phased out the policy as he desperately tried to raise revenue while sticking to his promise not to raise tax rates. But people who invest in their own homes are relieving the Government of having to provide social housing for them. They are also more likely to take an interest in their communities. People care more about an area if they have invested their money there.

There is nothing extreme about MIRAS. It is part of the tax system in that most socially democratic of nations, Sweden. It is needed in Britain to serve the aspirations of a generation, which feels left behind. There is no need to grant MIRAS to everyone, which would be expensive, but it should be reintroduced for first-time buyers, allowing them perhaps five or 10 years of mortgage tax relief after they buy a first home. It should be available only on the first £20,000 of mortgage interest and limited to the under-35s. This would give young buyers an advantage in the housing market without punishing older buyers, or buy-to-let investors, in the way that the Greens want to do.

If we boost the purchasing power of first-time buyers, reduce the onerous burden of building regulations and, yes, reform the planning system – not to create a free-for-all, but in a way that speeds up decisions and cuts the cost of the application process, the housing market would revive.

Labour and the Greens, with their anti-business policies, are not going to put it right. The Conservatives can, if they fully recognise how big an issue the affordability of housing has become for young voters. Labour’s failures over housing have presented them with an open goal to re-establish themselves as the party of home-ownership and win over undecided young voters who feel ignored by the present Government.

Ken Costa is an investment banker and author of ‘The 100 Trillion Dollar Wealth Transfer’
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Germany’s ‘HS2’ delayed for five years after engineering blunder
data.description:
A German high-speed railway project likened to HS2 has been delayed after engineers incorrectly laid 1,000km (620 miles) of cables.

Stuttgart 21, an €11bn (£9.4bn) mega-project that was scheduled to open in 2019, will not open for at least another five years as the cables need to be replaced, German media reported on Wednesday.

The setback is the latest in a litany of troubles for Stuttgart 21, which has been compared to Britain’s HS2 network because of its spiralling costs, huge ambition and severe building delays.

According to news outlet SWR, Deutsche Bahn, the rail provider, rushed the cable-laying process as it was frustrated that the project was still dragging on.

As a result, engineers incorrectly laid an estimated 1,000km of railway cables and cable ducts, with the majority of them later declared unfit for purpose.

Begun in April 1994, Stuttgart 21 was promoted as a next-generation railway project with high-speed lines, new underground routes and a brand-new railway station.

Three decades later, state leaders in Baden-Württemberg are beginning to wonder whether the project will be worthwhile, should it ever be completed.

The project had an initial budget of €4.5bn (£3.8bn) when it was due for completion in 2009, with the cost more than doubling as of 2026.

Cem Özdemir, the state president of Baden-Württemberg, has complained that the project is turning into a “Lachplatte [laughing stock]”, according to Die Welt, the German newspaper.

He has ordered the railway network to commit to a fixed budget and timeline. “I don’t want us to get new every year... it has to end sometime,” he said.

Despite their international reputation for efficiency and punctuality, Germany’s trains are a source of embarrassment domestically.

Long delays, cancellations and overcrowded carriages have become the norm in a country that once prided itself on being more advanced than many of its European neighbours.

A study in 2022 found that only 62 per cent of German trains ran on time, compared with 90 per cent in Denmark, 99 per cent in Switzerland and 73 per cent in Britain.

Switzerland has become so fed up with the Germans’ poor punctuality that it has started turning away cross-border train services between the two countries if they are running late.

The humiliating decline of German rail has been blamed on aggressive cost-cutting measures imposed on Deutsche Bahn in the early 1990s, and then a lack of public investment in the following decades.

data.title: Despite the gloom, Britain can flourish. Here’s how
data.description:
Looking around Britain today, surely only a fool would be an optimist. The nation slithers towards the abyss. Every Budget announces new punishment beatings for business. The state makes a sarcastic mockery of working hard and being honest.

The utilitarian law of globalisation, that price is the only thing that matters, no longer exerts such a vice-like grip over the fate of nations. For decades we have been told that for a fractional base point of additional GDP growth we should surrender strong communities, open the borders and abandon industry.

But the world has changed since the collapse of confidence in globalisation – and in many ways, it’s changed to our advantage. This is a conversation the UK has been postponing. In this context, I shall offer five reasons why Britain can flourish.

Firstly, we have natural energy resources that Continental Europe envies. 

It is obscene to impose the highest industrial energy prices in Europe when we have centuries of exploitable natural gas reserves, plus coal. Although electricity blackouts are probably now baked into the system, we can ensure they don’t keep recurring into the 2030s. 

I have commended the tiny Social Democratic Party’s plan of an emergency “grid stabilisation” of 60GW of new gas and coal, allowing a Messmer-scale building programme of dozens of new gigawatt reactors to proceed. There is no need to race to nuclear, skimping on safety as some of the ersatz “pro-growth” groups insist. Not when reactors last 70 years. An abundance of cheap energy must be our top priority.

Our second important natural resource is minerals. We have copper and gold in Northern Ireland, lithium and tin in Cornwall, and cobalt and rare earths in Scotland. 

There may be even more: as I reported a year ago, we haven’t conducted a national geophysical survey since the early 1960s. Whitehall is waking up to this with some urgency.

Now the third piece of the puzzle can fall into place: by reviving our refining and processing industries. 

British pragmatism thrives in devising new industrial processes. Our petrochemicals sector was once a world-beater, and our excellent chemistry and chemical engineering departments at universities provide the brains. Alas, thanks to high energy prices and policy indifference, this is a sector on its knees.

“If you don’t have a chemicals industry... then you can’t have pharmaceutical production, aerospace production or car production,” Grant Pearson, chairman of Teesside ethanol company Ensus, told The Telegraph recently.

Fourth, British business continues to command respect abroad. 

When I mention this, some readers assume I am being sentimental or ironic. But I hear it affirmed every week. Our reputation for fair play has endured. 

It is astonishing that successive prime ministers and chancellors, from George Osborne to Rachel Reeves, have looked up to the hucksters of Silicon Valley and American venture capital as our model for how to conduct business. Unlike them, we leave something on the table in a deal. Global customers know this, and like it.

Finally, we can capitalise on the engineering and manufacturing talent that creates world-class technology. 

Dyson and Renishaw and Martin-Baker are world beaters in consumer markets, medical instruments and aviation, respectively. “The Germans fear our engineers,” Marcus Gibson, whose research company monitors the health of our SMEs, likes to say.

Writing on the Dyson company blog recently, Sir James Dyson pointed out that nine out of 10 private sector companies are family firms, and employ more than half of all private sector workers. It shows how Labour’s war on family businesses and private property owners is so spiteful. At such times Labour looks much more like a global lobbying firm for hedge funds than a political operation acting in the British national interest.

These five parts are interlocking gears in a modern industrial society; each benefits from the other. For example, a supply of low-cost novel plastics encourages entrepreneurs to innovate and makes prototyping easier.

An industrial revival is not uncharted territory. None of this requires a Stalinist top-down plan or autarky. Government interventions could be limited to giving refining, processing, manufacturing and engineering companies a 10-year amnesty period from tax. 

Other state measures that could help include getting finance to co-operate, which forces successful companies to sell out to grow; and protecting inheritance and strengthening intellectual property enforcement. Other than that, the state should get out of the way and let markets do their thing.

However, this does require something almost unthinkable today: a shift away from the utilitarian logic of globalisation which the UK has embedded. 

This is what I call “performative laissez-faire”. It is performative in the same way that making international climate change pledges is performative: no one else really cares how virtuous we are.

The overarching utilitarian creed is that the consumer must get the cheapest goods, at the lowest price, no matter what the consequences may be for national security or communities. China boomed, while we hollowed ourselves out. 

Surely this ideology is now broken on the rocks. Some will sneer that reviving industry means “picking winners”, but this is nonsense: the phrase should hold no fear for us anymore. Giving industrial sectors a break from punishing taxes would make a refreshing change from the Government consistently picking losers.

The UK has already tried being the management consultancy to the world. Now it’s time to make stuff again.

data.title: The adviser at the centre of BP’s boardroom battle
data.description:
Speaking at an event in London last month, long-serving BP executive Ben Mathews was keen to stress that he was not one to sit silently on the sidelines.

When it comes to team meetings, “there shouldn’t be any mice in the room”, he said. “We have a role to play. In the roles I’ve had, there has been an expectation that I contribute to the debate.”

Just weeks later, Mathews’ contribution at BP, where he serves as company secretary, has been thrust into the spotlight.

The 59-year-old has been described as a key driving force behind a boardroom battle at the oil major, having clashed with Albert Manifold in the run-up to the BP chairman’s dramatic defenestration this week amid claims of bullying.

Manifold has strongly refuted the allegations. He has claimed they are “lies” and accused individuals of hiding behind “anonymity”.

But the row has now cast a spotlight on the little-known Mathews and the growing power wielded by the once arcane role of company secretary. As Mathews himself put it just a month ago: “As a company secretary, you see it all.”

Mathews, who began his career at PwC, where he qualified as a chartered company secretary, has stayed loyal to the profession throughout his career. Beginning at BG Group, the oil and gas giant that was bought by Shell for $70bn (£52bn) in 2016, he subsequently moved to mining firm Rio Tinto and then HSBC before joining BP in 2019.

As company secretary, Mathews occupies a unique role in BP’s hierarchy and is responsible for providing independent advice to the board on issues such as corporate governance, legal duties and regulatory requirements. He sits on the board and reports directly to the chairman, acting as a conduit between the board and the executive team.

One of his critical responsibilities in recent months was to help with preparations for BP’s annual general meeting in April – the first where Manifold held the role of chairman following his appointment in September.

The meeting was a disaster for the chairman, with almost a fifth of shareholders voting against his re-election. Legal & General, one of Europe’s biggest asset managers, said before the AGM that it would oppose Manifold, while Glass Lewis, a proxy adviser, recommended investors do the same.

In an embarrassing defeat, shareholders also rejected BP’s attempts to move to online AGMs and scrap the publication of company-specific climate reports.

However, it is Mathews’ apparent role in Manifold’s acrimonious departure this week that has propelled the company secretary into the public eye.

The two men clashed in the run-up to Manifold’s exit, with Mathews raising concerns over the Irish businessman’s behaviour.

The board of BP this week said it was ousting Manifold after discovering “issues it deems unacceptable” regarding his alleged conduct and corporate governance. A source close to the board said Manifold’s dismissal followed allegations of “bullying” and “verbal abuse” of staff. He was also said to be “shouty” and have a “volcanic” temper.

The source said much of this behaviour had been directed at Mathews, often in front of other staff.

In a lengthy statement published on Thursday, Manifold, who was previously the chief executive of the building materials company CRH, said he had “never once had accusations made against me such as those made in recent days”. He is said to have been left “shell-shocked” by the allegations.

Perhaps more telling, however, were suggestions that profligate spending was at the heart of his dispute with BP and, more specifically, its top team.

“Where I saw unnecessary or excessive expenditure, I called it out,” he wrote. “I had no interest in having a dedicated chauffeur-driven limousine at my beck and call on the occasions that I was in London.”

Manifold, who is considering legal action against BP, added: “I, like most people, walked, took taxis, trains, etc. I had no interest in taking private aviation nor in availing myself of corporate tickets for sports events. I made my own coffee and bought my lunch in the local café. I sat in a small office, eschewing the grand corner-office privilege of previous chairmen.”

The ousted chairman praised new chief executive Meg O’Neill, who he described as “ambitious and very talented”, and finance chief Kate Thomson. Pointedly, however, there was no mention of BP’s board.

As the official in charge of governance issues, Mathews is thought to have been at the heart of this clash over costs.

A source close to Manifold said: “It’s not wrong to view Mathews as a driver of this, and there was certainly an argument with him over the approach to costs.”

Meanwhile, a source familiar with the BP board played down claims of an argument about costs and said the clash was primarily related to the chairman’s “personal conduct”.

The source added: “Ben has great integrity and he’s used to tough corporate environments. His former boss at HSBC was Sir Mark Tucker, who is known as a really hard driving guy.

“He is not the only person who had complaints, they were from more than one person, senior people and some who are more junior.”

It is not the first incident of boardroom turmoil under Mathews’ watch. Previous chairman Helge Lund stepped down last year amid a shareholder revolt over BP’s green energy pledge. That was compounded by the departure of chief executive Murray Auchincloss after less than two years and his predecessor Bernard Looney, who resigned over revelations about workplace relationships.

There is no evidence that Mathews, who has been granted medical leave due to stress, played a role in those departures as well.

Nevertheless, the latest saga raises questions over whether BP risks sacrificing shareholder value on the altar of corporate governance.

Manifold, who built CRH from its modest Dublin roots into a global building materials giant, has acknowledged he may have been forthright in some of his dealings at BP.

“Is it possible that in my determination to drive change on costs, performance, the balance sheet and shareholder communications, I pushed hard and challenged people directly? Yes, it is,” he said. “But there is a considerable distance between driving an organisation with urgency and the characterisation of my conduct that is now being put about.”

In Manifold’s telling, his no-nonsense approach was simply too much for BP’s governance-obsessed board to handle.

Other high-profile members of BP’s board include Dame Amanda Blanc, chief executive of insurance giant Aviva, and Johannes Teyssen, the former boss of German energy group E.On and Lufthansa chairman.

Hina Nagarajan, the president of Diageo Africa, and Satish Pai, who leads one of the world’s largest aluminium rolling and recycling companies, Hindalco Industries, also sit on the board.

BP has said it stands by its statement on Manifold’s behaviour, adding: “We have a duty of care to all our employees, particularly those impacted by his behaviour.”

Still, Manifold’s abrupt departure has left BP with yet another leadership vacuum at a time when the company is grappling for direction and raises serious questions about the board’s oversight.

“It’s very messy and it’s not a good look for the board,” says Ashley Kelty, an analyst at Panmure Liberum. “It essentially shows apart from Meg O’Neill and the CFO, you’ve got a board that arguably isn’t fit for purpose.”

data.title: Hedge fund billionaire buys £300m superyacht
data.description:
Billionaire hedge fund tycoon Steve Schwarzman has bought one of the world’s most expensive superyachts.

The Blackstone boss has paid $400m (£300m) to become the new owner of a 332ft superyacht called Project 1014.

Built in the Netherlands, the cutting-edge vessel comes equipped with its own helicopter pad, beach club and glass-bottomed pool.

The yacht, which was designed by British engineering firm RWD with an interior by Paris-based studio Chahan, has been under construction since 2023 and recently began sea trials.

Mr Schwarzman’s purchase, which comes ahead of his 80th birthday next year, is likely to draw scrutiny owing to his environmental credentials.

As well as running one of the world’s largest investment funds, which has more than $1.3tn in assets under management, Mr Schwarzman has sought to advance his philanthropic endeavours in recent years.

In 2020, he signed a so-called Giving Pledge committing to give the majority of his wealth to philanthropic causes.

This includes a £150m donation to the University of Oxford – the biggest single gift since the Renaissance – to fund a new faculty for the humanities bearing his name.

The building is heavily focused on its environmental credentials, achieving certification to the Passivhaus standard for low-energy consumption earlier this year. It is the largest Passivhaus scheme in England and the only concert hall built to the standard in the world.

The environmental credentials stand in stark contrast to Mr Schwarzman’s purchase of a superyacht that guzzles hundreds of gallons of marine diesel per hour.

Oxfam has estimated that the average superyacht emits 5,672 tonnes of carbon a year, a total that would take the average person 860 years to match.

The billionaire supporter of Donald Trump has previously come under scrutiny for transporting tankers of water to fill a lake on his 2,500-acre estate in Wiltshire during a hosepipe ban.

While the actions were legal, Mr Schwarzman was blocked from receiving the water deliveries following a backlash from residents.

Blackstone has also been accused of contributing to deforestation in the Amazon rainforest through its investment in Brazilian firm Hidrovias. The company has described the claims as “blatantly wrong and irresponsible”.

Mr Schwarzman is one of the world’s richest people with a net worth of roughly $40bn. He started his first business at the age of 14, launching a lawn-mowing company and hiring his two younger brothers to carry out the work.

After rising through the ranks at investment bank Lehman Brothers, he left to co-found Blackstone with Peter Peterson in 1985.

Mr Schwarzman backed Mr Trump in the 2024 election and has spoken in favour of the president’s controversial tariff policies.

His other philanthropic ventures include a $350m donation to establish the MIT Schwarzman College of Computing and $150m to Yale University to create the Schwarzman Center, an events and exhibition space for students.

Blackstone did not respond to five requests for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: BP board faces shareholder backlash after ousting chairman
data.description:
BP is facing a shareholder backlash over the board’s “damaging” decision to oust the oil giant’s chairman.

Investors have questioned the board’s judgment and called for a clear-out following the shock sacking of Albert Manifold just eight months into the job.

Dame Amanda Blanc, BP’s senior independent director and the chief executive of Aviva, is under particular pressure after overseeing the recruitment of Mr Manifold.

One shareholder told The Telegraph: “The entire board – including the senior independent director, Amanda Blanc, who led both the hiring and the departure of Albert Manifold – needs to make a collective ‘soul search’ and learn any lessons.

“Over time, there will be the need to inject new blood in the board and progressively replace some members so that serenity can return.”

Another US shareholder said: “It’s obviously damaging because [Mr Manifold is] highly regarded, he’s popular in America and he was doing a good job at CRH. He was instigating change.

“It does look like a sort of board stitch-up, to be honest ... all the potential successors [for chairman] at the board level all look pretty dull.”

Mr Manifold was ousted over what BP said were “serious concerns” about his behaviour, with a source close to the board alleging Mr Manifold bullied and verbally abused colleagues.

Dame Amanda said that the board had been “surprised and disappointed to learn of governance oversight and conduct issues it deems unacceptable and has taken decisive action”.

However, the move has triggered a damaging briefing war.

Mr Manifold has said lies were being spread about him and suggested he was ousted for being too aggressive in pushing cost-cutting measures at BP. He is understood to be considering legal action.

The debacle has reignited a sense of crisis at BP, which is now on its third chairman and fifth chief executive since 2020. Shares slumped by more than 5pc last week.

The US investor said blame for the crisis lay with Dame Amanda as the senior independent director on the board.

Announcing Mr Manifold’s appointment last year, Dame Amanda praised his “impressive track record” and “relentless focus on performance”.

Mr Manifold was seen as an unconventional choice for chairman at the time because of his lack of experience in the oil and gas industry. The 63-year-old made his reputation after turning CRH, an Irish building supplies company, into an international behemoth during his more than 10 years in charge.

A senior business figure who worked closely with Mr Manifold in the past said: “My experience over a number of years is that Albert had a very constructive, respectful, transparent relationship with the board [when he was at CRH].”

Executives were “given the opportunity to fully contribute to engagements with the board”, the person said, following claims that the Irishman had attempted to restrict the ability of Meg O’Neill, BP’s chief executive, to meet independently with non-executive directors.

“Albert had a very clear understanding of and respect for the different roles and responsibilities between executives and non-executives,” the source said.

A shareholder in CRH told The Telegraph: “Whilst we are not shareholders of BP and have no insight into the allegations and situation there, we had an excellent experience with Albert Manifold at CRH and only ever observed high quality management execution and professional conduct.”

Mr Manifold replaced Helge Lund at BP. Mr Lund was pushed out after backing a disastrous pivot to green energy in 2020.

Mr Manifold’s arrival came soon after US activist investor Elliott Management disclosed a significant stake in BP and pushed for the company to cut costs and focus on its core oil and gas business.

Before his ousting, Mr Manifold did just that. He hired Ms O’Neill, a former senior executive at ExxonMobil, two months after becoming chairman in a signal of intent.

During his short tenure heading the board, BP’s share price rose by roughly 29pc, though this was partly driven by a rise in oil and gas prices caused by the war in Iran.

While Mr Manifold’s swift action won him fans in some corners, not all investors approved. Around 18pc of investors voted against his appointment at BP’s annual general meeting last month. One shareholder said they had voted against Mr Manifold because of concerns he may reduce the influence of shareholders, particularly through a proposal to permit virtual-only AGMs.

Ashley Kelty, an analyst at Panmure Liberum, said of BP: “It’s a pretty unedifying situation for the company. They’ve obviously gone through a fair number of chief execs and interims and whatnot in recent years.

“It’s a mess. It’s certainly not going to do anything to encourage any industry heavyweights to take on the new role.”

BP declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Japan’s stock market is the ultimate picks and shovels play
data.description:
Ask any investor where the best stock market returns have been achieved in recent years and they will almost certainly mention the US. Most likely they will talk about tech stocks and the “magnificent seven”. The more engaged might give a nod to India. More recently they are likely to have noticed the fireworks in Korea.

The market they are very unlikely to place at the top of the leaderboard is Japan. Forty years on from the Japanese stock market and property boom, Asia’s most developed market still struggles to shake off its reputation as a deflationary demographic disaster.

It is the market I have always struggled to interest our investors in. Which is a shame. Because in the past decade and a half, it has been a fantastic performer.

If you had invested £100 in the FTSE 100 in the summer of 2012, you would have around £180 today. Not terrible, but an unexciting annualised return. By contrast, the same £100 invested in the US during that 14-year period would be worth £531, nearly three times as much.

No surprise there. The post-financial crisis recovery years were dominated by big, successful US tech stocks. What might surprise you, however, is that £100 invested in Tokyo over the same period is today worth £532. By the narrowest of margins, Japan has pipped the US to the post.

Japan has been the best-performing slice of my pension, un-flashily delivering the goods year after year. I think it will continue to do so. For a variety of reasons, the stars are well aligned for Japan. And I am no longer in a small minority of eccentric Japanophiles. The broader investment community is cottoning on.

Part of the case for Japan has been well rehearsed. The escape from 30 years of deflation – in which GDP barely grew, wages stagnated, and companies sat on their cash in the absence of anything better to do with it – is now firmly in the rear-view mirror.

Now the country is benefiting from a healthy level of inflation at around the Bank of Japan’s target, positive wage growth, and consequently improving domestic demand.

A second tailwind that is well understood is the positive impact of corporate reforms. Encouraged by the government and Tokyo Stock Exchange, the country’s leading companies have implemented these with surprising alacrity.

Better governance is showing up in the unwinding of complex cross-shareholdings, more mergers and acquisitions, and notably in growing levels of share buybacks and dividends. More than two-thirds of companies are increasing their payouts to shareholders, a new record.

What is new, and has the potential to keep the Japanese market bubbling, is the country’s exposure to the world’s big capital expenditure themes. Japan is the ultimate picks and shovels play, not just on artificial intelligence (AI), but on reindustrialisation, reshoring, automation and higher defence spending.

In a new boring-is-good world in which capital-light Silicon Valley hands the baton to a power-hungry “real stuff” economy, Japan offers investors a broad-based, high-quality, diversified, sensibly priced opportunity. And unlike Korea, it is not a two-horse race.

Japan has deep experience and expertise in many of the sectors that will underpin the AI revolution and the broader pendulum-swing to a hard-assets economy: factory automation, precision machinery, power equipment, defence electronics, industrial materials, telecom networks and construction.

Names that you will have heard of: Kawasaki, Mitsubishi, NTT. Others that you may not have: Keyence, Softbank, Fanuc.

The top-performing sectors so far in 2026 have been metals, electrical appliances and precision machinery, and they play on AI investment via optical fibre for data centres and memory chips. Machine tool orders are at a record high, driven by demand from both the US and China.

But the winners are broader than this. The third-best-performing sector is banks, a play on domestic reflation and higher interest rates, with another quarter-point increase looking likely next week. The sustainability of returns is reflected in the more subdued returns year to date of the broad-based Topix index, compared with the narrower, tech-exposed Nikkei 225 index.

Japan has a long history of disappointing overseas investors. A colleague of mine used to joke that it was never too late to short Japan. He was wrong, but even now, overseas investors have invested less in Japan than you would expect, given its weight in the global indexes.

Slowly but surely, that is changing. Having dipped in and out of Japan, treating it as a tactical trade, longer-term investors are now seeking a permanent exposure to the country’s shares.

Domestic investors, too, are learning to love their home-grown opportunities, with about half of holdings in the Japanese equivalent of our Isa now represented by local shares. Around a fifth of household assets are invested in equities – a notable shift from the traditional Japanese preference for bank deposits and government bonds.

It is still easy to find reasons to be sceptical about Japan. The country’s ageing population is in long-term decline; it has the highest level of government debt in the developed world; its exports are vulnerable to a strengthening in the yen. But Japan is slowly becoming a normal developed economy again, with rational levels of inflation, interest rates and bond yields.

Above all, Japan is well-placed for a resurgent bricks-and-mortar world in which wealth creation reverts to the manufacture of high-quality real stuff that you can touch. No one does that as consistently well as the Japanese.

Tom Stevenson is an investment director at Fidelity International. The views are his own.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Polanski rejoices as Left-wing media ‘troublemaker’ pledges to challenge BBC ‘bull----’
data.description:
In a slickly produced video unveiling the expansion of his news start-up to the UK, British-born journalist Mehdi Hasan criticised mainstream publishers and broadcasters.

Zeteo, his outlet, will “tell you what’s actually happening, not what the Right-wing billionaires and their loyal minions in the establishment media want you to think is happening,” Hasan declared.

Now in its third year, Zeteo has set out its stall as an unapologetically progressive news outlet, with a particular focus on covering the conflict in the Middle East from a pro-Palestinian perspective.

Hasan, 46, is overt in his challenge to conventional media outlets, not least what he claims is the “both-sides bulls--t” embodied by the BBC. Zeteo’s website describes Hasan as an “all-round troublemaker”.

His entry into the UK market hints at a new opportunity. As the Green Party gains popularity, could Zeteo’s anti-Israel stance offer a home for this new brand of Leftism?

Hasan, who was born in Swindon to Muslim Indian parents, began his career in the very establishment media he now seeks to disrupt.

After being privately educated at Merchant Taylors’ School and Oxford, he cut his teeth at ITV, followed by stints at the BBC, Channel 4 and Sky.

After moving to Al Jazeera’s English-language channel in 2012, Hasan became a well-known commentator, particularly on Islam, taking part in Oxford Union debates.

In 2020, Hasan landed his own show at MSNBC, launching him into a new stratum of media status in the US, which has since helped him acquire more than two million followers on X.

But the show was short-lived. It was cancelled in 2023 amid controversy over his coverage of Israel, including a fiery interview with Mark Regev, an adviser to Benjamin Netanyahu, the Israeli prime minister.

The following year, Hasan announced the launch of Zeteo, which takes its name from the Ancient Greek verb meaning to seek or inquire, on Substack.

Hasan raised $4m (£3m) from friends, family and viewers to launch the venture, which he promised would have a “strong bias for the truth and an unwavering belief in the media’s responsibility to the public”.

Alongside its newsletter, Zeteo publishes a weekly talk show and podcast fronted by Hasan, with guests and contributors including Greta Thunberg and Naomi Klein.

Zeteo’s newsletter has more than 650,000 subscribers, of which there are reportedly at least half a million paid subscribers forking out £10 a month. That would give the startup an estimated annual revenue of around $4m.

Hamish McKenzie, one of Substack’s co-founders, says the newsletter had done “freakishly well”, adding: “The success it’s had so far is unlike anything we’ve seen to date.”

Pro-Palestine stance

While Zeteo ostensibly has a broad political remit, there’s an undeniable focus on critical coverage of both the Trump administration and Israel’s actions in the Middle East.

On his talk show and on social media, Hasan frequently weighs in to criticise the Israeli regime and its actions in Gaza while highlighting the plight of Palestinians.

Zeteo has also distributed several documentaries on the topic and last year acquired Gaza: Doctors Under Attack, a controversial documentary about doctors working in Gaza that was shelved by the BBC because of impartiality concerns surrounding its production before ultimately being picked up by Channel 4.

Zeteo’s political stance is such that the outlet has frequently attracted questions over whether it is funded by Qatar, which has been investing in youth-focused Western outlets including The News Movement and The Recount.

Hasan denies receiving Qatari backing and describes the accusations as “lazy anti-Muslim racism”. He has not disclosed the identity of his investors, but has said they are all American citizens.

Regardless of its funding, however, there are already signs Zeteo will put pro-Palestinian viewpoints at the heart of its offering in the UK.

The video promoting the launch of the start-up includes several clips of the BBC’s coverage of the Gaza conflict, alongside a headline proclaiming that the broadcaster is “systematically biased against Palestinians”.

“No spin, no surface-level analysis, no both-sides bulls---”, Hasan promises of his own venture.

“We have a media that for far too long has hid behind euphemisms and patronised viewers and readers,” he tells The Telegraph. “And I think just as people are fed up with mainstream politicians and are leaning towards populists who speak a bit more authentically, I think the same thing is happening in our media.”

Hasan is tapping into a new genre of personality-led journalism that runs counter to the staid, balanced approach of traditional broadcasting and performs particularly well on social media.

His move mirrors that of Piers Morgan, albeit at a different end of the political spectrum. Morgan, who quit Rupert Murdoch’s TalkTV in 2024 to pursue his own show on YouTube, has invited Hasan on as a guest several times and frequently clashed with him on the topic of Israel.

“I think Mehdi’s very smart,” says Morgan, but adds that the former MSNBC host is “very to the Left”.

Hasan, who stresses that this is a “soft launch” for the UK, has so far assembled contributors including Guardian columnist Owen Jones, Left-wing economic commentator Grace Blakeley and writer Afua Hirsch.

Peter Oborne, the former Telegraph journalist who resigned in 2015 in a row over the newspaper’s coverage of major advertiser HSBC and has since become a vocal defender of Islam, will write a monthly column. Meanwhile, Shehab Khan, a former ITV News political correspondent, will helm Zeteo UK’s daily coverage of Westminster.

The outlet aims to become self-financing through subscriptions within a year.

While politics will remain its core, Hasan hints at expansion into other areas, pointing to Zeteo’s US podcast We’re Not Kidding. The show, which is described as funny people talking about serious things and is hosted by Hasan, has guests including Hollywood actors Mark Ruffalo and Riz Ahmed, though politics is still a key element.

Alex DeGroote, a media analyst, says the outlet’s proposition will be “Left-wing, anti-imperialist, anti-Zionist content across all media formats”. He adds: “There’s definitely a market there.”

Hasan himself describes the target audience as “people who are frustrated with a media that’s unable to ‘call balls and strikes’, as they say where I live these days, say a genocide is a genocide, say a fascist is a fascist, say racism is racism.”

DeGroote compares Zeteo to Novara, the Corbyn-backing media start-up that has gained traction among young, Left-wing audiences. “[Zeteo] is a small business but it could grow fast if it gains traction outside the US,” he adds.

Announcing the UK launch on Tuesday, Hasan outlined what he viewed as a litany of failures in recent British media coverage. Chief among them were false accusations of “family voting” – a form of electoral fraud – for the Green Party and a “media pile-on” against its leader Zack Polanski.

Hasan’s focus on the Greens is notable at a time when the party has grown in popularity but has become embroiled in a row over anti-Semitism and Islamist rhetoric among its candidates.

With its staunchly anti-Israel stance, Zeteo now offers a new home for this burgeoning branch of Left-wing populism that puts the Arab-Israeli conflict at the heart of its offer to voters.

“Clearly, there’s a hunger for a different type of politics,” says Hasan. “I think these people are fed up with politics and media both. I think they’re fed up with establishment parties and establishment media. So, clearly, there is an underserved audience.”

It may prove to be a symbiotic relationship, with the Greens promoting Zeteo to new potential subscribers as the outlet writes supportive coverage.

Just hours after its UK launch, Polanski wrote on social media: “Brilliant news. And not a moment too soon!”
